rows.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 3 months (24 weeks from baseline)
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint is assessed at 12 weeks. A follow-up (clinical ratings and attention test) is carried out after 3 months post-treatment (i.e., 24 weeks from baseline). The study completion date was 24 February 2026, with primary completion on 21 November 2025.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 15 (Attexis + verum tDCS) and C = 15 (Attexis + placebo/sham tDCS)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): 15 patients receive three-month access to the psychotherapy app Attexis and a Sooma tDCS device set to verum (2mA, 20 minutes/day, 60 sessions, stimulating left DLPFC). Arm 2 (Placebo Comparator): 15 patients receive three-month access to the psychotherapy app Attexis and a Sooma tDCS device set to sham.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes (assessed at 12 weeks): (1) Compliance – number of days (out of 60) of actual (placebo-)tDCS treatment; (2) Efficacy 1 – number of responders according to the ASRS-v1.1 total score; (3) Efficacy 2 – number of responders according to the ASRS-v1.1 attention subscale; (4) Efficacy 3 – number of responders according to the ASRS-v1.1 hyperactivity subscale; (5) Stability of effects – number of responders according to change in ASRS-v1.1 sumscore at 24 weeks. Secondary outcomes (assessed at 12 weeks): ASRS-v1.1 sumscore (0–72), ASRS-v1.1 attention subscale (0–36), ASRS-v1.1 hyperactivity subscale (0–36), and Usability Experience Questionnaire (UEQ) sumscore.

rows.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, 6 months post-allocation
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months post-allocation; follow-up assessment at 6 months post-allocation. The attexis program remains accessible for 180 days once registered.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (attexis + TAU) and control group (TAU only); total enrolled = 337 (planned 326)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (EXPERIMENTAL): attexis + TAU – participants receive access to the digital health application attexis (CBT-based, web browser accessible, 180-day access) in addition to treatment as usual. Arm 2 (NO_INTERVENTION): TAU only – participants receive treatment as usual without access to attexis.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: ADHD Symptom Severity assessed via ASRS v1.1 (total score 0-72; higher = worse) at 3 months. Secondary outcomes: Depressive Symptomatology via PHQ-9 (total score 0-27; higher = worse) at 3 and 6 months; Self-Esteem via Rosenberg Self Esteem Scale (RSES; total score 0-30; higher = better) at 3 and 6 months; Functioning via Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS; total score 0-40; higher = worse impairment) at 3 and 6 months; Health-Related Quality of Life via AQoL-8D (total score 0-100; higher = better) at 3 and 6 months; ADHD Symptom Severity via ASRS v1.1 at 6 months.

rows.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months post-randomization (T2)
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months post-randomization (T1); follow-up conducted at 6 months post-randomization (T2).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: attexis + TAU; Control: TAU alone. Total N = 337 (exact group sizes not reported in abstract).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: attexis (fully self-guided digital intervention based on CBT and mindfulness principles) as an adjunct to treatment as usual (TAU). Arm 2: Treatment as usual (TAU) alone.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: ADHD symptom severity measured by Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS) total score at 3 months post-randomization (T1). Secondary outcomes: functional impairment, depressive symptoms, self-esteem, and health-related quality of life. Follow-up assessments at 6 months (T2). Subgroup analyses by sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. Adverse events monitoring.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intent-to-treat analyses showed significantly lower ADHD symptom severity in the intervention group at T1 (baseline-adjusted mean difference = -5.0 points; d = 0.85, p < .001). Significant improvements were also observed across all secondary outcomes (functional impairment, depressive symptoms, self-esteem, health-related quality of life). Effects remained stable at T2 (6 months). Responder analyses confirmed clinical relevance. Subgroup analyses demonstrated consistent effects across sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. No adverse events related to attexis were reported.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were required to use the app for at least 12 weeks and answer questions at four time points over a minimum of 12 weeks. The study ran from December 2021 to February 2024.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: access to the full version of the Cara Care für Reizdarm app; Control group: standard of care without use of the Cara Care für Reizdarm app or an alternative app. Total enrolled: 378 (planned: 374).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Access to the full version of the Cara Care für Reizdarm app for at least 12 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Standard of care with no application of the Cara Care für Reizdarm app or an alternative app.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Change in disease-specific symptom severity measured by IBS-SSS (Irritable Bowel Syndrome Severity Scoring System) in intervention and control groups. Additional primary measures: Quality of life (IBS-QOL), work and activity impairment (WPAI:IBS), health literacy (HLS-EU-Q16). Secondary outcomes: Anxiety (GAD-7), depressiveness (PHQ-9). All data collected via standardized, validated health questionnaires within the app at four time points over at least 12 weeks.

rows.dtx_name: companion patella powered by medi - proved by Dt. Kniegesellschaft
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes measured at 4 time points: Day 0, 30, 60, and 90. The intervention arm receives a 90-day personalized exercise therapy program via the DiGA companion patella app.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention arm: patients receive access code for the DiGA companion patella app (90-day personalized exercise therapy with evidence-based exercises and education). Control arm: patients receive a doctor's prescription for conventional analog physiotherapy treatment corresponding to statutory health insurance agreements. Total n=140.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): 90-day personalized app-based exercise therapy and education via the digital health application 'companion patella'. Arm 2 (Control): Conventional analog physiotherapy treatment by physiotherapists as per statutory health insurance agreements.
rows.collected_data: 1. Pain reduction measured on the Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) at 4 time points (Day 0/30/60/90). 2. Improvement of knee joint function measured with the Kujala Score at 4 time points (Day 0/30/60/90).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is 90 days for both arms. Primary and secondary outcomes are assessed at day 90.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 135 (DiGA companion® shoulder digital health application) and C = 135 (standard of care physiotherapy); 1:1 randomization
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients receive access to the digital health application (DiGA) companion® shoulder, which includes a 90-day personalized exercise therapy with evidence-based exercises and education, delivered digitally. The DiGA is a class I medical device (MDR). Arm 2 (Control): Patients receive standard of care for 90 days, including prescription of physiotherapy with usually 1 to 3 prescriptions of 6 units each, as provided in the therapeutic products catalog.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Shoulder-specific pain reduction measured with the Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) after 90 days. Secondary outcomes: (1) Shoulder-specific functional improvement measured with the Shoulder Pain and Disability Index (SPADI) after 90 days; (2) Number of days of incapacity for work due to illness within the survey period measured via self-reporting at 1 measurement point (day 90). Inclusion criterion also references NPRS ≥4 for maximum pain intensity in the last 7 days at baseline.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is nine weeks (post-assessment), with a six-month follow-up assessment. The wait-list control group receives access to Deprexis after six months. Three assessment time points: baseline, post-intervention (9 weeks), and follow-up (6 months).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Deprexis (immediate access, experimental) and Wait-list (access to Deprexis after 6 months, active comparator); total enrolled = 180 (100 with multiple sclerosis and 100 with epilepsy planned, 180 actual).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Deprexis): Participants receive immediate access to the web-based Deprexis program consisting of 10 content modules plus introductory and summary modules, based on cognitive-behavioral techniques, delivered online over approximately 9 weeks. Arm 2 (Wait-list): Participants are placed on a wait-list and receive access to Deprexis after a six-month delay.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Severity of depressive symptoms assessed with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) over a 7-day timeframe, measured at baseline, post-intervention (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up. Secondary outcome: Quality of life assessed by the WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF) over a 14-day timeframe. Other outcomes: Depressive symptoms assessed by the Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (QIDS, 7 days); core self-evaluations assessed by the Core Self-Evaluations Scale (CSES, 7 days); suicidal behaviors and tendencies assessed by the Suicidal Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R, 7 days); quality of life assessed by the Hamburg MS Quality of Life Questionnaire (HALEMS, 7 days).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The QIDS-SR-16 is administered every 2 weeks during the 8-week treatment period. Pre- and post-treatment phone interviews and questionnaires are also conducted.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Computerized self-help program (Deprexis) for 8 weeks; Control: Wait List Control (8-week wait before accessing Deprexis). Estimated total enrollment: 500.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Active Comparator): Participants have access to the Deprexis computerized self-help program for depression for 8 weeks, consisting of 10 content modules based on cognitive-behavioral approaches. Arm 2 (No Intervention/Wait List Control): Participants wait 8 weeks after assignment before they can access the Deprexis program.
rows.collected_data: Primary: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report) (QIDS-SR-16), administered every 2 weeks during the 8-week treatment period to measure depression symptom severity. Secondary: Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D) at pre- and post-treatment phone interviews; Inventory of Depression and Anxiety Symptoms (IDAS) at pre- and post-treatment; Psychiatric Diagnostic Screening Questionnaire (PDSQ) at pre- and post-treatment; Risky Families Questionnaire (RFQ) at pre-treatment; Massachusetts General Hospital Antidepressant Treatment History Questionnaire (ATRQ) at pre-treatment; Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) at pre- and post-treatment. Saliva samples collected for DNA/genetic analysis.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The study involved a 10-week treatment period. The wait-list group received access to Deprexis after 10 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Three groups: Deprexis unguided (web-based self-help without therapist support), Deprexis guided (web-based self-help plus weekly e-mail contact with therapist), and Wait-list control. Total N=76.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Deprexis (unguided) - CBT-based online self-help program without any therapist contact during the 10-week treatment period. Arm 2: Deprexis (guided, with therapist) - same CBT-based online self-help program plus scheduled weekly e-mail feedback from a therapist and the possibility to ask questions via e-mail. Arm 3: Wait-list control - no intervention for 10 weeks, after which participants received access to Deprexis.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II; 21-item self-report, scores 0-63, higher = more severe depression) at 10 weeks. Secondary outcomes: Brief Symptom Inventory (53-item BSI; psychopathology severity, scores 0-4) at 10 weeks; Inventory of Interpersonal Problems (64-item IIP; scores 0-4) at 10 weeks; WHOQOL-BREF (quality of life, scores 0-100, higher = better quality of life) at 10 weeks. Other outcome: CSQ-8 (Client Satisfaction Questionnaire; scores 1-4, higher = greater satisfaction) at 10 weeks.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months and 12 months
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were randomised to twelve weeks of online self-help or waiting-list control. Secondary outcomes were assessed at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months. After the one-year period, controls also received online self-help. Primary completion date was February 2015; full study completion was February 2017.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis online self-help with and without e-mail support) and Control (waiting-list/no intervention); total N = 1013
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Deprexis – online self-help program with and without e-mail support for 12 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): No intervention (waiting-list control); after the one-year assessment period, controls also received access to online self-help.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) at 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HRSD) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms (QIDS) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; PHQ-9 at 6 months and 12 months.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint is assessed at 8 weeks post-randomization. The BDI timeframe is listed as '7 Days' (recall period), but the intervention/assessment period is 8 weeks. Wait-list control group receives access to Deprexis after 8 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis): immediate access to web-based program; Control (Wait-list): access to Deprexis after 8 weeks. Total N = 200 (individual group sizes not specified in the record).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental - Deprexis): Participants receive immediate access to the web-based intervention Deprexis, consisting of 10 content modules plus one introductory and one summary module, based on cognitive-behavioral techniques. Arm 2 (Active Comparator - Wait-list): Participants receive access to Deprexis after an 8-week delay.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Severity of depressive symptoms assessed with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) at baseline and 8 weeks. Secondary outcome: Quality of life assessed by the WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF, 26-item version covering physical, psychological, social, and environment domains) at baseline and 8 weeks. Exclusion screening: Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Six months after randomization
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint is assessed three months after randomization (~13 weeks). End of inpatient treatment is on average 6 weeks. The intervention (deprexis® or online information) runs for 90 days. Follow-up assessments occur at end of inpatient treatment (average 6 weeks), three months after randomization, and six months after randomization.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Experimental (deprexis® online self-help program): part of 229 enrolled; Control (online information about depression as placebo comparator): part of 229 enrolled. Exact group sizes not specified in the raw data.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Patients use deprexis® online self-help program as an additional treatment during inpatient stay and as aftercare for 90 days. Arm 2 (Placebo Comparator): Patients receive weekly online information about depression for 90 days. Both arms receive these interventions in addition to regular inpatient psychosomatic treatment.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck's Depression Inventory (BDI-II) at three months after randomization. Secondary outcomes: PHQ-9 (depression), BDI-II, GAD-7 (anxiety), Dysfunctional Attitude Scale (DAS), Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSE), EUROHIS-QOL 8 (quality of life), short form Work Ability Index (WAI), self-devised questionnaire on acceptance and utilization of deprexis® and of information placebo, Childhood Trauma Questionnaire (CTQ), OPD-Structure Questionnaire short form (OPD-SFK), Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ), course of mood/depressiveness/utilization of units during inpatient treatment, utilization of other treatments after inpatient treatment, satisfaction with inpatient treatment, willingness to pay, remission from depression (BDI score <13 and Reliable Change Index). Timepoints: randomization, end of inpatient treatment (average 6 weeks), three months after randomization, six months after randomization.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes, assessments at 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months from baseline
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary outcome (PHQ-D-9) is assessed at baseline, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months. The primary completion date was March 2015 and the study completion date was May 2015. The trial started in September 2012.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Two groups: Stepped Care Model (SCM) intervention group and Treatment as Usual (TAU) control group; cluster-randomized at the level of general practitioners. Total enrolled: 737 participants.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Stepped Care Intervention/SCM): Patients screened with PHQ-9 (inclusion if >4 points) and receive stepped treatment based on depression severity — mild depression: Step I (active monitoring) or Step II (bibliotherapy or online self-help 'Deprexis®') or Step II+ (telephone-based psychotherapy); moderate depression: Step III (outpatient psychotherapy or psychopharmacological treatment); severe depression: Step IV (combined psychotherapy and psychopharmacological treatment, optionally inpatient). Arm 2 (Control/Treatment as Usual): Patients screened with PHQ-D-9 and then receive treatment as usual from general physician or other health service providers.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Change in depression severity measured by PHQ-D-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire 9, German version) at baseline, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months. Secondary outcomes: Cost-benefit ratio in QALYs (baseline, 6 months, 12 months); Response/Remission/Relapse via PHQ-D-9 (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Quality of life via EQ-5D (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Quality of life via SF-12 (baseline, 3, 6, 9 months); Health service utilization and medication consumption via non-standardized items (baseline, 6, 12 months); Self-esteem via Rosenberg self-esteem scale (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Therapeutic alliance via Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ) (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Anxiety symptoms via PHQ-D anxiety scale (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Panic symptoms via PHQ-D (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Somatisation via PHQ-D (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); General anxiety disorder symptoms via GAD-7 (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, 8 weeks after post-treatment (week 16 from baseline)
rows.duration_additional_info: The study involves an 8-week intervention period (baseline to post-treatment at week 8), followed by an 8-week follow-up assessment at week 16. Primary completion is estimated April 2029.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Deprexis (internet-delivered intervention) vs. Treatment-as-Usual (TAU) control; RCT with n=132 for Aim 2 (plus n=16-20 for qualitative Aim 1), total estimated enrollment 152
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Deprexis – an 8-week self-guided internet-delivered psychosocial treatment for depressive symptoms, consisting of 12 modules (10 core content modules plus introductory and summary modules), drawing from various theoretical frameworks including CBT. Arm 2 (Control): Treatment-as-Usual (TAU) – access to standard non-study care with no additional intervention.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: (1) Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology Self-Report (QIDS-SR-16) – 16-item self-report measure of depressive symptom severity (scores 0–27), assessed at baseline, post-treatment (week 8), and follow-up (week 16); (2) World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule-II (WHODAS) – 36-item questionnaire assessing functional disability across 7 domains and total score (scores 0–100), assessed at baseline, week 8, and week 16; (3) Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) – 3-item self-report measure of symptom-related disability across work/school, family, and social functioning (scores 0–30), assessed at baseline, week 8, and week 16. Exploratory variables: demographic variables, baseline psychopathology, credibility, and Deprexis usage as potential moderators of treatment effects. Qualitative interviews (Aim 1) assessing Veterans' perceptions, needs, and preferences regarding Deprexis.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 3 months post-intervention (T3), assessed at approximately 20 weeks from baseline
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants are assessed at 4 time points: baseline (T0), mid-intervention at 4 weeks (T1), post-intervention at 8 weeks (T2), and follow-up at 3 months post-intervention (~20 weeks, T3). The intervention period is 8 weeks. Time frames include a +/- 2 week window.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Experimental group 1 (online program only), Experimental group 2 (online program + online forum), Control group (waitlist); total estimated N=240, approximately 80 per arm
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Access to Deprexis online self-help program for 8 weeks, at least 30 minutes 1-2 times per week. Arm 2 (Second Experimental/Active Comparator): Access to Deprexis plus an online peer-support forum for 8 weeks; participants divided into groups of 10 to share experiences, with a moderator. Arm 3 (Control): Waitlist control group with no intervention during the 8-week period, after which they gain access to the online program.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms at post-intervention (8 weeks). Secondary outcomes: Fribourg Reward Task (reaction times, accuracy, mood reactions to monetary and social rewards); Snaith-Hamilton Pleasure Scale (SHAPS) for anhedonia/hedonic capacity; Behavioral Inhibition System/Behavioral Activation System (BIS/BAS) for reward sensitivity; Perceived Social Support (PSS) questionnaire. Exploratory/additional outcomes: Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7); General Self-Efficacy Scale (GSES); Quality of Life (SF-12); Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSE); Perceived Stress Scale-Short Version (PSS); Somatic Symptom Scale (SSS-8); PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5); Childhood Trauma Questionnaire-Short Form (CTQ-SF); System Usability Scale (SUS); Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ). Assessments at T0 (baseline), T1 (4 weeks), T2 (8 weeks), T3 (3 months post-intervention/~20 weeks).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at month 6 and month 12
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint is assessed at Month 3 (approximately 12 weeks). The trial also includes an extension phase for maintenance with long-term online follow-up at month 6 and month 12. The waitlist control group gains access to Deprexis after a 6-month waiting period.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Three arms: Deprexis (intervention alone), DeprexisPlus (intervention plus weekly email support), and Waitlist Control (no intervention for 6 months). Total enrolled: 279 participants (individual group sizes not specified in the raw data).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Deprexis): Access to the web-based Deprexis program (MS-adapted CBT-based online tool) for 3 months. Arm 2 (DeprexisPlus): Access to the same Deprexis program plus scheduled e-mail contact once per week from a clinical psychologist for 3 months. Arm 3 (Waitlist Control): No intervention for 6 months, after which participants receive full access to Deprexis.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II), assessed from Month 0 to Month 3. Secondary outcomes: WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF, 4 subscales: Physical, Psychological, Social Relationships, Environmental); Multiple Sclerosis Impact Scale (MSIS, 2 subscales: Physical and Psychological); Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions (FSMC, total score and Motor/Cognitive subscales); Chalder Fatigue Scale — all assessed Month 0 to Month 3. Other outcomes: Brief International Cognitive Assessment for Multiple Sclerosis (BICAMS, including SMDT, CVLT-II, BVMT-R) from Month 0 to Month 3; Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R) at Month 3; Mini International Neuropsychiatric Interview (MINI v5.0.0) dichotomous variable 'Major Depressive Episode, Current (yes/no)' from Month 0 to Month 3.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months from baseline (i.e., 3 months after the primary endpoint)
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is 12 weeks (3 months). The primary endpoint is assessed at post (3 months). Secondary outcomes are assessed at baseline, post (3 months), and follow-up (6 months).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis online self-help) and Control (Care as usual / CAU); total N = 163 (individual group sizes not specified in the raw data)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Deprexis – 12 weeks of internet-based online self-help program. Arm 2 (Active Comparator): CAU – Care as usual (waiting-list control).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire – 9 items), assessed at baseline and post (3 months). Secondary outcomes: QIDS-SR16 (Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms – Self-Report), GAD-7 (Generalized Anxiety Disorder – 7 items), PHQ-15 (Patient Health Questionnaire – 15 items), SF-12 (Short Form Health Survey – 12 items); all assessed at baseline, post (3 months), and follow-up (6 months).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes – secondary outcomes measured at 12 weeks (T1), 6 months (T2), and 12 months (T3) after baseline
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention group used Deprexis for 12 weeks. The primary endpoint (costs) was assessed over a period of one year after the end of treatment. Secondary outcomes (PHQ-9, SF-12, EQ-5D, WSAS) were collected at baseline (T0), 12 weeks (T1), 6 months (T2), and 12 months (T3).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: access to Deprexis internet-based self-help program for 12 weeks; Control group: no access to Deprexis. Total enrolled: 3832 (planned: 5400); individual group sizes not specified in the record.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Uses the Deprexis internet-based self-help program for 12 weeks, with questionnaires (PHQ-9, SF-12, EQ-5D, WSAS) and cost data from DAK. Arm 2 (Control): No access to Deprexis; same questionnaires administered; also asked to confirm they had no contact with Deprexis during the observation period.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Healthcare costs over one year after end of treatment, determined from DAK (statutory health insurance) cost data. Secondary outcomes: Depression severity (PHQ-9) at T0, T1 (12 weeks), T2 (6 months), T3 (12 months); Health-related quality of life (SF-12 and EQ-5D) at T0, T1, T2, T3; Social and occupational functioning (WSAS) at T0, T1, T2, T3; Satisfaction with DAK advisory services and acceptance of the Deprexis program (intervention group); Confirmation of non-exposure to Deprexis (control group).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Intervention period was 8 weeks; assessments conducted at baseline and post-intervention (8 weeks). Wait-list control group gains access to Deprexis after completion of the post survey.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Deprexis internet intervention) and wait-list control group; total planned 140 participants (108 planned originally)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Internet-intervention 'Deprexis' for treating depressive symptoms, consisting of ten modules plus one summary module based on cognitive-behavioral therapy (behavioral activation, cognitive modification, interpersonal and problem solving skills, relaxation methods) over 8 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Wait-list control group receiving no additional treatment during the intervention period (may continue previously initiated treatments); gains access to Deprexis after completion of the post survey.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 items depression module (PHQ-9) - self-rating questionnaire measuring depressive symptoms over the past week (at baseline and post-assessment). Secondary outcomes: South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS) - screening for pathological gambling (at baseline and post-assessment); Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS) - measures severity of gambling symptoms within the past week (at baseline and post-assessment); General Anxiety Disorder Screener (GAD-7) - assesses anxiety levels over the last two weeks (at baseline and post-assessment); Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ) - brief screening instrument for common mental disorders (only at baseline); Subjective Appraisal of the intervention by treatment group participants (only at post-assessment). Demographic and psychopathological information, clinical anamnesis (current treatments, use of drugs and self-help, psychiatric diagnoses) also collected.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The 12-week intervention/waiting period includes baseline assessment (T1) and final assessment after 12 weeks (T4) for all arms. The supported intervention group (IG-A) also has interim assessments at 4 weeks (T2) and 8 weeks (T3). After the 12-week observation period, the waitlist control group also receives access to deprexis®. Scheduled completion date is June 2027.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: IG-A (Supported Intervention Group, n ≥ 100): DiGA deprexis® for 12 weeks plus brief structured check-in sessions at 4, 8, and 12 weeks; IG-B (Unsupported Intervention Group, n ≥ 100): DiGA deprexis® for 12 weeks without adjunct consultations; CG (Waitlist Control Group, n ≥ 100): treatment as usual during the 12-week waiting period, then access to deprexis® after the observation period.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (IG-A): Participants on the waiting list receive access to the digital health application deprexis® for 12 weeks plus brief structured therapeutic check-in sessions after 4, 8, and 12 weeks (not psychotherapy per se). Arm 2 (IG-B): Participants on the waiting list receive access to deprexis® for 12 weeks with no additional consultations. Arm 3 (CG): Participants on the waiting list receive treatment as usual (no DiGA, no additional intervention from the clinic) for 12 weeks; after the observation period they also receive access to deprexis®.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes assessed via standardized self-report questionnaires at T1 (baseline) and T4 (12 weeks), with additional assessments at T2 (4 weeks) and T3 (8 weeks) for IG-A: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) for depressive symptoms; GAD-7 (Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale-7) for anxiety symptoms; SCL-90-R (Symptom Checklist-90-R) for global psychological distress and subjective symptom-related functional impairment. Secondary outcomes: acceptance of the DiGA (user satisfaction, perceived fit, perceived usefulness); usage intensity and adherence (frequency and extent of engagement); dropout rates and reasons for discontinuation; subjectively perceived effects of and burden related to the intervention — assessed via study-specific standardized self-report questionnaires and structured interviews. Exploratory outcomes: comparison of supported vs. unsupported DiGA use regarding efficacy, adherence, and attrition; potential moderators and predictors (sociodemographic characteristics, baseline symptom severity, attitudes toward digital health applications); evaluation of clinical relevance of DiGA as a bridging intervention.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at pretreatment, posttreatment (12 weeks), and follow-up (6 months after treatment). All secondary outcomes also assessed at the same three timepoints.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Psychotherapy plus deprexis web-based program; Control group: Psychotherapy without deprexis. Target enrollment of 800 participants total.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Participants receive regular face-to-face psychotherapy plus access to the deprexis web-based self-help program (10 modules covering psychoeducation, behavioral activation, cognitive restructuring, mindfulness, interpersonal skills, relaxation, physical activity, problem-focused approaches, expressive writing/schema-focused content, and positive psychology). Arm 2 (Control): Participants receive traditional face-to-face psychotherapy as usual without access to deprexis.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depressive symptomatology measured with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) at pretreatment, posttreatment (12 weeks), and follow-up (6 months). Secondary outcomes (assessed at pretreatment, posttreatment, and follow-up): 1) Anxiety symptoms via GAD-7; 2) Somatic symptomatology via PHQ somatic symptom module; 3) Quality of life via SF-12; 4) Psychotherapeutic progress via FEP-2 (well-being, symptoms, interpersonal relationships, incongruence); 5) Common mental disorder screening via Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ, 15-item); 6) Suicidal tendencies via Suicidal Behaviours Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R); 7) Psychological empowerment via adapted Spreitzer scale; 8) Depression diagnosis via licensed assessment.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants are assessed at three time points: baseline (before treatment), after 10 weeks (primary endpoint), and after 6 months. The intervention group starts immediately after consent; the waitlist control group receives access after the 10-week waiting period.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (immediate access to web-based deprexis treatment) vs. waitlist control group (access after 10-week waiting period); target enrollment of 80 participants total.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Immediate access to the web-based psychological treatment 'deprexis', guided by a clinical psychologist, consisting of 10 content modules based on cognitive-behavioral principles. Arm 2 (Control): Waitlist control condition receiving access to deprexis after a waiting period of 10 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Depression levels by BDI-II (Beck Depression Inventory-II), clinical depression by the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ), and clinical interview using the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM Diagnosis (SCID). Secondary outcomes: Stress perception by Perceived Stress Questionnaire (PSQ); Social support by the ENRICHD Social Support Inventory (ESSI); Quality of life by the WHOQOL-BREF; Health behavior; Client satisfaction by the Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ). All assessments conducted at three time points: baseline, 10 weeks, and 6 months.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes – assessments at 18 weeks, and 6 months after program usage termination; control group also assessed 9 weeks after completing the delayed program
rows.duration_additional_info: The program itself runs for 9 weeks. Data were collected at baseline, 9 weeks, and 18 weeks for both groups. The control group (delayed treatment) also completed questionnaires 9 weeks after finishing the program. Both groups completed questionnaires 6 months after their respective program usage termination.
rows.study_size_participants: 200 (target enrollment; actual enrollment date listed as 30/06/2008 without a specific participant count confirmed)
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Deprexis® online self-help program (add-on to treatment as usual); Control: Waitlist (treatment as usual only, with delayed access to the program). Target enrollment: 200 participants.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Participants received access to the Deprexis® online self-help program for 9 weeks in addition to treatment as usual. Arm 2 (Control/Waitlist): Participants did not receive access to the program during the initial period and continued treatment as usual; they received delayed access to the program after the initial waitlist period.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depressive symptom level measured with the German version of the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) at baseline, 9 weeks, and 18 weeks; also 9 weeks post-program for the control group and 6 months after program usage termination for both groups. Secondary outcomes: (1) Work and Social Adjustment Scale (German translation) at the same timepoints; (2) Subjective usefulness of the program measured by individually designed items.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The study examined an 8-week course of Deprexis compared with a waitlist control.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Deprexis (internet intervention); Control: waitlist. Total N = 295.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Deprexis (8-week internet-based intervention for depression); Arm 2: Waitlist control. Nationally recruited sample from the United States.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depression Symptoms (QIDS) assessed at baseline and after 8 weeks; specific symptoms tracked included sadness, indecision, early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, agitation, and somatic symptoms. Network analysis and mean-level approaches were used to examine symptom-level changes.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Deprexis directly improved sadness and indecision. Changes in these symptoms were associated with changes in early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, and agitation. All of these symptoms except agitation and early insomnia showed significant decreases with Deprexis compared with waitlist after correcting for multiple comparisons. Six additional symptoms, particularly somatic symptoms, were not impacted by Deprexis compared with waitlist. The efficacy of Deprexis was attributed to its direct impact on sadness and indecision.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Patients were followed by structured telephone interviews post-discharge up to the study endpoint at 12 weeks after deprexis-initiation; no follow-up beyond the 12-week primary endpoint is described.
rows.duration_additional_info: There was no fixed time frame for inpatient treatment duration; the study used a pragmatic approach with flexible discharge management. Assessments were carried out at study entry and every 2 weeks. The 12-week period began at deprexis-initiation (during inpatient stay), and post-discharge follow-up via telephone interviews continued until the 12-week endpoint.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: TAU-PLUS (TAU + guided deprexis): N=36; TAU (treatment-as-usual): N=33
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (TAU): Treatment-as-usual for 12 weeks, including routine psychiatric inpatient care with flexible discharge and post-discharge follow-up. Arm 2 (TAU-PLUS): Treatment-as-usual plus guided use of the web-based self-help program deprexis for 12 weeks, started during inpatient treatment and continued post-discharge.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II) assessed at study entry and every 2 weeks up to week 12. Secondary outcomes: Hamilton Depression Scale (HAMD), Clinical Global Impression-Severity (CGI-S), WHO Well-Being Index (WHO-5), Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ). Additional measures: working alliance with deprexis, inpatient treatment duration, daily activity, utilization of post-hospital care after discharge, re-admission rates, prescribed antidepressant medication.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12, modified ITT analyses showed significant between-group differences in BDI-II scores in favor of TAU-PLUS (p=.03), corresponding to a medium effect size (d=-.73, 95% CI -1.4 to .06). TAU-PLUS patients showed greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70, 95% CI -.03 to 1.38) and were discharged significantly earlier from inpatient treatment (p=.003). Post-discharge, the TAU-PLUS group reported a lower rate of post-hospital care (p=.01) and re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcome measures including therapist alliance were not significantly different between groups at study endpoint. The patients' working alliance with deprexis significantly predicted MDD improvement and wellbeing. TAU plus deprexis was superior to TAU in improving subjective depression severity and daily activity.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; three-months' and nine-months' follow-up after post-treatment
rows.duration_additional_info: The 12-week online intervention was followed by assessments at post-treatment, 3-month follow-up, and 9-month follow-up. Primary outcome (statutory health insurance costs) was estimated over the study year using administrative data.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Participants randomized to either a 12-week online intervention (deprexis) or a control condition; specific group sizes not reported in the abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): 12-week internet-based intervention using 'deprexis'. Arm 2 (Control): control condition (details not specified in the abstract).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: statutory health insurance costs estimated using health insurers' administrative data. Secondary outcomes assessed via patient self-report at baseline, post-treatment, 3-month follow-up, and 9-month follow-up: depression severity, health-related quality of life, and impairment in functioning.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: In both groups, total statutory health insurance costs decreased during the study period. The intervention group showed a 32% reduction in total costs (from 3139€/year at baseline to 2119€ in the study year), compared to a 13% mean reduction in the control group; changes from baseline differed significantly between groups. The intervention group also showed a significantly greater reduction in depression severity, significantly greater reduction in impairment in functioning, and a significantly greater increase in health-related quality of life compared to the control group.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; the intervention group had access to Deprexis for 90 days (approximately 13 weeks), and the control group received delayed access after 8 weeks
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed at 8 weeks. The intervention group received immediate access to Deprexis for 90 days (~13 weeks). The control group received delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks. Overall program duration was 90 days.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (immediate access): n=94 (treatment as usual plus immediate access to Deprexis for 90 days); Control (delayed access): n=95 (treatment as usual and delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Treatment as usual plus immediate access to Deprexis (internet-based self-guided CBT program) for 90 days (n=94). Arm 2 (Control): Treatment as usual with delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks (n=95).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptom self-assessment. Secondary outcomes: Clinical Outcome in Routine Evaluation-Outcome Measurement (CORE-OM) for general psychological state; perceived self-efficacy measure. Additional data: login frequency/engagement with Deprexis, remission rates, deterioration rates, dropout rates, demographic and clinical variables, participant satisfaction with Deprexis. Participants also underwent a semistructured interview at screening.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analysis (linear mixed model) showed participants receiving immediate Deprexis access improved significantly more than the delayed access control group on: PHQ-9 (Cohen d=0.80; P<.001); CORE-OM (Cohen d=0.82; P<.001); perceived self-efficacy (Cohen d=0.63; P<.001). Remission was achieved by 21% (20/94) in the intervention group vs 7% (7/95) in the control group (P<.001). Deterioration rates were lower in the immediate access group. Dropout rate was high but no significant differences in demographic and clinical variables between dropouts and completers were found. Participants reported medium to high satisfaction with Deprexis. Average logins in the intervention group: 14.81 (SD 12.16).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were randomized to receive an 8-week course of treatment immediately or after an 8-week delay (waitlist control). No follow-up beyond the primary endpoint is mentioned.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 285 (immediate 8-week Deprexis treatment) and C = 91 (8-week waitlist control, then received treatment)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Immediate access to Deprexis (Internet intervention for depression) for 8 weeks with relatively minimal support (n=285). Arm 2: Waitlist control for 8 weeks, after which they received the Deprexis intervention (n=91).
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms-Self-Report (QIDS-SR) for self-reported depression symptoms; interviewer-rated depression symptoms; well-being measures; depression-related disability; symptoms of social anxiety; panic symptoms; traumatic intrusions. Primary outcome: reduction in self-reported depression symptoms (QIDS-SR) at 8 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analyses showed treatment was associated with greater reduction in self-reported depression symptoms (effect size d = .80) and 12 times greater likelihood of experiencing at least 50% symptom improvement compared with waitlist control. Similar effects were observed for secondary outcomes including interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, and depression-related disability. Treatment effects for symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions were relatively small.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at 12 weeks; secondary outcomes assessed at both 12 weeks and 6 months follow-up. Working alliance assessed after 6 and 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Two active treatment conditions: (1) regular psychotherapy alone (control), and (2) psychotherapy plus web-based depression program 'deprexis' (intervention). Exact group sizes not reported separately.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Regular psychotherapy alone. Arm 2: Regular psychotherapy plus adjunctive web-based depression program ('deprexis'). Participants were randomized within therapists.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: depressive symptoms measured with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) at 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: anxiety symptoms, somatic symptoms, and quality of life at 12 weeks and 6 months follow-up. Working alliance assessed after 6 and 12 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The combination of psychotherapy with the web-based program was more effective than psychotherapy alone at 12 weeks, with medium between-group effect sizes on primary depressive symptoms (Cohen's d = .51) and small to medium between-group effect sizes on secondary outcomes (Cohen's d = .07–.55). No negative side effects were observed in the blended format (e.g., working alliance was not lower than in psychotherapy alone). The study had a smaller than planned sample size and a high dropout rate at follow-up.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were assessed at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up. The intervention consisted of 9 weeks of immediate program access.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 320 (immediate program access as add-on to treatment-as-usual) and C = 76 (9-week delayed-access plus treatment-as-usual)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 9 weeks of immediate access to the Deprexis web-based program as an add-on to treatment-as-usual (N=320). Control arm: 9-week delayed-access plus treatment-as-usual (N=76). Randomization was 80:20 weighted.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depression severity measured by the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up. Secondary outcome: Social functioning measured by the Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSA) at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up. Subjective helpfulness of the program was also assessed.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: 55% (216/396) completed post-measurement at 9 weeks. Available case analyses: significant reduction in depression severity (BDI), Cohen's d = 0.64 (95% CI 0.33–0.94); significant improvement in social functioning (WSA), Cohen's d = 0.64 (95% CI 0.33–0.95). Improvements maintained at 6-month follow-up. Intention-to-treat analyses: BDI Cohen's d = 0.30 (95% CI 0.05–0.55); WSA Cohen's d = 0.36 (95% CI 0.10–0.61). Intervention group had significantly higher rate of depression symptom reduction (BDI OR = 6.8, 95% CI 2.90–18.19) and recovery (OR = 17.3, 95% CI 2.3–130). More than 80% of users felt the program was helpful.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months post randomization (secondary endpoint)
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint is at 3 months post randomization; secondary endpoint is at 6 months post randomization.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: regular psychotherapy plus web-based depression program ('deprexis'); Control: regular psychotherapy only. Total N = 800.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (intervention): regular psychotherapy plus the web-based depression program 'deprexis'. Arm 2 (control): regular psychotherapy only (no web-based program).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: depressive symptoms measured with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) at 3 months post randomization. Secondary outcomes: anxiety, somatic symptoms, quality of life, all assessed at 3 months and again at 6 months post randomization. Additional measures: working alliance, feasibility and acceptability of the treatment condition.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants had access to Deprexis (internet-based CBT) for 12 weeks total. After the first 2 weeks, eligible participants were randomized. TILS was administered once a week for 4 weeks following randomization.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: 40 participants were randomized after initial 2-week screening phase: TILS + Deprexis (intervention) vs. sham/placebo + Deprexis (control); exact per-group sizes not specified beyond total of 40 randomized.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (intervention): Deprexis (internet-based CBT) combined with active transcranial infrared laser stimulation (TILS) to the right forehead once a week for 4 weeks. Arm 2 (control): Deprexis combined with sham/placebo stimulation.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology-Self-Report (QIDS-SR) measured at baseline, week 2 (post-screening/pre-randomization), and week 12. Adverse effects were also monitored.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The sham group showed a 43% reduction in QIDS-SR scores from baseline to week 12, while the TILS group showed a 56% reduction, representing an additional 30% reduction attributable to TILS ([56-43]/43 = 30%). Participants who received TILS once a week for 4 weeks showed a significantly greater reduction in depressive symptoms compared to sham/placebo. No adverse effects were reported.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: After 8 weeks, all participants were invited for re-assessment. No follow-up beyond the 8-week primary endpoint is mentioned.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: 140 participants randomly allocated to intervention group (Deprexis online intervention) or wait-list control group; exact per-group sizes not specified in the abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Received access to 'Deprexis', an online intervention for depression, for 8 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Wait-list control group, assessed at the same 8-week timepoint.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms. Secondary outcomes: Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS) and South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS) for problematic gambling symptoms. Moderator variables included baseline gambling-related symptom severity and reasons for gambling (e.g., loneliness). Assessments conducted at baseline and at 8 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: ITT analyses showed the intervention led to a significant reduction in depressive symptoms and gambling-related symptoms compared to the wait-list control group, with moderate to strong effect sizes. PP analyses failed to yield significant results due to high rates of non-completion and limited statistical power. Moderator analyses indicated Deprexis was particularly beneficial in reducing problematic gambling for participants scoring high on baseline gambling-related symptoms and for those who gamble due to loneliness.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, long-term follow-up data collected over 36 months
rows.duration_additional_info: The EVIDENT study conducted assessments at intermediary and long-term time points over 36 months total.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Internet intervention for depression (Deprexis); Control: Care as usual. Total N = 1,013.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Internet-based cognitive-behavioral therapy intervention (Deprexis); Arm 2: Care as usual (control group).
rows.collected_data: Item-level and scale-level depressive symptom scores, quality of life measures (including overall impairment through emotional problems), Network Intervention Analysis variables (symptom-specific effects including worthlessness, fatigue, depressed mood, concentration, activity level). Assessments conducted at baseline, intermediary time points, and long-term follow-up over 36 months.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Item-level analysis showed that scale-level effects can be explained by small item-level effects on most depressive symptoms at all assessment points. Higher baseline scores on certain items predicted overall symptom reduction throughout the entire assessment period. Network intervention analysis revealed that Deprexis directly affected certain symptoms (e.g., worthlessness and fatigue) and certain quality of life aspects (e.g., overall impairment through emotional problems), while other domains were affected indirectly (e.g., depressed mood, concentration, and activity level). The configuration of direct and indirect effects replicated findings from a previous study examining the same intervention. The intervention demonstrated both short-term and long-term effects over 36 months.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months (outcomes assessed at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months)
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasted 12 weeks; outcomes were assessed at baseline, three months (primary endpoint), and six months (follow-up).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 12-week web-based intervention (deprexis) plus care as usual; Control group: care as usual only. Total N = 1013 (individual group sizes not specified in the abstract).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): web-based cognitive behavioral intervention 'deprexis' for 12 weeks in addition to care as usual. Arm 2 (Control): care as usual only.
rows.collected_data: Health-related resource use, use of medication, incapacity to work, direct health care costs (including outpatient treatment costs and psychotherapeutic treatment costs); all collected via patients' self-report at baseline, three months, and six months.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Total direct health care costs decreased in both groups during the study period, but changes from baseline did not significantly differ between groups. Costs for psychotherapeutic treatment decreased in the intervention group (-16.8%, €80) while they increased in the control group (+14.7%, €60); this difference was statistically significant (t(df=685) = 2.57; p = 0.008). The study suggests that deprexis over 12 weeks leads to a decrease in outpatient treatment costs, particularly psychotherapeutic treatment costs.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were allocated to either the intervention group or a waitlist control group for 9 weeks. No follow-up beyond the primary endpoint is mentioned.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis) = 45, Waitlist control = 45; 71 completed the trial (35 intervention, 36 control)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received a fully automated, internet-based cognitive behavioural therapy programme called Deprexis for 9 weeks. Control arm was a waitlist control group for the same 9-week period.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) assessed at 9 weeks via intention-to-treat analysis. Additional tracking included worsening of depressive symptoms from below to above clinical cutoff (BDI >13) and occurrence of suicidal ideation (adverse event monitoring).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: 71 patients completed the trial (35 intervention, 36 control). BDI scores decreased in the Deprexis group and increased in the control group; mean group difference -4.02 points (95% CI -7.26 to -0.79), p=0.015, effect size d=0.53. Worsening of depressive symptoms (BDI >13) occurred in 3 (7%) of 45 control patients and 0 Deprexis patients. No adverse events regarding new suicidal ideation were noted.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Depression symptoms were assessed pre and post intervention at 12 weeks.
rows.study_size_participants: 509 (intervention group analyzed; total RCT sample larger)
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (CAU + Deprexis): 509 participants; Control group: care as usual (CAU) - size not specified in this report
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Care as usual (CAU) alone (control). Arm 2: CAU plus the Internet intervention Deprexis (an online program for depression). Some participants in the intervention group also received e-mail guidance support (those with moderate symptom severity at baseline).
rows.collected_data: Depressive symptoms (pre and post intervention at 12 weeks); patient characteristics (age, sex, depressive symptoms); self-rating screening for mental disorders; attitudes towards online interventions; quality of life; adherence measures (usage time in hours, number of sessions); confidence in effectiveness of the program; guidance/e-mail support receipt.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Average adherence was 7 hours of usage time and 8 sessions. Patient characteristics explained 15–25% of variance in number of sessions. Older age (β = .16) and higher depressive symptoms (β = .15) were associated with higher usage duration. Higher adherence predicted greater symptom reduction over 12 weeks (number of sessions: β = .13, usage duration: β = .14), but this was largely explained by receiving guidance (β = .27 and .26). Effects were small overall.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months and 12 months
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 12 weeks post-test; secondary outcomes also assessed at 6 and 12 months follow-up.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 509 (Deprexis + CAU) and C = 504 (CAU alone)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received online intervention (Deprexis) in addition to care as usual (CAU); control group received CAU alone.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) at baseline, 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; sociodemographic variables including sex, age, educational attainment, employment status, relationship status, and lifetime frequency of depressive episodes.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Between-group differences were observed in post-test scores indicating the effectiveness of Deprexis. While effects of the intervention were demonstrated across all subgroups, some showed larger between-group differences than others. However, none of the selected sociodemographic variables (sex, age, educational attainment, employment status, relationship status, lifetime frequency of episodes) was found to moderate treatment outcomes based on linear regression models. The findings suggest Deprexis is equally beneficial across a wide range of participant characteristics.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: This is a reanalysis of data from the EVIDENT trial examining anxiety-related comorbidities as moderators of an Internet-based intervention for depression. The primary endpoint duration is not specified in this publication.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Deprexis + care-as-usual): n = 509; Control group (care-as-usual only): n = 504
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: access to the Internet intervention Deprexis adjunctively to care-as-usual. Control arm: access to care-as-usual only.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depression severity (scores 5–14 at baseline); Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ) for anxiety-related comorbidities at baseline (generalized anxiety disorder, social phobia, panic disorder, agoraphobia, panic disorder with agoraphobia, specific phobia, posttraumatic stress disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder); depression severity as continuous PHQ-9 scores and clinically important PHQ-9 changes as outcomes; multilevel models examining 12 potential moderators (8 anxiety comorbidities, depression severity, and 3 previously identified moderators).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Only social phobia moderated the intervention effect on depression outcome in favor of Deprexis (p < .05), among 12 potential moderators examined. This moderating effect was present for both continuous PHQ-9 scores and clinically important PHQ-9 changes. However, the moderating effect did not reach small effect sizes, accounted for less than 1% of the variance in change in depressive symptoms, and showed limited reproducibility in randomly selected split halves. Deprexis appears most effective for participants with mild to moderate depressive symptoms and comorbid social phobia, but further replications are necessary.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Data collected at baseline and posttreatment; no specific week duration for primary endpoint stated in the abstract.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: 223 patients completed the WAI-I; all received a guided Internet intervention (deprexis) for mild to moderate depression. Specific group sizes not detailed in the abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Multicenter trial examining a guided Internet intervention (deprexis) for patients with mild to moderate depression.
rows.collected_data: Working Alliance Inventory adapted for guided Internet interventions (WAI-I) at posttreatment; Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire (ZUF-8) at posttreatment; Attitudes toward Psychological Online-Interventions Questionnaire (APOI) at baseline. Confirmatory factor analyses (CFA) testing two- and three-factor solutions, Cronbach's α, item-total correlations, and correlations of WAI-I with APOI and ZUF-8.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Results suggested a two-factor solution for the WAI-I with very good model fit and evidence of factor independency, adequate internal consistency, and external validity for the complete scale and sub-scales. The WAI-I was found to be a reliable and valid instrument to capture alliance in guided Internet interventions.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes, 36 months (3-year follow-up)
rows.duration_additional_info: Early change patterns were assessed in the first 4 weeks of the intervention; long-term remission was tracked over 36 months. The study is a follow-up analysis of a previously conducted RCT.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: 409 individuals experiencing mild to moderate depressive symptoms using the web-based intervention deprexis; specific group sizes not detailed in the abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants used the web-based intervention 'deprexis' for mild to moderate depressive symptoms. The RCT involved a web-based intervention arm; control arm details not specified in the abstract.
rows.collected_data: Early change patterns in depressive symptoms during the first four weeks of intervention (used to identify three latent classes: early response after registration, early response after screening, and early deterioration); baseline variables; onset of remission over 36 months assessed via Stepwise Cox Proportional Hazard Multiple Regression.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Three latent classes were identified based on early change in the first four weeks: early response after registration, early response after screening, and early deterioration. Early change class was a significant predictor of remission over 36 months. Compared to early deterioration after screening, both early response after registration and after screening were associated with a higher likelihood of remission. In sensitivity and secondary analyses, only change class consistently emerged as a predictor of long-term outcome. Early improvement in depression symptoms predicted long-term outcome, and those showing early improvement had a higher likelihood of long-term remission.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6-month and 12-month follow-up
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants completed the PHQ-9 biweekly during the 12-week treatment period. Follow-up assessments were conducted at 6 and 12 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: All 483 participants were randomized to a 12-week Internet-based intervention (deprexis); specific control group size not reported in this abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to a 12-week Internet-based intervention (deprexis, NCT01636752). The abstract does not detail a separate control arm but references randomization, suggesting a comparator arm existed in the original trial.
rows.collected_data: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) administered biweekly during the 12-week treatment; dropout indicators; change-dropout patterns assessed at 6-month and 12-month follow-up; reliable change rates.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Four change-dropout patterns were identified using the Muthen-Roy model: C1 (N=198): low impairment, improvement, high dropout probability; C2 (N=32): high impairment, deterioration, high dropout probability; C3 (N=134): high impairment, improvement, low dropout probability — this group had the highest rate of reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up; C4 (N=119): high impairment, no change, low dropout probability. Both deterioration and rapid improvement (perceived 'good enough' dosage) were associated with dropout.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment/primary endpoint), and 6 months (follow-up). The intervention duration was 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis POI) vs. Care as Usual (CAU) control; subset of N=577 from the EVIDENT trial, split into Millennials (18-35 years) and Baby Boomers (50-65 years) subgroups.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: 12-week online psychological intervention (Deprexis); Arm 2: Care as usual (CAU). The study also compared two age subgroups (Millennials aged 18-35 and Baby Boomers aged 50-65) within these arms.
rows.collected_data: PHQ-9 (self-rated depression severity) at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment), and 6 months (follow-up); intervention usage frequency and duration; attitudes towards psychological online interventions (POIs).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Boomers used the intervention significantly more often than Millennials (d=0.45) and for a longer duration (d=0.46), and endorsed more positive attitudes towards POIs (d=0.14). There was no significant Age Group by Intervention Group interaction for change in PHQ-9. Post-assessment between-group effect sizes (intervention vs. CAU) for Millennials and Boomers were d=0.26 and d=0.39, respectively, and were stable at 6-month follow-up (d=0.37 and d=0.39). The POI was superior to CAU and comparably effective across both age groups.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The publication date is November 2019; no specific primary endpoint duration or follow-up timing is mentioned in the abstract.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Deprexis®) and control group (care as usual); total N=1013, specific group sizes not reported in the abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received the internet-based intervention Deprexis® in addition to any type of treatment; control arm received care as usual (any type of treatment permitted). Both groups were permitted to use any type of treatment.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) assessing nine depressive symptoms; overall depression severity; treatment condition; changes in all nine PHQ symptoms; network estimation including treatment condition and symptom changes.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Of nine depressive symptoms assessed with the PHQ, seven showed larger improvements in the Deprexis intervention condition relative to care as usual (effect sizes ranging from 0.15 to 0.31). No significant differences were found for the two remaining symptoms. Network analysis indicated that four of the seven symptom-specific effects were direct, while three were indirect (mediated by effects on other symptoms). Exploratory analyses showed the intervention was more effective in improving overall depression severity for participants with higher baseline scores on the four directly affected symptoms.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes (follow-up studies mentioned in MeSH terms)
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period was 3 months; this study is a subgroup analysis of the EVIDENT trial.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received the Internet intervention 'deprexis'; control group received care as usual. Total N=1,004.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Internet intervention (deprexis, a depression-focused iCBT program); Arm 2: Control group (care as usual).
rows.collected_data: Attitudes toward Internet interventions (pre and post), depression symptoms (mild-to-moderate), usage time of the deprexis program, efficacy of the Internet intervention over a 3-month period. Attitude measures assessed at pre- and post-intervention.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Positive initial attitudes toward Internet interventions were associated with greater efficacy (η2p = .014) independent of usage time. A negative attitude (perceived lack of personal contact) was associated with reduced efficacy (η2p = .012). Users' attitudes changed during the trial, and both the magnitude and direction of attitude change were associated with the efficacy of the program over time (η2p = .030). Conclusions: Internet interventions may be most beneficial for individuals with positive attitudes toward them.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months (follow-up assessment at 6 months after baseline)
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment/primary endpoint), and 6 months (follow-up). The intervention itself was 12 weeks in duration.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Participants randomized to either a 12-week Internet intervention (Deprexis) adjunctive to usual care or care as usual alone (CAU); total N=1013 (specific group sizes not reported in abstract).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: 12-week Internet intervention (Deprexis) in addition to usual care. Arm 2: Care as usual (CAU) alone. Both arms included participants with mild to moderate depressive symptoms (PHQ-9 score 5-14).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-rated depression severity measured by the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment), and 6 months (follow-up). Minimally clinically important change in PHQ-9 was also tracked. The trial used linear mixed models for the main intention-to-treat analysis.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001 for the main effect of group). Post-assessment between-group effect size in favour of the intervention: d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13–0.64). Effect was stable at 6-month follow-up: d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06–0.69). Rate of participants experiencing at least minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%), with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5–10). The Internet intervention was superior to CAU alone in reducing mild to moderate depressive symptoms.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention was a 12-week minimally guided Internet intervention (Deprexis). Outcomes were assessed at baseline, post-treatment (12 weeks), and 6-month follow-up.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Augmented therapy group (Internet intervention + routine psychotherapy) n = 166; Standard psychotherapy group (routine psychotherapy only) n = 174
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (augmented therapy): routine outpatient psychotherapy plus adjunctive 12-week minimally guided Internet intervention (Deprexis) for mild to moderate depression. Arm 2 (standard psychotherapy): routine outpatient psychotherapy alone.
rows.collected_data: Symptoms of depression, therapeutic progress, mental health-related quality of life, physical health-related quality of life, rates of clinical improvement. Assessments conducted at baseline, post-treatment (12 weeks), and 6-month follow-up.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analyses showed combined treatment led to greater reduction in depression symptoms (effect size d = 0.32; p = .002), improved therapeutic progress (d = 0.36; p = .003), and higher mental health-related quality of life (d = 0.34; p = .004). No significant intervention effect on physical health-related quality of life. The same pattern was found at 6-month follow-up, and adjunctive treatment also resulted in increased rates of clinical improvement. Treatment success was independent of the therapeutic orientation of the combined face-to-face therapy.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, up to 12 months (one year) from baseline
rows.duration_additional_info: The 12-week internet intervention was followed by regular PHQ-9 assessments over twelve months total. The between-group effect decreased from d=0.36 at three months to d=0.13 at twelve months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care; Control group: care as usual alone. Total N=1013 randomized.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus care as usual. Arm 2: Care as usual alone (no internet intervention).
rows.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity assessed using PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) measured regularly over twelve months. Remission rates over time were tracked. Subgroup analysis by antidepressant medication use was also conducted.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Remission rates over time were significantly higher in the intervention group (Cox regression: HR 1.31; p=0.009). The intervention was more effective in the subgroup not taking antidepressant medication (Cox regression: HR 1.88; p<0.001). PHQ-9 change from baseline was greater in the intervention group (linear mixed model [LMM]: p<0.001), with the between-group effect gradually decreasing from d=0.36 at three months to d=0.13 at twelve months (LMM: group by time interaction: p<0.001).
