Conclusion: The internet intervention contributed to achieving remission in people with mild to moderate depressive symptoms, especially those not on antidepressant medication.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at 3 months (end of 12-week intervention) and again at 6 months follow-up.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care; Control: care-as-usual alone. Exact group sizes not reported in this abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): 12-week internet-based intervention (Deprexis) plus care-as-usual. Arm 2 (Control): care-as-usual alone.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-rated depression severity using Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) at 3 months and 6 months. Secondary measures: demographic and clinical parameters, attitudes towards internet interventions measured by the Attitudes towards Psychological Online Interventions Questionnaire (APOI). Recruitment source was also recorded and examined as a moderator.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Recruitment source was associated with very few demographic and clinical characteristics. Participants recruited through insurance companies were more likely to be employed compared to clinically recruited participants. Clinically recruited participants were as severely affected as those from other recruitment sources but more sceptical of internet interventions. The effectiveness of the intervention was not differentially associated with recruitment source (treatment by recruitment source interaction = 0.28, p = 0.84). The intervention was found to be effective across different recruitment sources including clinical settings.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months (T2)
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months (T1) post-randomization; additional follow-up at 6 months (T2).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (IG): n = 304 (lancivis + TAU); Control group (CG): n = 300 (TAU only)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the digital CBT-based program lancivis alongside treatment as usual (TAU); control group received TAU only.
rows.collected_data: Quality of life (QoL), cancer-related fatigue, distress symptoms, and fear of cancer progression. Assessments conducted at baseline, 3 months (T1), and 6 months (T2). Intent-to-treat (ITT) analyses used.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: ITT analyses revealed significant improvements in fatigue in the IG compared to the CG after 3 months (T1; Cohen's d = 0.29) and 6 months (T2; Cohen's d = 0.31). Participants in the IG reported significantly lower fear of cancer progression than the CG at T1 and T2, and lower levels of distress symptoms at T2. No between-group differences were found for health-related QoL. Results suggest that lancivis may present a beneficial addition to psycho-oncological care services.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: No additional follow-up beyond the 12-week observation period is described; assessments occur at baseline (U1), ~3 weeks (U2), ~6 weeks (U3), and ~12 weeks (U4).
rows.duration_additional_info: Data are collected under routine conditions over an observation period of 3 months (approximately 12 weeks), with four assessment time points: baseline (U1), ~3 weeks after U1 (U2), ~6 weeks after U1 (U3), and ~12 weeks after U1 (U4).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single observational group of ambulatory patients using deprexis®24 (with or without antidepressive comedication); no separate control group. Total participants: 104 (planned: 1800).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One observational arm: ambulatory patients with depressive mood or depression using the online psychotherapy program deprexis®24 under routine care, with or without antidepressive comedication.
rows.collected_data: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9), short version Montgomery Asberg Depression Scale (svMADRS), Clinical Global Impression-Severity/Improvement (CGI-S/CGI-I), Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) — all collected at all 4 visits (U1–U4); Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ-4) at U4; standardized questionnaires on compliance with the online psychotherapy program (U2–U4); questionnaire on internet usage behavior (U2–U4); standardized documentation of comedication (U1–U4); standardized questionnaire on safety-related incidents and adverse events under possible comedication (U2–U4).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline and at weeks 3, 6, 9, and 12. Patients received 12-week access to the SMI in addition to usual care.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single intervention group only (n = 104); no control group. Patients received 12-week access to deprexis in addition to usual care.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Uncontrolled observational study with one arm: patients with depressive disorder received access to the internet-based self-management intervention deprexis for 12 weeks in addition to their usual care (76.0% also took concomitant antidepressant medication). Optional guidance by the treating physician was available but only a minority (n = 7, 8.0%) received the guided version.
rows.collected_data: Clinician-rated short version of the Montgomery Åsberg Depression Scale (svMADRS); Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) self-rating for depressive symptoms; usage data (number of sessions, whether intervention was used at least once); receipt of guided vs. unguided intervention; concomitant antidepressant medication use. Assessments at baseline and weeks 3, 6, 9, and 12.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Most patients used the intervention at least once (n = 87, 83.6%); mean number of sessions among users was 18.05 (SD = 11.33). Only 7 patients (8.0%) received the guided version. svMADRS scores decreased significantly from 29.72 (SD = 10.03) at baseline to 15.73 (SD = 9.74) at week 12 (Cohen's d = 1.42, 95% CI 0.08–2.76). PHQ-9 scores decreased from 15.20 (SD = 5.03) to 8.77 (SD = 5.03) (Cohen's d = 1.29, 95% CI 0.60–1.97). The pre-post effect size was comparable to that reported in an RCT using the same intervention in patients with similar symptom severity.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 3 months after training
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention programme lasts 8 weeks (8 modules). Primary outcome is assessed immediately after training and again 3 months later. The study ran from July 2014 until May 2015 (completion date 26/05/2015).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (IG) received access to the online ELEVIDA fatigue management programme; Control group (CG) was a wait-list control group that received the programme after the study completed. Total target enrollment: 204.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention Group): Participants received a personal access code to the ELEVIDA online CBT-based fatigue training tool for 8 weeks. Arm 2 (Wait-list Control Group): Participants were informed about their option to enter the FATIMA programme after the study and received a brief explanation about the need for control groups in RCTs.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Fatigue measured by the Chalder Fatigue Scale (Cella & Chalder et al., 2010) assessed pre- and post-training immediately after and 3 months later. Secondary outcomes: 1. FSMC (Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions, Penner et al., 2009); 2. Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS, Zigmond & Snaith, 1983); 3. Hamburg Quality of Life Questionnaire in MS (HAQUAMS, Gold et al., 2001); 4. Multiple Sclerosis Neuropsychological Screening Questionnaire (MSNQ, Benedict et al., 2003). Exploratory: 5. Cognitive and Behavioural Responses to Symptoms Questionnaire (Skerett & Moss-Morris, 2006); 6. Brief Illness Perception Questionnaire (Broadbent et al., 2006); 7. Coping Self Efficacy Scale (CSES, Chesney et al., 2006); 8. Frenchay Activity Index (Turnbull et al., 2000); 9. Revised NEO Personality Inventory (Costa & McCrae, 1992). A subgroup also underwent clinical examination and MRI (DTI and resting state fMRI connectivity measures). Screening used HAQUAMS fatigue subscale (score ≥2) and FSMC total fatigue score ≥43 for inclusion.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at week 24 (12 weeks after primary endpoint)
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasted 12 weeks; outcomes were assessed at baseline, week 12 (post-intervention/primary endpoint), and week 24 (follow-up).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 139 (ELEVIDA online intervention) and C = 136 (waitlist control group)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 12-week self-guided interactive online fatigue management programme (ELEVIDA) based on CBT and related psychotherapeutic approaches (e.g., mindfulness), n=139, 82% female, mean age 40.8, median PDDS 3.0. Control arm: waitlist control group, n=136, 79% female, mean age 41.9, median PDDS 3.0.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Chalder Fatigue Scale (assessed at baseline, week 12, and week 24). Secondary outcomes not explicitly listed in the abstract. Patient Determined Disease Steps (PDDS) used as a measure of disability at baseline. Randomisation was 1:1 with no blocking or stratification.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12 (primary endpoint), the ELEVIDA group showed significantly greater reductions in Chalder Fatigue Scale scores compared to control (intention-to-treat: between-group mean difference 2.74 points; 95% CI 1.16 to 4.32; p=0.0007; effect size d=0.53). Effects were sustained at week 24 (intention-to-treat: between-group mean difference 2.19 points; 95% CI 0.57 to 3.82; p=0.0080). The trial provides evidence for the effectiveness of a self-guided internet-based intervention to reduce fatigue in MS.

rows.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; uncontrolled follow-up at 6 and 12 months after randomisation
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months (post-treatment) after randomisation. Uncontrolled follow-up assessments for both intervention and control groups at 6 and 12 months after randomisation. Control group participants receive free access to the online programme after the 3-month data collection period if they wish.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (IG): direct access to HelloBetter ratiopharm chronic pain digital therapy programme (7 units, ACT-based); Control group (CG): care-as-usual (CAU) waiting control group with unrestricted access to routine care. Total planned and enrolled: 360 participants.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Immediate access to the web-based ACT programme HelloBetter ratiopharm chronic pain (7 units), assessed at baseline, post (3 months), and follow-up at 6 and 12 months after randomisation. Arm 2 (Control): Care-as-usual (CAU) waiting control group with unrestricted access to all routine care options (GP, pain physicians, psychotherapy, medication, physical therapy, exercise therapy); assessed at baseline, 3, 6, and 12 months after randomisation. After 3-month data collection, control participants may receive free access to the programme on request.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain interference measured by the West Haven-Yale Multidimensional Pain Inventory (MPI) interference scale at post-treatment (3 months after randomisation). Secondary outcomes (collected at baseline, post/3 months, 6-month and 12-month follow-up unless noted): Emotional functioning (PHQ-9); Health-related quality of life (AQoL-8D); Pain intensity and interference (Brief Pain Inventory, BPI); Multidimensional pain interference including pain intensity, self-control, negative mood (MPI); Pain impairment in everyday life (Pain Disability Index, PDI); Anxiety (GAD-7); Pain processing (FESV); Pain acceptance (Chronic Pain Acceptance Questionnaire, CPAQ); Psychological flexibility (FAH-II / AAQ-II; Psychological Inflexibility in Pain Scale, PIPS); Pain-specific self-efficacy (Pain Self-Efficacy Questionnaire, PSEQ); Patient satisfaction (CSQ-8/ZUF-8, post, IG only); Negative effects (NEQ, post, IG only); Subjective global assessment of change (PGIC, post).

rows.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The study includes 3 visits per patient: baseline (V1), telephone visit after 4 weeks (V2, intervention group only), and a visit at month 3 (V3, all patients). The primary endpoint is assessed at month 3 (approximately 12 weeks). The online course (HelloBetter app) consists of seven weekly units of 45-60 minutes each.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: 1:1 randomization; total enrolled: 136 (planned: 250). Group 1 Intervention: axSpA patients prescribed the HelloBetter ACT-app in addition to standard therapy. Group 2 Control: axSpA patients receiving standard therapy without the HelloBetter app. Exact per-group sizes not reported.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients with axSpA who continue to suffer from pain despite adequate anti-inflammatory medication are prescribed the digital health application (DiGA) 'HelloBetter ratiopharm chronic pain®' (HelloBetter ACT-app), an interactive online behavioral therapy/ACT program consisting of seven weekly units of 45-60 minutes. Visits: V1 (baseline), V2 (telephone visit at 4 weeks to verify app activation), V3 (month 3). Arm 2 (Control): Patients with axSpA who continue to suffer from pain despite adequate anti-inflammatory medication receive standard therapy without the HelloBetter app. Visits: V1 (baseline) and V3 (month 3).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Change in the pain interference scale of the West Haven-Yale Multidimensional Pain Inventory (MPI-D) at month 3. Secondary outcomes and instruments collected at baseline (V1) and month 3 (V3): NRS global pain (0-10), back pain (BASDAI question 2), nocturnal back pain, inflammatory back pain (BASDAI questions 5 and 6), morning stiffness (BASDAI questions 5 and 6), West Haven-Yale Multidimensional Pain Inventory (MPI-D), neuropathic pain (painDetect questionnaire), PASS (patient acceptable symptom state), Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS), Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Score (ASDAS), Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Index (BASDAI), Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Functional Index (BASFI), Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index (BASMI), modified Short Questionnaire to Assess Health-enhancing Physical Activity (mSQUASH), ASAS Health Index (ASAS HI), Fibromyalgia Impact Questionnaire (FIQ), Short Form 36 (SF36), patient and physician global assessment, Patient Activation Measure (PAM12), patient global impression of change (V3 only). Intervention group only at V3: benefit from app use (yes/no), recommendation likelihood (NRS 0-10), Mobile Application Rating Scale (MARS), system usability questionnaire. V2 (intervention group only): app activation status (dichotomous yes/no). Demographic characteristics including educational and employment status, current pharmacological and non-pharmacological therapy.

rows.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months after randomisation (T2)
rows.duration_additional_info: T0 = before randomisation, T1 = 9 weeks after randomisation (post-intervention), T2 = 6 months after randomisation (follow-up). The intervention consists of 7 lessons to be processed weekly.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Three arms: (1) GET.ON chronic pain guided (with e-coach feedback emails after each lesson), (2) GET.ON chronic pain unguided (no e-coach feedback, self-directed), (3) waitlist control group. Total planned: 300, total enrolled: 302.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Guided internet-based ACT intervention (GET.ON chronic pain guided) - 7 weekly lessons with feedback emails from an e-coach after each lesson, plus optional SMS coach. Arm 2: Unguided internet-based ACT intervention (GET.ON chronic pain unguided) - same 7 weekly lessons but without e-coach feedback, plus optional SMS coach. Arm 3: Waitlist control group - receives no active intervention during the study period.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain interference measured by the MPI Interference Scale at T0 (pre), T1 (9 weeks post-randomisation), and T2 (6 months post-randomisation). Secondary outcomes: At screening - demographic variables (sex, age, education, social support), prior pain treatments, pain type, chronicity, comorbidities (self-report). At T0, T1, T2 - Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), Generalised Anxiety Disorder Screener (GAD-7), Pain intensity (11-point NRS), Quality of life: SF-12 and EQ-5D, Acceptance and Action Questionnaire-II (AAQ-II / FAH-II), Chronic Pain Acceptance Questionnaire (CPAQ-D), adapted TiC-P (costs associated with psychiatric illness). Treatment process (after lessons 2, 4, 6): FAH-II and CPAQ-D. At T1 and T2: Patient Global Impression of Change scale (PGIC). Active groups only at T1: intervention adherence (attrition rate), satisfaction with intervention (CSQ-8 / ZUF-8).

rows.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months and 12 months after baseline
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention consists of a 6-week online training (6 lessons, one per week), followed by a 7th lesson 4 weeks after the final lesson. Primary endpoint is assessed at 8 weeks (post-intervention), with follow-ups at 6 and 12 months after baseline.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (N=130): access to online program 'Get.On Diabetes and depressive symptoms'; Control group (N=130): psychoeducation materials about depression
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention, N=130): Six-week online training with 6 lessons (one per week, ~45 min each), guided self-help via weekly email contact, optional SMS support, and a 7th lesson 4 weeks after the final lesson. Arm 2 (Control, N=130): Receives psychoeducation materials on depression; after the last measurement point (12 months), the control group also gets access to the online training.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depressive symptoms measured by the Center for Epidemiological Studies Depression Scale (CES-D / Allgemeine Depressionsskala, ADS): Pre, Post (8 weeks), 6 and 12 months follow-up. Secondary outcomes: Demographic questionnaire (Pre); Worry (Penn State Worry Questionnaire, PSWQ, short version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Activation (Behavioural Activation Depression Scale, BA-DS, short version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Problem solving (Social Problem Solving Inventory, short version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Long-term blood sugar level (HbA1c): Pre, 6 and 12 months; Problem areas in diabetes (PAID, short version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Acceptance and Action Diabetes Questionnaire (AADQ, German version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Diabetes Self-Management Questionnaire (DSMQ): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Depression (HADS-D): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Therapy expectation (Pathev): Pre (intervention group only); Negative effects of online health trainings (based on INEP): Post (intervention group only); Attitude toward seeking professional help (ATSPHS): Pre, Post; Satisfaction with the program (adaptation ZUF-8): Post (intervention group only); Quality of life (EuroQol): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Quality of life (SF-12): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Costs associated with psychiatric illness (German adaptation of TiC-P): Pre, 6 and 12 months.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, secondary outcome on movement and health competence (PAHCO) assessed at 24 weeks
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention group uses the DiGA Kaia Health app for 12 weeks (10-30 minutes daily). One secondary outcome (PAHCO) is assessed at both 12 and 24 weeks, indicating a follow-up period extending to 24 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Planned 120 participants total; Intervention group receives DiGA Kaia Health app over 12 weeks; Control group receives standard prevention for back pain. Exact group sizes not specified.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Subjects receive the DiGA Kaia Health app over 12 weeks, with exercises performed daily for 10-30 minutes. Arm 2 (Control): Standard prevention for back pain.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes (at 12 weeks): Physical function (IPAQ, HAQ); Quality of life (SF-36); Sleep behavior (PSQI); Depressive moods (BDI). Secondary outcomes: Movement and health competence (PAHCO) at 12 and 24 weeks; Gait pattern via sensor-based gait analysis at 12 weeks; Mobility (BASMI) at 12 weeks; Compliance with DiGA Kaia use (Compliance Questionnaire).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed at day 90 (approximately 13 weeks) from baseline. Secondary outcomes were also assessed at 30 and 60 days. The study was prematurely terminated on 20.03.2023 by the sponsor due to: (1) recruitment challenges, (2) 'Kaia Back Pain' having already achieved permanent DiGA listing on 3 February 2023, and (3) ethical concerns about withholding a proven effective therapy from the control group.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Two parallel arms: Intervention group uses Kaia Back Pain app in addition to usual care for 90 days; Control group receives usual care only (with access to Kaia Back Pain app at end of study). Total enrolled: 87 participants (planned 210).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Control): Usual care only, with access to Kaia Back Pain app provided at end of study. Arm 2 (Intervention): Usual care plus use of the Kaia Back Pain smartphone app for 90 days.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Mean change in Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) score from baseline to day 90. Secondary outcomes: (1) Percentage of patients with ≥30% reduction in ODI from baseline to study end; (2) Health-related Quality of Life via Short Form Health Survey 12 (SF-12); (3) Pain intensity on an 11-point NRS (average pain in previous week) from baseline to study end; (4) Depression, anxiety, and stress via Depression-Anxiety-Stress-Scale (DASS); (5) Percentage of partial responders (average pain ≤2 on 11-point NRS); (6) Percentage of complete responders (average pain of 0 on 11-point NRS); (7) Physical function via ODI version 2.1b at 30 and 60 days; (8) Pain intensity on 11-point NRS at 30 and 60 days.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 12 and 24 weeks
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint (pain index on NRS) is assessed at enrollment and at 6-, 12-, and 24-week follow-up. The total trial duration is 6 months. Control group patients receive standard physical therapy once a week for 6 weeks, then gain access to the kaia app from week 12 until trial end.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: kaia LBP app used self-administered on own mobile device throughout the 6-month trial period (at least 4 times per week). Control group: standard physical therapy once a week for 6 sessions plus weekly online education links; after week 12, control group also receives access to kaia app until trial end. Total N = 101.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Treatment with kaia back pain app for the entire 6-month study duration, self-administered by patients on their own smartphone/tablet, encouraged to use app at least four times a week. Arm 2 (Control): Standard physiotherapy at an affiliated center once a week for 6 sessions total, plus weekly email with links to online LBP education resources (6 links total); after week 12, control group patients are granted access to the kaia app until trial end at 6 months.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain index calculated as the mean of current pain, maximum pain, and average pain over the last 4 weeks, each measured on an 11-point Numeric Rating Scale (NRS; 0 = no pain, 10 = unbearable pain), assessed at enrollment and at 6-, 12-, and 24-week follow-up. Secondary outcomes: Functional capability (Hannover Functional Ability Questionnaire for back pain, HFAQ/FFbH-R), health-related quality of life (VR-12), Graded Chronic Pain Scale (GCPS/von Korff questionnaire), and pain self-efficacy (FESS/pain self-efficacy questionnaire), all assessed at enrollment and at 6-, 12-, and 24-week follow-up.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 12 months post-baseline
rows.duration_additional_info: Measurements collected at pre-treatment (T0), 3 months (T1), 6 months (T2), 9 months (T3), and 12 months (T4). The trial was terminated early due to the COVID-19 pandemic; participant recruitment was stopped. Primary completion date was 30 September 2021 and full completion date was 30 April 2022.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Optimune): immediate 12-month access to web-based CBT program Optimune plus care-as-usual; Control group (Care-as-Usual/waitlist): care-as-usual with access to Optimune delayed by 6 months. Exact group sizes not reported.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental - Optimune): Participants receive immediate 12-month access to the web-based psychological intervention Optimune (CBT-based, including sleep/stress management, mindfulness, dietary and physical activity advice) plus care-as-usual. Arm 2 (Active Comparator - Care-as-Usual): Participants continue with any treatment they require (care-as-usual) and receive access to Optimune only after a 6-month delay (waitlist control).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes (change from baseline to 6 months, also at 12 months): plasma CRP concentration, circulating IL-6, circulating TNF-α, stimulated IL-6 (LPS-stimulated PBMCs), stimulated TNF-α (LPS-stimulated PBMCs). Secondary outcomes: plasma cytokine concentrations (IL-1β, IFN-α, IFN-γ, MCP-1, IL-8, IL-10, IL-12p70, IL-17A, IL-18, IL-23, IL-33 via CBA); secreted cytokines after PMA stimulation of PBMCs; secreted cytokines after LPS stimulation of PBMCs; circulating lymphocytes, monocytes, granulocytes (TruCount Flow Cytometry); T and NK cell subset phenotyping (11-color Flow Cytometry); cortisol awakening response (CAR) from saliva; Brief Fatigue Inventory (BFI-9); Impact of Event Scale-Revised (IES-R); Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9); Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD-7); Fear of Progression questionnaire (PA-F12); program usefulness questionnaire; Inventory for the Assessment of Negative Effects of Psychotherapy (INEP). Other outcomes: recurrence of breast cancer, frequency of common cold/virus flu, frequency of unscheduled medical encounters.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months post-baseline (and for some secondary outcomes at 9 and 12 months post-baseline)
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months post-baseline (T1); additional assessments at 6 months post-baseline (T2). Some secondary outcomes (e.g., IES-R) also assessed at 9 and 12 months post-baseline. The intervention (Optimune) is accessible for 365 days after registration. The control group receives access to Optimune after a delay of 6 months (wait list). Primary completion date: June 2020; study completion date: September 2020.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Optimune): immediate 12-month access to web-based intervention plus care as usual; Control group (Care-as-Usual/wait list): care as usual with access to Optimune delayed by 6 months post-baseline. Total N = 360.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental - Optimune): Participants receive immediate 12-month access to Optimune, a web-based CBT-based psychological intervention including techniques targeting depression, anxiety, fatigue, sleep, stress management (mindfulness), and lifestyle optimization (diet and physical activity), plus care as usual. Arm 2 (Active Comparator - Care-as-Usual): Participants continue with any treatment they require (care as usual) and receive access to Optimune after a 6-month delay (wait list control).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes (assessed at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months post-baseline): Quality of Life (WHOQOL-BREF, 26-item, 5-point scale, range 26-130); Physical Activity (IPAQ-short, 7-item questionnaire, min/week and sitting time); Diet Quality (Food Quality Questionnaire/FQQ, 10-item, 5-point scale, range 0-39). Secondary outcomes: Cancer-related fatigue (Brief Fatigue Inventory/BFI-9, 9-item, 11-point scale, range 0-90; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Cancer-related emotional stress (IES-R, 22-item, 5-point scale, range 0-88; assessed at baseline, 3, 6, 9, and 12 months); Depression (PHQ-9, range 0-27; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Anxiety (GAD-7, range 0-21; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Fear of progression (PA-F12, 12-item, 5-point Likert scale, range 12-60; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Insomnia (ISI/Insomnia Severity Index, range 0-28; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Usefulness of the program (questionnaire; assessed at 3 and 6 months); Negative effects (INEP/Inventory for the Assessment of Negative Effects of Psychotherapy; assessed at 3 and 6 months). Other outcomes (assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months): Recurrence of breast cancer (questionnaire); Frequency of common cold or virus flu (questionnaire); Frequency of unscheduled medical encounters (questionnaire); Body Mass Index (height and weight via questionnaire); Psychotherapy initiation during trial (questionnaire).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes, follow-up surveys ongoing after primary endpoint at 3 months
rows.duration_additional_info: The online program optimune is used for 3 months in addition to usual treatment. Intervention group has access to optimune for 365 days. The recruitment target (680 participants) was not achieved within the designated timeframe; follow-up surveys are still ongoing.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: access to online program optimune for 365 days in addition to routine treatment (care as usual). Control group: routine treatment (care as usual) only, with access to optimune for 365 days after study completion. Total enrolled: 405 (planned: 680).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Access to the internet-based self-help program optimune for 365 days in addition to routine treatment (care as usual). Arm 2 (Control): Routine treatment (care as usual) only during the study; access to optimune for 365 days granted after study completion.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Quality of life measured by the total score of the Assessment of Quality of Life (AQoL-8D) at T1 (3 months after baseline). Secondary outcomes: General distress (Depression-Anxiety-Stress-Scales, DASS-21); Work and social ability (Work and Social Adjustment Scale, WSAS); Fatigue (Brief Fatigue Inventory, BFI); Responder rate of AQoL-8D; Subscales and dimensions of AQoL-8D.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; control group received Optimune after a 3-month delay, and intervention group had 12-month access to Optimune, implying follow-up beyond the 3-month primary endpoint
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoints assessed at 3 months post-randomization. Participants in the intervention group received 12-month access to Optimune. The control group received Optimune after a delay of 3 months (waitlist control).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (n = 181): care as usual (CAU) plus immediate 12-month access to Optimune; Control group (n = 182): CAU plus Optimune after a 3-month delay (waitlist control)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): CAU + immediate access to Optimune (CBT-based Internet intervention) for 12 months. Arm 2 (Control): CAU only for 3 months, then access to Optimune after the 3-month delay.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Quality of life (QoL), physical activity, and dietary habits assessed at 3 months. Measures included patient-reported outcomes via questionnaires on QoL, physical exercise, and dietary habits. Intention-to-treat (ITT) analyses were conducted.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: ITT analyses at 3 months showed significant effects on QoL (d = 0.27, 95% CI: 0.07–0.48) and dietary habits (d = 0.36, 95% CI: 0.15–0.56). The effect on physical exercise was not statistically significant (d = 0.30; 95% CI: 0.10–0.51). The authors concluded that Optimune is effective in improving quality of life and dietary habits in breast cancer survivors.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes – the control group received Optimune after a 3-month delay, implying continued observation; intervention group had 12-month access to Optimune
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoints were assessed at 3 months (approximately 12 weeks). Participants in the intervention group received 12-month access to Optimune immediately after randomization. The control group received care as usual and then gained access to Optimune after a 3-month delay.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (n = 181): care as usual (CAU) plus immediate 12-month access to Optimune; Control group (n = 182): CAU plus access to Optimune after a 3-month delay
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): CAU + immediate access to Optimune (CBT-based Internet intervention) for 12 months. Arm 2 (Control): CAU only for the first 3 months, then access to Optimune after the 3-month delay (waitlist-controlled design).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: quality of life (QoL) assessed with WHOQOL-BREF, physical activity assessed with IPAQ-SF (International Physical Activity Questionnaire – Short Form), and dietary habits – all measured at 3 months. Secondary outcomes/measures included: fatigue (Brief Fatigue Inventory), depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), fear of progression (Herschbach et al. instrument), insomnia (Insomnia Severity Index), post-traumatic stress (Impact of Event Scale – Revised), net promoter score, negative effects of psychotherapy (INEP inventory), and immune/inflammatory biomarkers (referenced in background). Adherence and usage logs of the Optimune platform were also tracked.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat (ITT) analyses at 3 months revealed: significant effect on quality of life (d = 0.27, 95% CI: 0.07–0.48); significant effect on dietary habits (d = 0.36, 95% CI: 0.15–0.56); non-significant effect on physical exercise (d = 0.30, 95% CI: 0.10–0.51). The authors concluded that Optimune was effective in improving quality of life and dietary habits in breast cancer survivors.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary outcome (KOOS) was collected at enrollment, after 3 weeks, 1 day pre-surgery, 2 weeks post-surgery, 6 weeks post-surgery, 10 weeks post-surgery, and 14 weeks post-surgery. The study covered both preoperative and postoperative periods.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Arm 1 (Intervention): Standard of care + Use of the Orthopy App; Arm 2 (Control): Standard of care only. Total planned and enrolled: 80 participants.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients received standard of care plus access to the Orthopy smartphone application, which provided individually tailored training content and exercises as an add-on to conventional therapy. Arm 2 (Control): Patients received standard of care only (conventional treatment without the app).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: KOOS (Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score) – collected at enrollment, after 3 weeks, 1 day pre-surgery, 2 weeks post-surgery, 6 weeks post-surgery, 10 weeks post-surgery, and 14 weeks post-surgery. Secondary outcomes: Kinesiophobia (Tampa Scale); subjective pain level (numeric VAS); subjective function (numeric VAS); subjective patient satisfaction (numeric VAS); physical therapy/lymphatic drainage needed (yes/no); ability to work (yes/no); patient-relevant structural and procedural improvements (pSVV). Exploratory medical endpoints: thigh circumference, lower leg circumference, patellar center; angular measurements (range of motion); stability test (anteroposterior stability of the knee joint); coordination, quick strength, and agility (end of study only). Exploratory endpoints on app use (intervention group only) via custom-developed questions.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The clinical trial showed positive outcomes on the KOOS Pain and Symptoms subscales in the interventional group compared to the standard of care group. A statistically significant pain reduction was seen at t1 (p = 0.0005), t2 (p = 0.0068), and t4 (approximately 6 weeks after surgery; p = 0.01). A significant reduction of symptoms was found at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036), and t6 (p = 0.0377). These findings were further supported by significant benefit on the pain NRS at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5, and t6. The KOOS subscales quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009), and Sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The total intervention period is at least 15 weeks, consisting of a preoperative phase of at least 1 week and a postoperative phase of 14 weeks. The primary endpoint (VAS pain change) is assessed at 14 weeks postoperatively (T6). Data collection occurs at T0 (baseline, 7–30 days pre-op), T1 (1 day pre-op), T2 (3 days post-op), T3 (2 weeks post-op), T4 (6 weeks post-op), T5 (10 weeks post-op), and T6 (14 weeks post-op).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (I = 164): Orthopy app (video-based home training and educational content, used 3x/week) in addition to usual care. Control group (C = 164): Usual care alone (routine medical treatment and 6 sessions of physiotherapy).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Postoperative use of the Orthopy smartphone app in addition to standard orthopedic therapy (usual care) in patients with meniscus injuries; app used 3 times per week. Arm 2 (Control): Standard therapy in Germany (regular physical therapy / usual care), essentially 6 sessions of physiotherapy.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: VAS Pain (subjective pain intensity) assessed at T0, T1, T2, T3, T4, T5, T6 via patient questionnaire. Secondary outcomes: Health-related quality of life and functional limitations (WOMET); subjective pain intensity (KOOS subscale pain); subjective perception of symptoms and functional deficits (KOOS subscale symptoms and function); objective assessment of proprioceptive abilities (angle reproduction/position sense test) assessed at on-site visits T4 and T6; range of motion (ROM via goniometer) assessed at T4 and T6. All patient-reported outcomes collected digitally via online questionnaires at T1, T2, T3, T5 and in-person at T0, T4, T6.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The total intervention period is at least 18 weeks, consisting of a preoperative phase of at least 4 weeks and a postoperative phase of 14 weeks. The primary endpoint (KOOS Symptoms score change) is assessed at 14 weeks postoperatively (T6). Data collection occurs at T0 (28–36 days before surgery), T1 (1 day before surgery), T2 (3 days post-op), T3 (2 weeks post-op), T4 (6 weeks post-op), T5 (10 weeks post-op), and T6 (14 weeks post-op).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (n=114): Orthopy app (video-based home training and educational content) in addition to usual care; Control group (n=114): Usual care alone (18 sessions of physical therapy and 6 sessions of manual lymphatic drainage).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients use the Orthopy smartphone app 3 times per week in addition to standard care (usual care) for at least 18 weeks (pre- and postoperative phases). Arm 2 (Control): Patients receive usual care in Germany, consisting of routine medical treatment and physiotherapy prescriptions (18 sessions of physical therapy and 6 sessions of manual lymphatic drainage).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: KOOS subscale – Symptoms and Function (change over 14 weeks postoperatively). Secondary outcomes: KOOS-ACL subscale – Function; VAS Pain (subjective pain intensity); LERAS-13 (everyday functional recovery); KOOS subscale – QoL (knee-related quality of life); Angle Reproduction Test (proprioceptive ability of the knee joint); Range of Motion via goniometer. Assessment time points: T0 (≥28 days before surgery), T1 (1 day before surgery), T2 (3 days post-op, online), T3 (2 weeks post-op, online), T4 (6 weeks post-op, on-site), T5 (10 weeks post-op, online), T6 (14 weeks post-op, on-site). Patient-reported outcomes collected digitally; objective functional endpoints (angle reproduction test, range of motion) collected by study staff at on-site visits (T0, T1, T4, T6). Primary analysis: Intention-to-Treat (ITT) using Mixed Model for Repeated Measures (MMRM). Randomization stratified by study center, gender, and preoperative functional status.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is at least 16 weeks, consisting of a preoperative phase of at least 2 weeks and a postoperative phase of 14 weeks. Data is collected at T0 (baseline), T1 (preoperative, end of preoperative phase), T2 (2 weeks post-op), T3 (6 weeks post-op), T4 (10 weeks post-op), and T5 (end of intervention period, 14 weeks post-op). Inclusion occurs at least 2 weeks before surgery.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: pre- and postoperative use of Orthopy smartphone app in addition to standard orthopedic therapy. Control group: standard therapy in Germany (regular physiotherapy). Planned 200 patients per cohort (400 total); 419 actually enrolled. Two cohorts: ACL rupture (with or without meniscal damage) and meniscal damage alone, each split 1:1 into intervention and control.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention group): Pre- and postoperative use of the Orthopy smartphone app in addition to the standard orthopedic therapy standard for anterior cruciate ligament tears (with or without meniscal damage) or meniscal damage alone. Arm 2 (Control group): Standard therapy in Germany (regular physiotherapy / standard of care), including physiotherapy per therapeutic products catalog, with possible supplementary analgesics, lymphatic drainage, orthoses, and motion splints.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: KOOS (Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score) Subscale Symptoms and functional limitations. Secondary outcomes: a) KOOS Pain subscale; b) Subjective pain level (numerical VAS); c) Subjective function (numerical VAS); d) KOOS Activities of Daily Living subscale; e) KOOS Quality of Life subscale; f) KOOS Sport subscale. Exploratory endpoints (collected by study personnel): thigh circumference, range of motion (extension, neutral position, and flexion via angle measure). All primary and secondary endpoints collected digitally at T0, T1, T2, T3, T4, and T5. Prescribed and utilized standard therapy services also recorded.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: No follow-up beyond 12 weeks is mentioned; assessments occur at 1, 3, 6, and 12 weeks postoperatively.
rows.duration_additional_info: The study duration per participant is approximately 12 weeks, corresponding to the usual follow-up period after ACL surgery. Secondary outcomes are assessed at 1, 3, 6, and 12 weeks postoperatively. Cryotherapy starts on the day of surgery and is applied 5 times within the first 48 hours; shockwave therapy is administered once weekly for 5 weeks starting on postoperative day 2. The Orthopy app is used from the third postoperative week onward.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: 4 groups of 15 participants each: Group 1 (combination rESWT + cryotherapy, n=15), Group 2 (rESWT alone, n=15), Group 3 (cryotherapy alone, n=15), Group 4 (control: standardized physiotherapy only, n=15).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Postoperative application of rESWT and hyperbaric cryotherapy (HCT) combined with standardized physiotherapy. Arm 2: Postoperative application of rESWT alone with standardized physiotherapy. Arm 3: Postoperative application of HCT alone with standardized physiotherapy. Arm 4: Control group receiving standardized physiotherapy only. All arms also use the Orthopy app from week 3 postoperatively.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: IKDC score at 12 weeks postoperatively. Secondary outcomes: Tegner activity scores, Lysholm scores, VAS pain scores, swelling status, and range of motion at 1, 3, 6, and 12 weeks postoperatively; IKDC score at 1, 3, and 6 weeks postoperatively; patient satisfaction; rehabilitation progress documented via the Orthopy mobile application.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: No follow-up beyond the primary endpoint (t6 at 14 weeks postsurgery was the final assessment)
rows.duration_additional_info: The study included a presurgery period of 2-6 weeks and a postsurgery period of 14 weeks. Interim assessments were conducted at t1 (if more than 3 weeks before surgery), t3, t4, and t5 (postsurgery). Clinical assessments were conducted at t2 (presurgery) and t6 (14 weeks postsurgery), which was the primary endpoint.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 40 (app-assisted therapy plus standard of care) and C = 40 (standard of care alone); 1:1 randomization
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Orthopy digital health app combined with standard of care (conventional orthopaedic and physiotherapeutic measures). Arm 2 (Control): Isolated standard of care (conventional orthopaedic and physiotherapeutic measures only).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS) including subscales for Pain, Symptoms, Quality of Life, Activities of Daily Living, and Sport. Secondary outcomes: Subjective pain levels via Numeric Rating Scale (NRS) and function levels via NRS. Assessments collected at baseline (t0), t1 (if >3 weeks before surgery), t2 (presurgery clinical assessment), t3, t4, t5 (postsurgery online questionnaires), and t6 (14 weeks postsurgery clinical assessment).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed statistically significant improvements in KOOS Pain subscale at t1 (p=0.0005), t2 (p=0.0068), and t4 (~6 weeks postsurgery; p=0.01). Significant reduction in KOOS Symptoms at t1 (p=0.0001), t2 (p=0.0036), and t6 (p=0.0377). Pain NRS showed significant benefit at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5, and t6. KOOS subscales Quality of Life (p=0.0066), Activities of Daily Living (p=0.0009), and Sport (p=0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery. Conclusion: Combining the Orthopy app with standard of care significantly improves prehabilitation and rehabilitation outcomes compared to standard of care alone.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoints are assessed directly after the 12-week intervention phase. The study is monocentric, conducted at the Department of Sports Medicine in Tübingen, Germany. Recruitment status is listed as complete with follow-up complete.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 12-week app-guided exercise intervention; Control group: no intervention. Randomized 1:1. Total enrolled: 195.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention group): 12-week app-guided home exercise program including progressive video-supported exercises for mobility, flexibility, strength and balance, with movement quality and quantity controlled by accelerometers providing biofeedback. Arm 2 (Control group): no study intervention for 12 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Knee Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS) Subscale Pain and KOOS Subscale Activities of Daily Living (ADL), assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: health-related quality of life (VR12), fear of movement (kinesiophobia), other KOOS subscales (symptoms, sport/recreation, quality of life), Patient's Global Assessment, leg strength and strength endurance (30-Second Chair Test). During intervention: perceived exertion during exercise, exercise adherence, safety aspects (pain, unexpected events/side effects), usability and patient satisfaction.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes assessed at 3 months (12 weeks). The intervention consisted of 3 exercise sessions per week over 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 98 (re.flex + usual care) and C = 97 (usual care only); primary analysis included 194 participants
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 12-week self-directed app-based and sensor-assisted exercise program (re.flex) with 3 sessions per week in addition to usual care. Control arm: usual care only.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: OA-specific knee pain and physical function measured using the Knee Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS) subscales for pain and activities of daily living (ADL) (0-100 scale) at 3 months. Secondary outcomes: adherence (percentage of scheduled exercise sessions completed) and safety (adverse events). Statistical methods: multiple imputation for missing data, baseline-adjusted ANCOVA, Bonferroni correction (alpha=0.025).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Pain reduction was significantly larger in re.flex than in usual care: adjusted mean difference of 4.8 points (95% CI 0.7–8.9; P=.02; Cohen d=0.35). Improvement in physical function was not statistically significant (β=3.9 points, 95% CI 0.0–7.9; P=.049, did not meet Bonferroni-corrected threshold of .025). Adherence: 77% of scheduled sessions completed (2705/3528). Twelve adverse events were linked to re.flex, none serious. Conclusion: re.flex demonstrated superiority over usual care for pain reduction.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention included a 12-week home exercise program with 3 sessions per week. Outcomes were assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks. No follow-up beyond the 12-week primary endpoint is mentioned.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (IG): n=30 (split into IG A: app-based training, and IG AB: app-based training + supportive knee brace); Control group (CG): n=31
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Randomization was 1:1:2 into three arms: (1) IG A – app-based exercise training only; (2) IG AB – app-based exercise training plus a supportive knee brace; (3) CG – control group receiving no study intervention but allowed usual care. The intervention arms used a 12-week home exercise program (3 sessions/week) guided via app and monitored with 2 accelerometers placed above and below the affected knee.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS) pain subscale. Secondary outcomes: all other KOOS subscales (physical function, symptoms, sport and recreation activities, knee-related quality of life); general health-related quality of life (Veterans RAND 12-item Health Survey [VR-12]); psychological measures (e.g., exercise self-efficacy); performance measures (strength and postural control); adherence monitoring (percentage of scheduled exercise sessions completed); safety monitoring. All outcomes assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: 61 participants were analyzed (IG: n=30; CG: n=31). Statistically significant effects in favor of the IG were found for: pain reduction (P<.001; ES=0.76), improvements in physical function (P<.001; ES=0.64), improvements in symptoms (P=.01; ES=0.53), improvements in sport and recreation activities (P=.02; ES=0.47), improvements in knee-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.76), and improvements in the physical component of general health-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.74). Mean differences ranged from 6.0 to 13.2 points (scale range 0–100). Effect sizes indicated small to medium effects. No significant effects were found for psychological or performance measures. Adherence was 92.5% (899/972 scheduled sessions completed).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes measured at individual starting point (T0) and after 3 months (T1). Recruitment status listed as COMPLETE_FOLLOW_UP_COMPLETE.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Planned N=164; actual total participants=282. Intervention group: Rehappy app users. Control group: Waiting control group (receives no treatment).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients use the Rehappy app in daily living, which provides feedback on physical activity (via activity tracker), individually tailored motivational messages, medical background information, hints on activities of daily living, goal setting, and checklists. Arm 2 (Control): Waiting control group receiving no treatment.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes collected at T0 (individual start) and T1 (after 3 months): post-stroke depression (PHQ-2), activity and participation (IMET), therapy adherence, medication adherence (MMAS-8), health literacy (HLS-EU-Q16), self-efficacy (ASKU), health-related quality of life (SOEP-SF12v2), and rate of recurrent strokes. Additional data collected: physical and mental health, activity and mobility, medication compliance, age, gender, and health behavior.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, 24 weeks post-randomization
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes collected at baseline, 12, and 24 weeks post-randomization. Group 1 receives dCBT-I for 3 months (alongside CAU); Group 2 is a waitlist control receiving dCBT-I after 6 months. The primary endpoint is at 12 weeks; the 24-week assessment serves as a follow-up.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Group 1 (intervention): access to somnio (dCBT-I) plus care-as-usual (CAU); Group 2 (waitlist control): CAU only, with access to somnio after 6 months. 1:1 randomization; total enrolled = 137 (planned = 122).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Receives access to somnio (mementor DE GmbH), a digital health application delivering cognitive-behavioral psychotherapy for insomnia (dCBT-I), in addition to care-as-usual (CAU), for 24 weeks. Arm 2: Waitlist control group that continues CAU only and receives access to somnio after 24 weeks (6 months).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depression severity measured with the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) at 12 weeks post-randomization. Secondary outcomes: Insomnia severity (ISI; 12 & 24 weeks), Daytime sleepiness (ESS; 12 & 24 weeks), Fatigue (FSS; 12 & 24 weeks), Quality of life (WHOQOL-BREF; 12 & 24 weeks), Emotional state (PANAS; 12 & 24 weeks), Sleep continuity (sleep diary; 12 weeks), Self-efficacy (PCS; 12 & 24 weeks), Emotion regulation (RSQ; 12 & 24 weeks). Inclusion screening also used Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) and Insomnia Severity Index (ISI > 10).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, 16 weeks post-randomization (i.e., 8 weeks after the primary endpoint)
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasted 8 weeks. Outcomes were collected at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomization. Both groups wore an activity tracker and used a digital app for 8 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Group 1): digital CBT-I via somnio app (n = 29); Control (Group 2): digital sleep monitoring via malio app/sleep diary only (n = 27)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Group 1 received access to somnio (mementor DE GmbH), a digital health application delivering cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) plus a digital sleep diary, for 8 weeks. Group 2 received access to malio (mementor DE GmbH), a digital health application enabling daily sleep tracking via a digital sleep diary only, for 8 weeks. Both groups also wore an actigraphy/activity tracker for the 8-week intervention period.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomization. Secondary outcomes: Sleep continuity measured via application-integrated sleep diary and actigraphy (continuously for 8 weeks); Dysfunctional Beliefs and Attitudes about Sleep (DBAS) at 8 & 16 weeks; Pre-Sleep Arousal Scale (PSAS) at 8 & 16 weeks; Daytime well-being (WHO-5) at 8 & 16 weeks; Quality of life (WHOQOL-BREF) at 8 & 16 weeks; Symptoms of anxiety and depression (HADS) at 8 & 16 weeks. Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) suicide item used at screening.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: 56 participants (48 females, mean age 45.55 ± 13.70 years) were randomized: dCBT-I (n=29) vs. digital sleep monitoring (n=27). At 8 weeks post-randomization, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions in insomnia severity in the dCBT-I arm relative to digital sleep monitoring (-3.70 points on ISI; p ≤ 0.003). At 16 weeks post-randomization, reductions were -2.97 points on ISI (p ≤ 0.003). Treatment effects in favour of dCBT-I were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables, indicating sleep improved throughout the 8-week intervention period. Linear mixed models were used to assess between-group differences. The study concluded that dCBT-I achieves clinical improvements for patients with insomnia, supporting the specific effects of CBT-I.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months after study inclusion
rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments take place prior to the intervention, at 10 weeks after study inclusion (primary endpoint), and at 6 months after study inclusion (follow-up).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: CBT-I delivered via the 'Somnio' app in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic. Control: Sleep diary in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic. Specific group sizes not reported.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) delivered via the 'Somnio' app as add-on to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic. Arm 2: Filling in a sleep diary in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic (active control).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Posttraumatic symptoms assessed via PCL-5 (Weathers et al., 2013) and PTCI (Foa et al., 1999); sleep quality assessed via sleep diary and PSQI (Buysse et al., 1989). Secondary outcomes: Anxiety (GAD-7, Spitzer et al., 2006), depression (QIDS-SR16, Rush et al., 2003), and quality of life (WHODAS 2.0). All measures collected at baseline (pre-intervention), 10 weeks post-inclusion, and 6 months post-inclusion.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 24 weeks (6 months follow-up)
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention (somnio dCBT-I) consists of 11 modules completed over 8 weeks. Primary outcome is assessed at baseline, 8 weeks (post), and 24 weeks (6-month follow-up). Secondary outcomes are also assessed at baseline, 8 and 24 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Digital CBT-I (dCBT-I) via somnio programme (11 modules over 8 weeks); Waitlist control group. Total enrolled: 207 (planned: 204).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Digital cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) using the somnio therapy programme, consisting of 11 modules (approx. 20 minutes each on average, approx. 3.5 hours total) completed over 8 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Waitlist control group – no digital therapy but can continue all usual therapy measures.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at baseline, 8 weeks, and 24 weeks. Secondary outcomes (all at baseline, 8 and 24 weeks unless noted): Pain symptoms (German Pain Questionnaire [DSF], Multidimensional Pain Inventory [MPI]); Daytime sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale [ESS]); Fatigue (Fatigue Severity Scale [FSS]); Dysfunctional attitudes to sleep (Dysfunctional Beliefs and Attitudes about Sleep [DBAS]); Well-being (WHO-5 Well-Being Index); Quality of life (WHOQOL-BREF); Psychological need satisfaction (Basic Psychological Need Satisfaction and Frustration Scale [BPNSFS]); Dreams and nightmares (Mannheim Dream Questionnaire [MADRE], Nightmare Distress Questionnaire [NDQ]); Depression and anxiety (General Depression Scale [ADS-K], State-Trait Anxiety Inventory [STAI]); Motivational schemas (FAMOS at baseline only; Incongruence Questionnaire [K-INK] at baseline, 8 and 24 weeks).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months and 12 months post-treatment (intervention group only)
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention consists of 11 modules completed over 6-8 weeks. The primary endpoint is assessed at 8 weeks post-randomization. Follow-ups at 6 and 12 months were conducted for the intervention group only.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (dCBT-I + care-as-usual): n = 118; Waitlist control group (+ care-as-usual): n = 120
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Unguided digital cognitive-behavior therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) via somnio (www.somn.io), consisting of 11 modules (~20 minutes each, ~3.5 hours total) completed over 6-8 weeks, plus care-as-usual. Arm 2 (Control): Waiting list control group plus care-as-usual.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: ISI (Insomnia Severity Index) assessed at pre, post (8 weeks), follow-up 6 months, follow-up 12 months. Secondary outcomes: DBAS-16 (dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep); FSS (Fatigue Severity Scale); ESS (Epworth Sleepiness Scale); CES-D/ADS-K (depression scale, short version); STAI-T (State-Trait Anxiety Inventory, trait version); WHOQOL-BREF (WHO Quality of Life Questionnaire, brief version); WHO-5 (WHO-Five Well-Being Index); MADRE (Mannheim Dream Questionnaire); DTI (Düsseldorf Dream Inventory); NDQ (Nightmare Distress Questionnaire); CAN (Cognitive Appraisal of Nightmares); nightmare frequency. All secondary outcomes assessed at pre, post, follow-up 6 months, and follow-up 12 months.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: N = 238 participants (67.6% female, age range 19–81 years); n = 118 randomized to dCBT-I, n = 120 to waitlist control. At post-treatment (8 weeks), dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in ISI (Diffadj = –7.60) compared to waitlist control (effect size d = –2.08). Clinical improvement was reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were also observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, depression, and anxiety symptoms (ds = 0.26–1.02). Long-term follow-up effects (intervention group only) were sustained (ds = 0.18–1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency. Conclusion: dCBT-I reduces insomnia symptoms and improves daytime functioning in a heterogeneous insomnia population in Germany with sustained long-term effects.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 24 weeks post randomization
rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments took place at baseline, 12-weeks, and 24-weeks post randomization. The primary endpoint was at 12 weeks, with a follow-up assessment at 24 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I (dCBT-I) = 70 and C (WLC, waiting list control) = 70
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: fully automated digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) added to care-as-usual (CAU), delivered via a mobile app or web platform consisting of 10 sequential core modules based on evidence-based CBT-I techniques. Control arm: waiting list control (WLC), receiving care-as-usual only.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: severity of depressive symptoms measured by the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) at baseline, 12 weeks, and 24 weeks. Secondary outcomes: insomnia severity, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, well-being, and mechanistic effect measures, assessed at baseline, 12 weeks, and 24 weeks. Linear mixed models were used to determine between-group effects.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: A total of 140 participants were randomized (dCBT-I n=70, WLC n=70; 120 women, mean age 39.76 ± 11.65 years). Large treatment effects at 12 and 24 weeks showed significant reductions in depressive symptoms (PHQ-9) in the dCBT-I group compared to WLC (-3.34 and -2.83 respectively; ps <0.001; Cohen's d = 0.66–0.78). Treatment effects in favor of dCBT-I were also found for insomnia severity (ds = 1.46–1.94) and most secondary outcomes (ds = 0.33–1.14). Effects remained stable across the heterogeneous sample.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 24 weeks post-randomization
rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments took place at baseline, 8 weeks, and 24 weeks post-randomization. The primary endpoint was at 8 weeks, with a follow-up assessment at 24 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 103 (dCBT-I) and C = 104 (waitlist control)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) added to regular chronic pain treatment. Arm 2: Waitlist control (WLC) condition receiving regular treatment without dCBT-I. Randomization was 1:1.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia severity measured by the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at baseline, 8 weeks, and 24 weeks post-randomization. Secondary outcomes: sleep-related symptoms, pain-related symptoms (including pain-related impairments and life control), measures of well-being, dreams, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (DBAS). Linear mixed models were used to determine between-group differences.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: 207 participants were randomized (dCBT-I n=103, WLC n=104; 182 women, mean age 51.96 ± 12.97 years). The dCBT-I group showed large improvements in insomnia severity compared to WLC at 8 weeks (difference = -4.36, p < 0.001, d = -1.18) and 24 weeks (difference = -4.88, p < 0.001, d = -1.32). Pain-related impairments and life control also improved favoring dCBT-I (effect sizes ds = 0.22–0.35). Moderate-to-large treatment effects were observed for daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (ds = 0.47–1.12). The study concluded that dCBT-I effectively reduces insomnia severity and improves chronic pain symptoms in individuals with comorbid chronic pain and insomnia.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 16 weeks post-randomisation (i.e., 8 weeks after the primary endpoint)
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomisation. The intervention itself lasted 8 weeks, with a follow-up assessment at 16 weeks post-randomisation.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I (digital CBT) = 29 and C (digital sleep monitoring) = 27
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: 8 weeks of digital cognitive behavioural therapy (dCBT) for insomnia. Arm 2: 8 weeks of digital sleep monitoring (control application).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia severity (assessed at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomisation). Secondary outcomes: Sleep assessed via application-integrated sleep diaries and actigraphy (sleep continuity variables, both self-reported and actigraphy-derived). Linear mixed models used for between-group differences.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions in insomnia severity in the dCBT arm relative to digital sleep monitoring (reductions of -3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003). Treatment effects in favour of dCBT were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables, indicating sleep improved throughout the 8-week intervention period.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The study ran for 12 weeks with measurements collected at baseline and after 12 weeks. Patients in the intervention group exercised with the ViViRA app at least 3 times per week for 20 minutes over 12 weeks. The control group attended physiotherapy at least once a week.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Group 1): received a prescription for the ViViRA digital health app and exercised for 12 weeks (3x/week, 20 min), plus an activity sensor wristband. Control group (Group 2): underwent physiotherapy at least once a week as standard of care. Total enrolled: 70 (planned: 75).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Group 1 (Intervention): Patients with axial spondyloarthritis or axial psoriatic arthritis (per ACR/ASAS criteria) received the ViViRA digital health app as a prescription, exercising 3x/week for 20 minutes over 12 weeks, plus an activity sensor wristband. Group 2 (Control): Patients with the same diagnoses underwent standard physiotherapy at least once a week. Both groups had clinical measurements collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: pain reduction, change in range of motion (BASMI score), change in gait pattern, change in clinical parameters. Secondary outcomes: differences in gait pattern and disease activity between groups; influence of ViViRA on movement competence, sleep quality, hand strength, work productivity, pain, fear of movement, and quality of life. Instruments/measures collected at baseline and after 12 weeks: BASMI (mobility), BASFI (function), BASDAI, ASDAS, ASAS 20 (disease activity); BSA, WPAI, PSQI, ASqL, SF-36, HAQ, PAIN, TSK, IPAQ, PAHCO; gait and movement analysis with sensors; physical activity evaluation via activity sensor (intervention group); clinical routine blood tests (CRP, blood sedimentation, biochemical markers, bone resorption markers: CTX, osteocalcin, BAP); hand strength measurement.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Assessments also at 2 and 6 weeks (intermediate timepoints)
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome (pain reduction via VNRS) measured at 2, 6, and 12 weeks. Trial ran from November 2020 to March 2021.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Arm 1 (Vivira app-based personalized exercise therapy) vs Arm 2 (conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists per German statutory health insurance agreements); total N=213
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Personalized exercise therapy with the Vivira app — digitally adapted guideline-based exercises and patient education. Arm 2: Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists according to agreements with the German statutory health insurance system.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain reduction measured by VNRS (Verbal Numeric Rating Scale) at 2, 6, and 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Function improvement of mobility.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Results published in a peer-reviewed journal (J Med Internet Res. 2022 Oct 28;24(10):e41899). Specific effect sizes, p-values, and statistical results are not detailed in the registry record.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at baseline and after 12 weeks as the primary endpoint timepoint.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received ViViRA DHA (digital health application); control group received standard physiotherapy. Total analyzed: 59 participants.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): SpAs patients received the ViViRA digital health application for home-based physiotherapy. Arm 2 (Control): SpAs patients received standard physiotherapy.
rows.collected_data: Pain assessed via verbal rating scale (VRS) and PAIN-Detect questionnaire; quality of life assessed via SF-36; spinal mobility assessed via BASMI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index). All measures collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Data from 59 participants (71.2% female, mean age 45.2 years) were analyzed. The intervention group showed a significant improvement in mobility (average BASMI score: baseline 1.1 [range 0.7–1.5]; follow-up 1.0 [range 0.5–1.4]; p = 0.05), whereas the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline 1.5 [range 1.1–1.9]; follow-up 1.8 [range 1.4–2.2]; p = 0.00). The intervention group demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level 3.5 ± 2.8) than the control group (VRS pain level 4.5 ± 2.0) after 12 weeks. The authors concluded that DHA use resulted in superior outcomes compared with standard physiotherapy for lower back pain in SpAs patients.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Assessments at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months (primary endpoint at 6 months, with interim assessment at 3 months)
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were instructed to exercise at least 2-3 times per week for 6 months. Assessments occurred at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Three groups: ViViRA with personal coaching (intervention), Kaia Health with AI-based coaching (intervention), ViViRA without coaching (control). Total N=78.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: ViViRA DHA with personal coaching (one-time 30-min online coaching session prior to DHA use); Arm 2: Kaia Health DHA with AI-based coaching (video-based AI integrated into DHA providing movement guidance during each session); Arm 3: ViViRA DHA without coaching (control group). All groups instructed to exercise at least 2-3 times per week for 6 months.
rows.collected_data: Sociodemographic data; Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index (BASMI, range 0-10, lower = better mobility); PainDETECT (neuropathic pain, range 0-38, higher = more severe pain); PAHCO (Physical Activity-related Health Competence, higher = better competence); mobility assessments; adherence (weekly use of DHA). Collected at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: All three digital interventions showed significant improvement in mobility (BASMI): baseline to 3 months mean change -0.6 to -0.7, all p < 0.001. Pain intensity (PainDETECT) decreased substantially in all arms: baseline to 6 months mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6 points, all p ≤ 0.006. PAHCO control competence increased over time and reached statistical significance only in the ViViRA + coaching group (baseline to 6 months: +1.02, p = 0.013), but did not exceed other interventions in direct comparison. None of the coaching strategies showed significant superiority over stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same in all groups after 3 months (2-3 weekly use of DHA).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary outcome was assessed at 12 weeks, corresponding to the full duration of the digital home exercise program. No follow-up beyond 12 weeks is mentioned.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (digital home exercise program) = 108; Control (physiotherapy standard of care) = 105
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 12-week stand-alone app-based digital home exercise program with 4 exercises daily. Control arm: standard physiotherapy consisting of 6 to 12 sessions depending on symptom severity.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-reported pain intensity measured using a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS) at baseline and 12 weeks. Clinical relevance thresholds: improvement of at least 1.4 points on the VNRS and a pain reduction of at least 30%. Retention rates were also tracked.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Mean difference in pain scores between groups at 12 weeks: -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group. Intervention group achieved a clinically relevant pain reduction: mean change of -3.35 (SD 2.05) points or -53.1% (SD 29.5) from baseline. Control group did not reach clinical relevance: mean change of -0.91 (SD 1.5) or -14.6% (SD 25.3). Retention rates: 89.9% in the intervention group and 97.3% in the control group. The app-based home exercise program led to a significant and clinically relevant reduction in pain intensity superior to standard physiotherapy.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Retrospective, intraindividually controlled study. The study is single-arm with intra-individual control (baseline/historical findings of the same patient serve as control). Data collection period: June 1–15, 2020. Patients included from January 9, 2018 onward.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single arm (N=179); intra-individual control design where each patient's baseline (historical) findings serve as the control. Intervention: use of the Vivira digital home exercise app.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm study. All participants used the Vivira app (CE-certified digital therapeutic exercise program for home use on smartphones/tablets). Control is the intra-individual baseline (pre-treatment) pain score of the same patient.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain assessed using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS/VNRS) after 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Proprietary functional test scores. Additional descriptive variables collected include measures of location (means, medians), dispersion (standard deviations), changes from baseline, 95% confidence intervals for changes from baseline, Cohen's effect sizes, and absolute/relative frequencies for qualitative variables.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Retrospective, intraindividual controlled study. The study is single-armed with the control being the pre-finding (historical finding) of the same patient. Data collected from patients using the Vivira app from 01/01/2020 onwards.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single arm: retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app (N=157 actual participants; 150 planned). Intraindividual control: the control is the pre-finding (historical baseline) of the same patient.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app (CE-marked digital therapeutic exercise program for home use on smartphones/tablets). The study is single-armed; the control is the intraindividual pre-finding (historical baseline) of the same patient.
