rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: VNRS (verbal-numerical rating scale) of pain after 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Proprietary functional test (series of functional scores). Quantitative variables described by measures of location (means, medians) and dispersion (standard deviations); changes from baseline; 95% confidence intervals for changes from baseline; Cohen's effect sizes. Qualitative variables: absolute and relative frequencies.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The study involves a twelve-week individualized home exercise program (digital therapeutic). The study is a single-arm observational study with pre-post comparison. Actual data collection ran from 06 April 2022 to 10 May 2022.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study; no control group. 7842 patients with non-specific and degenerative back, hip, and knee pain using a digital therapeutic (DiGA 'Vivira') consisting of an adaptive home exercise program with individualized exercise progressions.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm: intra-individual pre-post comparison of patients with non-specific and degenerative musculoskeletal conditions undergoing a twelve-week individualized home exercise program (digital therapeutic 'Vivira').
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Self-reported pain intensity assessed with a verbal-numeric rating scale (VNRS, 0-10). Secondary outcomes: (1) Clinical relevance of pain intensity improvement assessed via responder analysis using three criteria established in the literature (criterion met / not met); (2) Physical functional capacity assessed in the dimensions of mobility, strength, and coordination on a discrete scale; (3) Patient characteristics associated with good treatment adherence.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Pain scores were assessed at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks. The total observation period was 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm observational study; 3629 patients using the Vivira digital home exercise program (no separate control group).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: All participants received the smartphone-based home exercise program 'Vivira' (a fully approved DiGA) for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee. There was no comparator arm.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-reported pain intensity measured with a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS) at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: self-reported function scores for back, hip, and knee pain areas, assessed using matched-pair analysis (Wilcoxon signed-rank test). Demographic data including sex and age were also collected.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity were observed after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (Skillings-Mack test: T3628=5308; P<.001), with changes within the range of clinically relevant improvement. Function scores showed a generally positive yet more variable response across the pain areas (back, hip, and knee). The study noted relevant attrition at follow-up.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The study assessed pain score changes over the intended 12-week duration of the program. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single group of 2517 participants using a smartphone-based home exercise program (Vivira) for unspecific and degenerative back, hip, and knee pain; an incomplete matched block design was used for comparison.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm observational study. All participants used the Vivira smartphone-based exercise program for back, hip, and knee pain. An incomplete matched block design and matched comparison were employed for internal analyses rather than a separate control group.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores (0-10 scale) at baseline and 12 weeks; self-reported functional scores including strength, mobility, and coordination scores; adherence rates. An incomplete matched block design was used to assess pain score changes over 12 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: 2517 participants met inclusion criteria. Overall initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19/10 (SD 1.96) to 3.35/10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over early use phases. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores; participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in coordination scores. A high yet expected attrition rate was observed across all pain areas and pain durations.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The study does not specify the duration of the program or when the primary endpoint was assessed.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Participants were grouped by use profiles: high use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), and sub-low use (sub-LU). Specific group sizes are not reported in the abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Four use profile groups were compared: high use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), and sub-low use (sub-LU) of a digital home exercise program for nonspecific back pain (NSBP). Propensity score matching (PSM) was used to comparatively assess outcomes across these groups.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity (clinically relevant pain improvement as primary measure); use/adherence profiles (HU, IU, LU, sub-LU); sensitivity analyses using commonly accepted thresholds for clinically relevant improvements.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles were associated with a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement in self-reported pain intensities. Achieving any higher use level was associated with a statistically significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement. The study concluded that a high use (HU) profile should be pursued to enable optimal effectiveness of DTx home exercise programs.

rows.duration_additional_info: The Caterna visual training games are played for 90 days daily for 30-45 minutes during occlusion. The prospective data analysis will take place over a period of 5 years. Occlusion therapy continues until the worse eye reaches the same visual acuity as the better eye or no further improvement occurs.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single observational group: amblyopic patients receiving Caterna online visual training (app-based therapy) as an addition to standard occlusion therapy, played on a computer for 90 days daily for 30-45 minutes during occlusion.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One observational arm: amblyopia therapy using Caterna online visual training (CE-certified medical device) additive to standard occlusion therapy. No separate control arm described.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: visual acuity of the worse eye (improvement until equal to the better eye or no further improvement); regular check-ups by orthoptists and ophthalmologists. Secondary outcome: optimization of amblyopia therapy through data analysis of a Germany-wide network. Data includes retrospective and prospective treatment course data from practices using Caterna visual training combined with occlusion therapy.

rows.duration_additional_info: Children had stagnating BCVA despite at least 90 days of prior occlusion therapy before being included. The study does not explicitly state the duration of the FAVAS intervention or the time to primary endpoint assessment.
rows.study_size_participants: 227 children analyzed initially; after propensity score matching, 105 patients (114 eyes; 57 eyes per group) remained for analysis.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: FAVAS group (57 eyes): occlusion therapy plus focal ambient visual acuity stimulation (FAVAS, Caterna Vision GmbH); Control group (57 eyes): occlusion therapy alone.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (FAVAS group): amblyopic children received FAVAS integrated into a computer game alongside occlusion therapy. Arm 2 (Control group): amblyopic children received occlusion therapy alone. Post hoc propensity score matching was applied to balance the groups.
rows.collected_data: Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA, logMAR) as primary endpoint; stereo vision as secondary endpoint. Clinical records from a retrospective multicentre study were analyzed.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: FAVAS group: significant improvement in BCVA with a mean gain of 0.15 logMAR (95% CI [0.11; 0.18], p < 0.001). Control group: non-significant improvement of 0.01 logMAR (95% CI [-0.02; 0.04], p = 0.356). No significant changes in stereo vision were observed in either group. The combination of occlusion therapy and FAVAS was superior to occlusion alone in amblyopic children with stagnating BCVA.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed during a single dental appointment. Heart rate was measured at baseline (patient sitting in chair prior to treatment), during sealant placement with VR, during sealant placement with basic behavior guidance techniques, and post-operatively (5 minutes after procedure). The VR system was worn for approximately 5-10 minutes during dental sealant application. This was a split-mouth crossover design conducted within a single visit.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Crossover design with 4 sequence groups: VR with right side first then BBG with left side (n=4); VR with left side first then BBG with right side (n=4); BBG with right side first then VR with left side (n=8); BBG with left side first then VR with right side (n=4). Each participant received both VR and BBG (split-mouth design).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Virtual Reality (VR) using the RelievRx by AppliedVR goggle headset, worn for approximately 5-10 minutes during dental sealant application, providing an immersive game experience with bright colors and cartoon-like characters. Arm 2 (Active Comparator): Basic Behavior Guidance Techniques (BBG), the standard of care, including tell-show-do, ask-tell-ask, memory restructuring, positive pre-visit imagery, and distraction. Each participant received both interventions on opposite sides of the mouth in a crossover/split-mouth design.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: (1) Change in heart rate (beats per minute) from baseline to during sealant placement with VR; (2) Change in heart rate (beats per minute) from baseline to during sealant placement with BBG. Additional measures: Frankl behavior score (1-5) assigned by provider at end of each appointment; Healthcare Provider Questionnaire (via Qualtrics); parent-reported weekly screen time hours; parent and patient satisfaction survey post-treatment; demographic factors from medical chart. Heart rate measured at: baseline (patient sitting in chair prior to treatment), during sealant placement with VR, during sealant placement with BBG, and post-operatively (5 minutes after procedure).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: This was a pilot study; only means and ranges were reported (no formal statistical analysis). Baseline mean heart rate across all 20 participants: 82.575 bpm (range 61-108). Change in heart rate (beats per minute) with VR: Group VR-right-first: mean -2 (range -9 to 2); Group VR-left-first: mean -6.25 (range -9 to -4); Group BBG-right-first then VR-left: mean -8.8 (range -18 to 4); Group BBG-left-first then VR-right: mean -6.25 (range -12 to -3). Change in heart rate with BBG: Group VR-right-first: mean -2 (range -13 to 8); Group VR-left-first: mean -2.75 (range -10 to 1); Group BBG-right-first: mean -3 (range -14 to 15); Group BBG-left-first: mean -0.5 (range -16 to 7). Heart rate tended to decrease from baseline in both VR and BBG conditions across all groups.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; study completion date was 12 months after primary completion (August 2023 vs August 2022), suggesting follow-up beyond the 90-day primary endpoint period.
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoints assessed at 90 days post-surgery. Adverse events monitored for 4 months per patient. Primary completion date was August 2022; overall study completion date was August 2023.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Standard of Care (control) group: n=32; Standard of Care + RelieVRx (intervention) group: n=31
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Active Comparator): Standard of care group – received multi-modality pain management (local anesthesia, ibuprofen, acetaminophen, gabapentin, ketamine, lidocaine, and/or opioids) without RelieVRx. Arm 2 (Experimental): Standard of care + RelieVRx group – received the same multi-modality pain management plus the RelieVRx virtual reality headset as adjunctive treatment for acute surgical pain.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: (1) Acute Postoperative Pain measured via Visual Analog Pain Scale (0–10) at 90 days; (2) Opioid Consumption measured in Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME) over 90 days (pre-op to post-op change). Secondary outcomes: (1) KOOS Jr. Score (Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score short form; 0–100) at 90 days; (2) Veterans RAND 12 Health Survey (VR-12) Physical Component Score (T-score metric, mean 50, SD 10) at 90 days. Baseline: Numerical Pain Rating Scale (0–10). Adverse events tracked over 4 months per patient.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Acute Postoperative Pain (Visual Analog Scale, 0–10): SOC group mean 5.13 (SD 1.79) vs SOC+RelieVRx group mean 4.42 (SD 1.17) over 90 days. Opioid Consumption (median change in MME from pre-op to post-op, n=24 SOC and n=18 RelieVRx completers): SOC group median −28.0 MME (IQR −35.0 to −23.5); SOC+RelieVRx group median −34.8 MME (IQR −37.0 to −23.5). KOOS Jr. Score (median change pre- to post-surgery): SOC group 27.6 (IQR 17.5–38.0); SOC+RelieVRx group 32.2 (IQR 21.2–42.0). VR-12 Physical Component Score (median change): SOC group 7.2 (IQR 2.0–11.4); SOC+RelieVRx group 9.5 (IQR 4.0–12.8). No deaths or serious adverse events were reported in either group.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed at day 56 (end of 8-week treatment). Data were collected from January 31, 2022, to October 31, 2022.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I (RelieVRx) = 536 and C (Sham) = 531
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received RelieVRx, an 8-week self-administered in-home behavioral skills virtual reality program training diaphragmatic breathing, biofeedback, cognition and emotion regulation, mindfulness, and pain education. Control arm received an active Sham VR program. Randomization was 1:1.
rows.collected_data: Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), pain intensity (0-10 scale), pain interference (0-10 scale), assessed from pretreatment to day 56 (end of treatment). Participants had self-reported nonmalignant chronic low back pain ≥3 months duration with average pain intensity and pain interference ≥4/10.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: RelieVRx was superior to Sham for pain intensity reduction (difference from Sham: 0.406 [95% CI: 0.170–0.642]) and pain interference reduction (difference from Sham: 0.523 [95% CI: 0.285–0.760]) from pretreatment to day 56. Clinically meaningful reductions were observed for RelieVRx: pain intensity reduced by 2.0 points out of 10 [1.73–2.06] and pain interference reduced by 2.3 points [1.99–2.33]. The 8-week self-administered behavioral skills VR program imparted clinically meaningful improvements above the active control on both pain intensity and pain interference in clinically severe and diverse adults with chronic low back pain.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 12 months posttreatment
rows.duration_additional_info: The study involved a 56-session self-administered in-home VR program. Data were collected from January 31, 2022 to October 31, 2023. The primary analyses compared pretreatment to 12 months posttreatment outcomes.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Participants randomized to Skills-Based VR (RelieVRx) or active Sham; total N=1,093 (individual group sizes not reported in abstract)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Skills-Based VR (RelieVRx) – a 56-session, self-administered in-home virtual reality program incorporating pain education and cognitive behavioral therapy. Arm 2: Active Sham – a sham/control VR condition.
rows.collected_data: Average pain intensity (0-10 scale), pain interference (0-10 scale), responder rates (≥2-point reduction in pain intensity, pain interference, or both) assessed at baseline and 12 months posttreatment. Participants had self-reported nonmalignant chronic low back pain >3 months duration with average pain intensity and interference scores >4/10.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: From baseline to 12 months posttreatment, Skills-Based VR showed reductions in average pain intensity (1.7 ± 2.1) and pain interference (1.9 ± 2.3) that were robust and significantly greater than those found for Sham. More than half of Skills-Based VR participants reported at least a 2-point reduction in pain intensity, pain interference, or both at 12 months posttreatment.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months post-intervention
rows.duration_additional_info: The 9-week intervention period is the primary endpoint; a second primary endpoint assessment occurs at 6 months post-intervention. The overall study completion date was July 15, 2024. Adverse events were monitored up to 6 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I (Culturally adapted digital CBT-I / Somryst) = 3 and C (Minimally Enhanced Usual Care / mEUC) = 4
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Culturally adapted digital CBT-I program (Somryst) – FDA-authorized, translated and culturally adapted for Spanish speakers, self-guided mobile-based program with six cores over 9 weeks covering behavioral (sleep restriction, stimulus control), cognitive (cognitive restructuring), educational (sleep hygiene), overview, and consolidation/relapse prevention domains, plus usual primary care and a sleep hygiene brochure. Arm 2 (Active Comparator): Minimally Enhanced Usual Care (mEUC) – continued standard care with primary care provider (which may include pharmacotherapy) plus a sleep hygiene brochure.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Change in mean Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) score (7-item self-report, range 0-28) from baseline to post-intervention (9 weeks) and from baseline to 6 months post-intervention. Secondary outcomes: Change in mean SF-12 Mental Component Score (MCS) quality of life (12-item, 0-100 scale) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS) daytime sleepiness (8-item, range 0-24) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean Patient Assessment of Chronic Illness Care (PACIC) satisfaction with care (20-item, range 20-100) from baseline to 9 weeks. Other pre-specified outcomes: Change in mean Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) sleep quality (18-item, range 0-21) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean self-reported wake after sleep onset (14-day sleep diary, minutes) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean actigraphy-assessed wake after sleep onset (14-day wrist actigraphy, minutes) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean self-reported sleep onset latency (14-day sleep diary, minutes) from baseline to 9 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Only 7 participants were enrolled (3 in CBT-I arm, 4 in mEUC arm); all completed the study. Primary outcome (ISI, baseline to 9 weeks): CBT-I group mean change = -6.7 (95% CI: -15.4 to 2.0); mEUC group mean change = -3.9 (95% CI: -12.5 to 4.7). Primary outcome (ISI, baseline to 6 months post-intervention): CBT-I group mean change = -5.7 (95% CI: -17.1 to 5.8); mEUC group mean change = -1.6 (95% CI: -13.0 to 9.9). Secondary outcome (SF-12 MCS, 9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = +10.8 (95% CI: 5.9 to 15.7); mEUC mean change = +0.8 (95% CI: -4.0 to 5.6). Secondary outcome (ESS, 9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = -2.3 (95% CI: -6.5 to 1.9); mEUC mean change = +0.5 (95% CI: -3.6 to 4.6). Secondary outcome (PACIC, 9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = +17.4 (95% CI: -20.5 to 55.4); mEUC mean change = -8.2 (95% CI: -41.9 to 25.6). Other outcomes (PSQI, 9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = -1.0 (95% CI: -2.6 to 0.6); mEUC mean change = -0.7 (95% CI: -2.2 to 0.8). Actigraphy-assessed WASO (9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = -2.5 min (95% CI: -38.5 to 33.6); mEUC mean change = -4.8 min (95% CI: -10.2 to 0.6). Self-reported sleep onset latency (9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = -53.3 min (95% CI: -128.6 to 21.9); mEUC mean change = -16.9 min (95% CI: -84.7 to 51.0). Self-reported WASO data were not collected and could not be analyzed. No adverse events (deaths, serious, or other) were reported in either arm. Note: The very small sample size (n=7 total) severely limits the interpretability of these results.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; secondary outcomes and follow-up are assessed beyond the 9-week primary endpoint (specific duration not stated beyond 9 weeks)
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at 9 weeks post-randomisation. The trial is prospective and multicentre. No explicit total study duration beyond the 9-week primary endpoint is stated in the protocol.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: I = 50 (PDT/Somryst for mobile-delivered CBT-I) and C = 50 (no PDT); randomised 1:1
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Participants receive the prescription digital therapeutic (PDT) PEAR-003b (Somryst), an FDA-authorised mobile app delivering CBT-I, plus a linked Fitbit Inspire 2. Arm 2 (Control): Participants do not receive the PDT but are enrolled on the Hugo Health platform and provided with a linked Fitbit Inspire 2.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Change in Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) score from baseline to 9-week post-randomisation. Secondary outcomes: Healthcare utilisation; health utility scores; clinical outcomes; change in sleep outcomes measured via sleep diaries; depressive symptoms; daytime sleepiness; health status; stress; anxiety; engagement with the PDT (for intervention arm only). Data collected via the Hugo Health patient-centred health data-aggregating platform and Fitbit Inspire 2 activity tracker.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; at 6, 12, and 18 months after posttest
rows.duration_additional_info: Posttest (primary endpoint) at 9 weeks; follow-ups at 6-, 12-, and 18-month intervals after baseline.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: SHUTi (digital CBT-I); Control: HealthWatch (attention-matched control internet program); total N = 1149
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: SHUTi – a self-guided, digital Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) program. Arm 2: HealthWatch – an attention-matched control internet program.
rows.collected_data: Online sleep diaries used to derive: sleep-onset latency (SOL), wake after sleep onset (WASO), sleep efficiency (SE), number of awakenings, sleep quality, and total sleep time (TST). Assessments conducted at baseline, posttest (9 weeks), and at 6-, 12-, and 18-month follow-ups. Also measured: insomnia severity, depression symptoms, anxiety symptoms, and suicidal ideation (referenced from prior RCTs context).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the SHUTi condition had greater improvements at posttest (9 weeks) compared with control for SOL, WASO, SE, number of awakenings, and sleep quality. These improvements were sustained at every follow-up (p < .02 for all outcomes except TST). Statistically significant increases in TST were observed only at 12- and 18-month follow-ups. Digitally delivered CBT-I produced lasting improvements in sleep outcomes among adults with insomnia and depressive symptoms.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: Retrospective pharmacovigilance study of the MAUDE database through April 30, 2025. No specific primary endpoint duration or follow-up period defined; the study reviewed postmarket adverse event reports retrospectively.
rows.collected_data: MAUDE database adverse event reports for 13 FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutics (PDTs); data extracted on event type, patient demographics, outcomes, and product details. Device-specific product codes used to identify reports related to each PDT.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Four reports were initially identified; two were excluded as they involved non-cleared or over-the-counter digital products. Of the remaining two: one involved Somryst (for insomnia) prescribed to a patient with a seizure disorder (a known contraindication), resulting in disability; the other involved EndeavorRx, reporting perceived ineffectiveness without clinical harm. No adverse events were identified for the remaining 11 PDTs. The authors concluded that while findings may suggest a favorable safety profile, results must be interpreted cautiously given limitations of passive surveillance systems including underreporting, reporting bias, and limited capacity to capture software-specific harms.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; 6-month and 1-year follow-ups after the 9-week intervention period
rows.duration_additional_info: The 9-week intervention period involved 6 interactive treatment cores. Assessments were completed at baseline, before completing treatment cores, immediately post-intervention, at 6 months, and at 1 year.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study; 1565 adults accessed the PDT (Somryst®). No control group. 908 (58.0%) were considered adherent and 733 (46.8%) completed all 6 cores.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm: all participants received the FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutic (PDT; Somryst®) delivering cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) via six interactive treatment cores and daily sleep diaries over a 9-week intervention period.
rows.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI), 8-item Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-8), Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 scale (GAD-7), adherence/completion rates (Core 4 completion as exposure to all mechanisms of action, completion of all 6 cores). Assessments collected at baseline, before completing each treatment core, immediately post-intervention, 6-month follow-up, and 1-year follow-up.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: For participants who completed assessments for all 6 cores (48.4%), ISI decreased from 18.8 at baseline to a mean of 9.9 (P<.001). ISI scores remained significantly lower than baseline at immediate post-intervention (11.0), 6-month (11.6), and 1-year (12.2) follow-ups (all P<.001). PHQ-8 and GAD-7 also showed significant decreases at all measured timepoints from baseline (P<.001). 58% of those who began the program completed Core 4 (adherent); 46.8% completed all 6 cores.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 2 years
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary clinical endpoint (ISI score change) assessed at 9 weeks; long-term HCRU analysis compared 2 years pre-index to 2 years post-index. Index date defined as successful initiation of the therapeutic between June 1, 2016 and December 31, 2018.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single cohort of 248 patients treated with Somryst (digital CBT-I therapeutic); pre/post design with no separate control group (2-year pre-index period served as comparator for HCRU outcomes).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm real-world study. All participants received Somryst (formerly SHUTi), a digital therapeutic delivering cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I). A pre/post analysis was used for HCRU comparisons.
rows.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) score (baseline and last observed score); ISI responder status (improvement ≥7 points); insomnia remission (ISI score <8); healthcare resource utilization (HCRU) including emergency department visits, hospitalizations, hospital outpatient visits, ambulatory surgical center visits, and office visits (2 years pre- and post-index); sleep aid medication use (proportion of patients and number of prescriptions pre- and post-index); total healthcare costs (2 years pre- and post-index).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: After 9 weeks, mean ISI score declined 37.2% from baseline (19.1 vs 12.0). 58.8% of patients achieved ISI responder status (NNT: 1.7); 26.6% achieved insomnia remission (NNT: 3.8). Over 2-year follow-up, ED visits reduced by 53% (IRR: 0.47; 95% CI 0.27–0.82; P=0.008); hospitalizations reduced by 21% (IRR: 0.79; 95% CI 0.46–1.35; P=0.389); hospital outpatient visits reduced by 13% (IRR: 0.87; 95% CI 0.66–1.14; P=0.315). Ambulatory surgical center visits increased 2% (IRR: 1.02; P=0.903) and office visits increased 2% (IRR: 1.02; P=0.672). Sleep aid medication use dropped from 52.4% to 42.7% (-18.5%). Average prescriptions decreased from 3.98 to 3.73 (P=0.552). Total 2-year cost reduction was $510,678 (-$2,059 per patient).

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: The program had an average duration of 10 weeks; the primary outcome was assessed from baseline to program-end.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study: 326 participants started the program; 264 (81.0%) completed the intervention. No control group.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm: all participants received a remote digital care program (DCP) combining education and pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) with real-time biofeedback, managed asynchronously by a physical therapist.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Urinary Impact Questionnaire-short form (UIQ-7) change from baseline to program-end, analyzed via latent growth curve analysis (LGCA). Secondary outcomes: Pelvic Floor Impact Questionnaire (PFIQ-7) for impact of pelvic conditions; Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depression; Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-7) for anxiety; Work Productivity and Activity Impairment (WPAI) questionnaire; intention to seek additional healthcare; engagement metrics; and participant satisfaction.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Of 326 participants who started the program, 264 (81.0%) completed the intervention. Significant improvement on UIQ-7 was observed (mean change 8.8, 95% CI 4.7–12.9, p < 0.001), corresponding to a response rate of 57.3%. Improvements were also observed in all secondary outcomes. High satisfaction was reported (mean 8.9/10, SD 1.8). The study concluded that the remote DCP with biofeedback is feasible and safe for reducing UI-related symptoms in a real-world setting.

rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention group uses the ViViRA Neck app for 12 weeks; the control group receives at least 6 sessions of individual physiotherapy over 12 weeks. No follow-up beyond 12 weeks is mentioned.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Smartphone app ViViRA Neck with access for 12 weeks; Control group: Standard of care, i.e., at least 1 prescription with 6 sessions of individual physical therapy over 12 weeks. Planned total: 214 participants.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Use of the CE-marked app 'ViViRA Neck' for 12 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Standard of care physiotherapy — at least 6 sessions of individual physical therapy over 12 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain intensity measured by Visual Analogue Scale (VAS, 0-100) at 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: Disability measured by Neck Disability Index (NDI) at 12 weeks; Health-related quality of life measured by Veterans Rand 12-item Health Survey (VR12) at 12 weeks.

rows.duration_additional_info: The study ran for 12 weeks. Patients in the intervention group exercised with the ViViRA app at least 3 times per week for 20 minutes. Measurements were collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: patients with axial spondyloarthritis or axial psoriatic arthritis who received a prescription for the ViViRA digital health app and exercised for 12 weeks (at least 3x/week, 20 min/session), plus an activity sensor wristband. Control group: patients with the same diagnosis who underwent physiotherapy at least once a week as standard of care. Total enrolled: 70 (planned: 75). Exact per-group sizes not specified.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients received a prescription for the ViViRA digital health app and were instructed to exercise for 12 weeks, optimally 3 times per week for 20 minutes, with back-specific exercises. They also received a wristband activity sensor. Arm 2 (Control): Patients underwent physiotherapy as standard of care at least once a week for 12 weeks. Both groups had clinical measurements collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcomes: pain reduction, change in range of motion (BASMI score), change in gait pattern, change in clinical parameters. Secondary outcomes: differences in gait pattern and disease activity between groups; influence of ViViRA on movement competence, sleep quality, hand strength, work productivity, pain, fear of movement, and quality of life. Instruments/measures collected: BASMI (spinal mobility), BASFI (function), BASDAI, ASDAS, ASAS 20 (disease activity), BSA, WPAI (work productivity), PSQI (sleep quality), ASqL, SF-36 (quality of life), HAQ, PAIN scale, TSK (fear of movement), IPAQ, PAHCO (movement competence), gait and movement analysis with shoe-attached sensors, physical activity via wristband activity sensor, hand strength measurement, clinical routine blood tests (CRP, blood sedimentation), biochemical markers, bone resorption markers (CTX, osteocalcin, BAP), physical examination.

rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome (pain reduction via VNRS) was assessed at 2, 6, and 12 weeks. The trial ran from November 2020 to March 2021.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Arm 1 (Vivira app-based personalized home exercise therapy) vs Arm 2 (Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists per German statutory health insurance agreements); total N=213
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Personalized exercise therapy with the Vivira app — digitally adapted guideline-based exercises and patient education. Arm 2: Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists according to agreements with the German statutory health insurance system.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain reduction measured by VNRS (Verbal Numeric Rating Scale) at 2, 6, and 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Function improvement of mobility.

rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at baseline and after 12 weeks as the primary outcomes.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received ViViRA DHA; control group received standard physiotherapy. Total analyzed: 59 participants.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): SpAs patients received the ViViRA digital health application for home-based physiotherapy. Arm 2 (Control): SpAs patients received standard physiotherapy.
rows.collected_data: Pain assessed via verbal rating scale (VRS) and PAIN-Detect questionnaire; quality of life assessed via SF-36; spinal mobility assessed via BASMI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index). All measures collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Data from 59 participants (71.2% female, mean age 45.2 years) were analyzed. The intervention group showed a significant improvement in mobility (average BASMI score: baseline 1.1 [range 0.7–1.5]; follow-up 1.0 [range 0.5–1.4]; p=0.05), whereas the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline 1.5 [range 1.1–1.9]; follow-up 1.8 [range 1.4–2.2]; p=0.00). The intervention group demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level 3.5 ± 2.8) than the control group (VRS pain level 4.5 ± 2) after 12 weeks. The authors concluded that DHA use resulted in superior outcomes compared with standard physiotherapy for lower back pain in SpAs patients.

rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Assessments at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months (primary endpoint at 6 months, with interim at 3 months)
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants exercised at least 2-3 times per week for 6 months. Assessments were conducted at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Three groups: ViViRA with personal coaching (intervention 1), Kaia Health with AI-based coaching (intervention 2), ViViRA without coaching (control). Total N=78; individual group sizes not specified.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: ViViRA DHA with personalized (human) coaching – one-time 30-min online coaching session prior to DHA use. Arm 2: Kaia Health DHA with AI-based coaching – video-based AI integrated into DHA providing movement guidance during each session. Arm 3 (Control): ViViRA DHA without any coaching. All arms instructed to exercise at least 2-3 times per week for 6 months.
rows.collected_data: Sociodemographic data; Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index (BASMI, range 0-10, lower = better mobility); PainDETECT (neuropathic pain, range 0-38, higher = more severe pain); PAHCO (Physical Activity-related Health Competence, higher = better competence); adherence (weekly use of DHA). Collected at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: All three digital interventions showed significant improvement in mobility (BASMI): baseline to 3 months mean change -0.6 to -0.7, all p < 0.001. Pain intensity (PainDETECT) decreased substantially in all arms: baseline to 6 months mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6 points, all p ≤ 0.006. PAHCO control competence increased over time and reached statistical significance only in the ViViRA + coaching group (baseline to 6 months: +1.02, p = 0.013), but did not exceed other interventions in direct comparison. No coaching strategy showed significant superiority over stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same in all groups after 3 months (2-3 weekly DHA use).

rows.duration_additional_info: The primary outcome was assessed at 12 weeks, corresponding to the duration of the intervention program. No follow-up beyond 12 weeks is mentioned.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (digital home exercise program) = 108; Control (physiotherapy) = 105
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 12-week stand-alone digital home exercise program with 4 exercises daily. Control arm: standard of care physiotherapy consisting of 6 to 12 sessions depending on symptom severity.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-reported pain intensity measured using a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS) at baseline and 12 weeks. Clinical relevance thresholds: improvement of at least 1.4 points on the VNRS and a pain reduction of at least 30%. Retention rates were also tracked.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Mean difference in pain scores between groups at 12 weeks: -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group. Intervention group achieved a clinically relevant pain reduction: mean change of -3.35 (SD 2.05) points or -53.1% (SD 29.5). Control group did not reach clinical relevance: mean change of -0.91 (SD 1.5) or -14.6% (SD 25.3). Retention rates: 89.9% in the intervention group and 97.3% in the control group. The app-based home exercise program led to a significant and clinically relevant reduction in pain intensity superior to standard physiotherapy.

rows.duration_additional_info: Retrospective, intraindividually controlled study. The study is single-arm with intra-individual control (baseline/historical findings of the same patient serve as control). Data collection period: June 1–15, 2020.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single arm of 179 patients using the Vivira app; intra-individual control (each patient's own baseline/historical findings serve as the control).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm study: all participants received the Vivira digital home exercise app. The control is the intra-individual historical (baseline) findings of the same patient.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain assessed using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS/VNRS) after 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Proprietary functional test scores. Descriptive variables including measures of location (means, medians), dispersion (standard deviations), changes from baseline, 95% confidence intervals, Cohen's effect sizes, and absolute/relative frequencies for qualitative variables.

rows.duration_additional_info: Retrospective, intraindividual controlled study. The primary outcome (VNRS pain score) is assessed after 12 weeks. The study analyzed data from patients using the Vivira app from 01/01/2020 onwards. Data analysis was conducted between 2021-06-15 and 2021-06-22.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study (N=157 analyzed, 150 planned). Intraindividual control: the control is the pre-treatment (historical/baseline) finding of the same patient. Intervention: use of the Vivira digital home exercise app.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app. Single-armed study with intraindividual control (baseline/pre-finding of the same patient serves as control).
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: VNRS (verbal-numerical rating scale) of pain after 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Proprietary functional test (series of functional scores). Descriptive variables including measures of location (means, medians), dispersion (standard deviations), changes from baseline, 95% confidence intervals, Cohen's effect sizes, and absolute/relative frequencies for qualitative variables.

rows.duration_additional_info: The digital therapeutic consists of a twelve-week individualized home exercise program. The study is a single-arm observational study with pre-post comparison. Actual data collection ran from 2022-04-06 to 2022-05-10.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study; no control group. 7842 patients with non-specific and degenerative back, hip, and knee pain using the digital therapeutic 'Vivira' (adaptive home exercise program with individualized exercise progressions).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm: intra-individual pre-post comparison of patients with non-specific and degenerative musculoskeletal conditions undergoing a twelve-week individualized home exercise program (DiGA 'Vivira').
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: Self-reported pain intensity assessed with a verbal-numeric rating scale (VNRS, 0-10). Secondary outcomes: (1) Clinical relevance of pain intensity improvement assessed via responder analysis using three criteria established in the literature (criterion met / criterion not met); (2) Physical functional capacity assessed in the dimensions of mobility, strength, and coordination on a discrete scale; (3) Patient characteristics associated with good treatment adherence.

rows.duration_additional_info: Pain intensity was assessed at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks. The observation period was 12 weeks total.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm observational study; 3629 patients using the Vivira digital home exercise program (no separate control group).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: All participants received the smartphone-based home exercise program 'Vivira' (a fully approved DiGA) for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee. No comparator arm.
rows.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-reported pain intensity measured with a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS) at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: self-reported function scores for back, hip, and knee pain areas, assessed using matched-pair analysis (Wilcoxon signed-rank test). Attrition/follow-up rates were also tracked.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (Skillings-Mack test: T3628=5308; P<.001), with changes within the range of clinically relevant improvement. Function scores showed a generally positive but more variable response across back, hip, and knee pain areas. Relevant attrition at follow-up was noted as a challenge.

rows.duration_additional_info: The study assessed pain score changes over the intended 12-week duration of the program. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Observational study using an incomplete matched block design; no separate control group described. All 2517 participants used the Vivira smartphone-based home exercise program.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm observational study. All participants used Vivira, a smartphone-based program for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee. A matched comparison of self-reported functional scores and adherence rates was also presented.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores (0-10 scale), self-reported functional scores (strength, mobility, coordination), adherence rates. Measures collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Overall, initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19/10 (SD 1.96) to 3.35/10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores; participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in coordination scores. A high yet expected attrition rate was observed across all pain areas and pain durations.

rows.duration_additional_info: The study does not specify the duration of the program or when the primary endpoint was assessed.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Groups defined by use profiles: High Use (HU), Intermediate Use (IU), Low Use (LU), and Sub-Low Use (sub-LU). Specific group sizes not reported in the abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Four use profile groups were compared: High Use (HU), Intermediate Use (IU), Low Use (LU), and Sub-Low Use (sub-LU) of a digital home exercise program for nonspecific back pain (NSBP). Propensity Score Matching (PSM) was used to comparatively assess outcomes across these groups.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity; clinically relevant pain improvement (using commonly accepted thresholds for clinically relevant improvements in sensitivity analyses); use/adherence profiles of a digital home exercise program.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles were associated with a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement in self-reported pain intensities. Achieving any higher use level was associated with a significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement. The study concluded that a High Use (HU) profile should be pursued to enable optimal effectiveness of DTx home exercise programs.

by_dtx.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter

studies.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 3 months (24 weeks from baseline)
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint is assessed at 12 weeks. A follow-up (clinical ratings and attention test) is carried out after 3 months post-treatment (i.e., 24 weeks from baseline). The study completion date was 24 February 2026, with primary completion on 21 November 2025.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 15 (Attexis + verum tDCS) and C = 15 (Attexis + placebo/sham tDCS)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): 15 patients receive three-month access to the psychotherapy app Attexis and a Sooma tDCS device set to verum (2mA, 20 minutes/day, 60 sessions, stimulating left DLPFC). Arm 2 (Placebo Comparator): 15 patients receive three-month access to the psychotherapy app Attexis and a Sooma tDCS device set to sham.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes (assessed at 12 weeks): (1) Compliance – number of days (out of 60) of actual (placebo-)tDCS treatment; (2) Efficacy 1 – number of responders according to the ASRS-v1.1 total score; (3) Efficacy 2 – number of responders according to the ASRS-v1.1 attention subscale; (4) Efficacy 3 – number of responders according to the ASRS-v1.1 hyperactivity subscale; (5) Stability of effects – number of responders according to change in ASRS-v1.1 sumscore at 24 weeks. Secondary outcomes (assessed at 12 weeks): ASRS-v1.1 sumscore (0–72), ASRS-v1.1 attention subscale (0–36), ASRS-v1.1 hyperactivity subscale (0–36), and Usability Experience Questionnaire (UEQ) sumscore.

studies.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, 6 months post-allocation
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months post-allocation; follow-up assessment at 6 months post-allocation. The attexis program remains accessible for 180 days once registered.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (attexis + TAU) and control group (TAU only); total enrolled = 337 (planned 326)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (EXPERIMENTAL): attexis + TAU – participants receive access to the digital health application attexis (CBT-based, web browser accessible, 180-day access) in addition to treatment as usual. Arm 2 (NO_INTERVENTION): TAU only – participants receive treatment as usual without access to attexis.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: ADHD Symptom Severity assessed via ASRS v1.1 (total score 0-72; higher = worse) at 3 months. Secondary outcomes: Depressive Symptomatology via PHQ-9 (total score 0-27; higher = worse) at 3 and 6 months; Self-Esteem via Rosenberg Self Esteem Scale (RSES; total score 0-30; higher = better) at 3 and 6 months; Functioning via Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS; total score 0-40; higher = worse impairment) at 3 and 6 months; Health-Related Quality of Life via AQoL-8D (total score 0-100; higher = better) at 3 and 6 months; ADHD Symptom Severity via ASRS v1.1 at 6 months.

studies.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months post-randomization (T2)
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months post-randomization (T1); follow-up conducted at 6 months post-randomization (T2).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: attexis + TAU; Control: TAU alone. Total N = 337 (exact group sizes not reported in abstract).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: attexis (fully self-guided digital intervention based on CBT and mindfulness principles) as an adjunct to treatment as usual (TAU). Arm 2: Treatment as usual (TAU) alone.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: ADHD symptom severity measured by Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS) total score at 3 months post-randomization (T1). Secondary outcomes: functional impairment, depressive symptoms, self-esteem, and health-related quality of life. Follow-up assessments at 6 months (T2). Subgroup analyses by sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. Adverse events monitoring.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intent-to-treat analyses showed significantly lower ADHD symptom severity in the intervention group at T1 (baseline-adjusted mean difference = -5.0 points; d = 0.85, p < .001). Significant improvements were also observed across all secondary outcomes (functional impairment, depressive symptoms, self-esteem, health-related quality of life). Effects remained stable at T2 (6 months). Responder analyses confirmed clinical relevance. Subgroup analyses demonstrated consistent effects across sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. No adverse events related to attexis were reported.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were required to use the app for at least 12 weeks and answer questions at four time points over a minimum of 12 weeks. The study ran from December 2021 to February 2024.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: access to the full version of the Cara Care für Reizdarm app; Control group: standard of care without use of the Cara Care für Reizdarm app or an alternative app. Total enrolled: 378 (planned: 374).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Access to the full version of the Cara Care für Reizdarm app for at least 12 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Standard of care with no application of the Cara Care für Reizdarm app or an alternative app.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Change in disease-specific symptom severity measured by IBS-SSS (Irritable Bowel Syndrome Severity Scoring System) in intervention and control groups. Additional primary measures: Quality of life (IBS-QOL), work and activity impairment (WPAI:IBS), health literacy (HLS-EU-Q16). Secondary outcomes: Anxiety (GAD-7), depressiveness (PHQ-9). All data collected via standardized, validated health questionnaires within the app at four time points over at least 12 weeks.

by_dtx.dtx_name: companion patella powered by medi - proved by Dt. Kniegesellschaft

studies.dtx_name: companion patella powered by medi - proved by Dt. Kniegesellschaft
studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes measured at 4 time points: Day 0, 30, 60, and 90. The intervention arm receives a 90-day personalized exercise therapy program via the DiGA companion patella app.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention arm: patients receive access code for the DiGA companion patella app (90-day personalized exercise therapy with evidence-based exercises and education). Control arm: patients receive a doctor's prescription for conventional analog physiotherapy treatment corresponding to statutory health insurance agreements. Total n=140.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): 90-day personalized app-based exercise therapy and education via the digital health application 'companion patella'. Arm 2 (Control): Conventional analog physiotherapy treatment by physiotherapists as per statutory health insurance agreements.
studies.collected_data: 1. Pain reduction measured on the Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) at 4 time points (Day 0/30/60/90). 2. Improvement of knee joint function measured with the Kujala Score at 4 time points (Day 0/30/60/90).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is 90 days for both arms. Primary and secondary outcomes are assessed at day 90.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 135 (DiGA companion® shoulder digital health application) and C = 135 (standard of care physiotherapy); 1:1 randomization
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients receive access to the digital health application (DiGA) companion® shoulder, which includes a 90-day personalized exercise therapy with evidence-based exercises and education, delivered digitally. The DiGA is a class I medical device (MDR). Arm 2 (Control): Patients receive standard of care for 90 days, including prescription of physiotherapy with usually 1 to 3 prescriptions of 6 units each, as provided in the therapeutic products catalog.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Shoulder-specific pain reduction measured with the Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) after 90 days. Secondary outcomes: (1) Shoulder-specific functional improvement measured with the Shoulder Pain and Disability Index (SPADI) after 90 days; (2) Number of days of incapacity for work due to illness within the survey period measured via self-reporting at 1 measurement point (day 90). Inclusion criterion also references NPRS ≥4 for maximum pain intensity in the last 7 days at baseline.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is nine weeks (post-assessment), with a six-month follow-up assessment. The wait-list control group receives access to Deprexis after six months. Three assessment time points: baseline, post-intervention (9 weeks), and follow-up (6 months).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Deprexis (immediate access, experimental) and Wait-list (access to Deprexis after 6 months, active comparator); total enrolled = 180 (100 with multiple sclerosis and 100 with epilepsy planned, 180 actual).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Deprexis): Participants receive immediate access to the web-based Deprexis program consisting of 10 content modules plus introductory and summary modules, based on cognitive-behavioral techniques, delivered online over approximately 9 weeks. Arm 2 (Wait-list): Participants are placed on a wait-list and receive access to Deprexis after a six-month delay.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Severity of depressive symptoms assessed with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) over a 7-day timeframe, measured at baseline, post-intervention (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up. Secondary outcome: Quality of life assessed by the WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF) over a 14-day timeframe. Other outcomes: Depressive symptoms assessed by the Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (QIDS, 7 days); core self-evaluations assessed by the Core Self-Evaluations Scale (CSES, 7 days); suicidal behaviors and tendencies assessed by the Suicidal Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R, 7 days); quality of life assessed by the Hamburg MS Quality of Life Questionnaire (HALEMS, 7 days).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The QIDS-SR-16 is administered every 2 weeks during the 8-week treatment period. Pre- and post-treatment phone interviews and questionnaires are also conducted.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Computerized self-help program (Deprexis) for 8 weeks; Control: Wait List Control (8-week wait before accessing Deprexis). Estimated total enrollment: 500.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Active Comparator): Participants have access to the Deprexis computerized self-help program for depression for 8 weeks, consisting of 10 content modules based on cognitive-behavioral approaches. Arm 2 (No Intervention/Wait List Control): Participants wait 8 weeks after assignment before they can access the Deprexis program.
studies.collected_data: Primary: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report) (QIDS-SR-16), administered every 2 weeks during the 8-week treatment period to measure depression symptom severity. Secondary: Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D) at pre- and post-treatment phone interviews; Inventory of Depression and Anxiety Symptoms (IDAS) at pre- and post-treatment; Psychiatric Diagnostic Screening Questionnaire (PDSQ) at pre- and post-treatment; Risky Families Questionnaire (RFQ) at pre-treatment; Massachusetts General Hospital Antidepressant Treatment History Questionnaire (ATRQ) at pre-treatment; Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) at pre- and post-treatment. Saliva samples collected for DNA/genetic analysis.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The study involved a 10-week treatment period. The wait-list group received access to Deprexis after 10 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Three groups: Deprexis unguided (web-based self-help without therapist support), Deprexis guided (web-based self-help plus weekly e-mail contact with therapist), and Wait-list control. Total N=76.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Deprexis (unguided) - CBT-based online self-help program without any therapist contact during the 10-week treatment period. Arm 2: Deprexis (guided, with therapist) - same CBT-based online self-help program plus scheduled weekly e-mail feedback from a therapist and the possibility to ask questions via e-mail. Arm 3: Wait-list control - no intervention for 10 weeks, after which participants received access to Deprexis.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II; 21-item self-report, scores 0-63, higher = more severe depression) at 10 weeks. Secondary outcomes: Brief Symptom Inventory (53-item BSI; psychopathology severity, scores 0-4) at 10 weeks; Inventory of Interpersonal Problems (64-item IIP; scores 0-4) at 10 weeks; WHOQOL-BREF (quality of life, scores 0-100, higher = better quality of life) at 10 weeks. Other outcome: CSQ-8 (Client Satisfaction Questionnaire; scores 1-4, higher = greater satisfaction) at 10 weeks.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months and 12 months
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were randomised to twelve weeks of online self-help or waiting-list control. Secondary outcomes were assessed at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months. After the one-year period, controls also received online self-help. Primary completion date was February 2015; full study completion was February 2017.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis online self-help with and without e-mail support) and Control (waiting-list/no intervention); total N = 1013
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Deprexis – online self-help program with and without e-mail support for 12 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): No intervention (waiting-list control); after the one-year assessment period, controls also received access to online self-help.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) at 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HRSD) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms (QIDS) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; PHQ-9 at 6 months and 12 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint is assessed at 8 weeks post-randomization. The BDI timeframe is listed as '7 Days' (recall period), but the intervention/assessment period is 8 weeks. Wait-list control group receives access to Deprexis after 8 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis): immediate access to web-based program; Control (Wait-list): access to Deprexis after 8 weeks. Total N = 200 (individual group sizes not specified in the record).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental - Deprexis): Participants receive immediate access to the web-based intervention Deprexis, consisting of 10 content modules plus one introductory and one summary module, based on cognitive-behavioral techniques. Arm 2 (Active Comparator - Wait-list): Participants receive access to Deprexis after an 8-week delay.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Severity of depressive symptoms assessed with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) at baseline and 8 weeks. Secondary outcome: Quality of life assessed by the WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF, 26-item version covering physical, psychological, social, and environment domains) at baseline and 8 weeks. Exclusion screening: Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Six months after randomization
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint is assessed three months after randomization (~13 weeks). End of inpatient treatment is on average 6 weeks. The intervention (deprexis® or online information) runs for 90 days. Follow-up assessments occur at end of inpatient treatment (average 6 weeks), three months after randomization, and six months after randomization.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Experimental (deprexis® online self-help program): part of 229 enrolled; Control (online information about depression as placebo comparator): part of 229 enrolled. Exact group sizes not specified in the raw data.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Patients use deprexis® online self-help program as an additional treatment during inpatient stay and as aftercare for 90 days. Arm 2 (Placebo Comparator): Patients receive weekly online information about depression for 90 days. Both arms receive these interventions in addition to regular inpatient psychosomatic treatment.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck's Depression Inventory (BDI-II) at three months after randomization. Secondary outcomes: PHQ-9 (depression), BDI-II, GAD-7 (anxiety), Dysfunctional Attitude Scale (DAS), Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSE), EUROHIS-QOL 8 (quality of life), short form Work Ability Index (WAI), self-devised questionnaire on acceptance and utilization of deprexis® and of information placebo, Childhood Trauma Questionnaire (CTQ), OPD-Structure Questionnaire short form (OPD-SFK), Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ), course of mood/depressiveness/utilization of units during inpatient treatment, utilization of other treatments after inpatient treatment, satisfaction with inpatient treatment, willingness to pay, remission from depression (BDI score <13 and Reliable Change Index). Timepoints: randomization, end of inpatient treatment (average 6 weeks), three months after randomization, six months after randomization.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes, assessments at 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months from baseline
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary outcome (PHQ-D-9) is assessed at baseline, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months. The primary completion date was March 2015 and the study completion date was May 2015. The trial started in September 2012.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Two groups: Stepped Care Model (SCM) intervention group and Treatment as Usual (TAU) control group; cluster-randomized at the level of general practitioners. Total enrolled: 737 participants.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Stepped Care Intervention/SCM): Patients screened with PHQ-9 (inclusion if >4 points) and receive stepped treatment based on depression severity — mild depression: Step I (active monitoring) or Step II (bibliotherapy or online self-help 'Deprexis®') or Step II+ (telephone-based psychotherapy); moderate depression: Step III (outpatient psychotherapy or psychopharmacological treatment); severe depression: Step IV (combined psychotherapy and psychopharmacological treatment, optionally inpatient). Arm 2 (Control/Treatment as Usual): Patients screened with PHQ-D-9 and then receive treatment as usual from general physician or other health service providers.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Change in depression severity measured by PHQ-D-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire 9, German version) at baseline, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months. Secondary outcomes: Cost-benefit ratio in QALYs (baseline, 6 months, 12 months); Response/Remission/Relapse via PHQ-D-9 (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Quality of life via EQ-5D (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Quality of life via SF-12 (baseline, 3, 6, 9 months); Health service utilization and medication consumption via non-standardized items (baseline, 6, 12 months); Self-esteem via Rosenberg self-esteem scale (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Therapeutic alliance via Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ) (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Anxiety symptoms via PHQ-D anxiety scale (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Panic symptoms via PHQ-D (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); Somatisation via PHQ-D (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months); General anxiety disorder symptoms via GAD-7 (baseline, 3, 6, 12 months).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, 8 weeks after post-treatment (week 16 from baseline)
studies.duration_additional_info: The study involves an 8-week intervention period (baseline to post-treatment at week 8), followed by an 8-week follow-up assessment at week 16. Primary completion is estimated April 2029.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Deprexis (internet-delivered intervention) vs. Treatment-as-Usual (TAU) control; RCT with n=132 for Aim 2 (plus n=16-20 for qualitative Aim 1), total estimated enrollment 152
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Deprexis – an 8-week self-guided internet-delivered psychosocial treatment for depressive symptoms, consisting of 12 modules (10 core content modules plus introductory and summary modules), drawing from various theoretical frameworks including CBT. Arm 2 (Control): Treatment-as-Usual (TAU) – access to standard non-study care with no additional intervention.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: (1) Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology Self-Report (QIDS-SR-16) – 16-item self-report measure of depressive symptom severity (scores 0–27), assessed at baseline, post-treatment (week 8), and follow-up (week 16); (2) World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule-II (WHODAS) – 36-item questionnaire assessing functional disability across 7 domains and total score (scores 0–100), assessed at baseline, week 8, and week 16; (3) Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) – 3-item self-report measure of symptom-related disability across work/school, family, and social functioning (scores 0–30), assessed at baseline, week 8, and week 16. Exploratory variables: demographic variables, baseline psychopathology, credibility, and Deprexis usage as potential moderators of treatment effects. Qualitative interviews (Aim 1) assessing Veterans' perceptions, needs, and preferences regarding Deprexis.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 3 months post-intervention (T3), assessed at approximately 20 weeks from baseline
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants are assessed at 4 time points: baseline (T0), mid-intervention at 4 weeks (T1), post-intervention at 8 weeks (T2), and follow-up at 3 months post-intervention (~20 weeks, T3). The intervention period is 8 weeks. Time frames include a +/- 2 week window.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Experimental group 1 (online program only), Experimental group 2 (online program + online forum), Control group (waitlist); total estimated N=240, approximately 80 per arm
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Access to Deprexis online self-help program for 8 weeks, at least 30 minutes 1-2 times per week. Arm 2 (Second Experimental/Active Comparator): Access to Deprexis plus an online peer-support forum for 8 weeks; participants divided into groups of 10 to share experiences, with a moderator. Arm 3 (Control): Waitlist control group with no intervention during the 8-week period, after which they gain access to the online program.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms at post-intervention (8 weeks). Secondary outcomes: Fribourg Reward Task (reaction times, accuracy, mood reactions to monetary and social rewards); Snaith-Hamilton Pleasure Scale (SHAPS) for anhedonia/hedonic capacity; Behavioral Inhibition System/Behavioral Activation System (BIS/BAS) for reward sensitivity; Perceived Social Support (PSS) questionnaire. Exploratory/additional outcomes: Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7); General Self-Efficacy Scale (GSES); Quality of Life (SF-12); Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSE); Perceived Stress Scale-Short Version (PSS); Somatic Symptom Scale (SSS-8); PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5); Childhood Trauma Questionnaire-Short Form (CTQ-SF); System Usability Scale (SUS); Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ). Assessments at T0 (baseline), T1 (4 weeks), T2 (8 weeks), T3 (3 months post-intervention/~20 weeks).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at month 6 and month 12
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint is assessed at Month 3 (approximately 12 weeks). The trial also includes an extension phase for maintenance with long-term online follow-up at month 6 and month 12. The waitlist control group gains access to Deprexis after a 6-month waiting period.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Three arms: Deprexis (intervention alone), DeprexisPlus (intervention plus weekly email support), and Waitlist Control (no intervention for 6 months). Total enrolled: 279 participants (individual group sizes not specified in the raw data).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Deprexis): Access to the web-based Deprexis program (MS-adapted CBT-based online tool) for 3 months. Arm 2 (DeprexisPlus): Access to the same Deprexis program plus scheduled e-mail contact once per week from a clinical psychologist for 3 months. Arm 3 (Waitlist Control): No intervention for 6 months, after which participants receive full access to Deprexis.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II), assessed from Month 0 to Month 3. Secondary outcomes: WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF, 4 subscales: Physical, Psychological, Social Relationships, Environmental); Multiple Sclerosis Impact Scale (MSIS, 2 subscales: Physical and Psychological); Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions (FSMC, total score and Motor/Cognitive subscales); Chalder Fatigue Scale — all assessed Month 0 to Month 3. Other outcomes: Brief International Cognitive Assessment for Multiple Sclerosis (BICAMS, including SMDT, CVLT-II, BVMT-R) from Month 0 to Month 3; Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R) at Month 3; Mini International Neuropsychiatric Interview (MINI v5.0.0) dichotomous variable 'Major Depressive Episode, Current (yes/no)' from Month 0 to Month 3.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months from baseline (i.e., 3 months after the primary endpoint)
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is 12 weeks (3 months). The primary endpoint is assessed at post (3 months). Secondary outcomes are assessed at baseline, post (3 months), and follow-up (6 months).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis online self-help) and Control (Care as usual / CAU); total N = 163 (individual group sizes not specified in the raw data)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Deprexis – 12 weeks of internet-based online self-help program. Arm 2 (Active Comparator): CAU – Care as usual (waiting-list control).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire – 9 items), assessed at baseline and post (3 months). Secondary outcomes: QIDS-SR16 (Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms – Self-Report), GAD-7 (Generalized Anxiety Disorder – 7 items), PHQ-15 (Patient Health Questionnaire – 15 items), SF-12 (Short Form Health Survey – 12 items); all assessed at baseline, post (3 months), and follow-up (6 months).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes – secondary outcomes measured at 12 weeks (T1), 6 months (T2), and 12 months (T3) after baseline
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention group used Deprexis for 12 weeks. The primary endpoint (costs) was assessed over a period of one year after the end of treatment. Secondary outcomes (PHQ-9, SF-12, EQ-5D, WSAS) were collected at baseline (T0), 12 weeks (T1), 6 months (T2), and 12 months (T3).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: access to Deprexis internet-based self-help program for 12 weeks; Control group: no access to Deprexis. Total enrolled: 3832 (planned: 5400); individual group sizes not specified in the record.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Uses the Deprexis internet-based self-help program for 12 weeks, with questionnaires (PHQ-9, SF-12, EQ-5D, WSAS) and cost data from DAK. Arm 2 (Control): No access to Deprexis; same questionnaires administered; also asked to confirm they had no contact with Deprexis during the observation period.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Healthcare costs over one year after end of treatment, determined from DAK (statutory health insurance) cost data. Secondary outcomes: Depression severity (PHQ-9) at T0, T1 (12 weeks), T2 (6 months), T3 (12 months); Health-related quality of life (SF-12 and EQ-5D) at T0, T1, T2, T3; Social and occupational functioning (WSAS) at T0, T1, T2, T3; Satisfaction with DAK advisory services and acceptance of the Deprexis program (intervention group); Confirmation of non-exposure to Deprexis (control group).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Intervention period was 8 weeks; assessments conducted at baseline and post-intervention (8 weeks). Wait-list control group gains access to Deprexis after completion of the post survey.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Deprexis internet intervention) and wait-list control group; total planned 140 participants (108 planned originally)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Internet-intervention 'Deprexis' for treating depressive symptoms, consisting of ten modules plus one summary module based on cognitive-behavioral therapy (behavioral activation, cognitive modification, interpersonal and problem solving skills, relaxation methods) over 8 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Wait-list control group receiving no additional treatment during the intervention period (may continue previously initiated treatments); gains access to Deprexis after completion of the post survey.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 items depression module (PHQ-9) - self-rating questionnaire measuring depressive symptoms over the past week (at baseline and post-assessment). Secondary outcomes: South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS) - screening for pathological gambling (at baseline and post-assessment); Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS) - measures severity of gambling symptoms within the past week (at baseline and post-assessment); General Anxiety Disorder Screener (GAD-7) - assesses anxiety levels over the last two weeks (at baseline and post-assessment); Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ) - brief screening instrument for common mental disorders (only at baseline); Subjective Appraisal of the intervention by treatment group participants (only at post-assessment). Demographic and psychopathological information, clinical anamnesis (current treatments, use of drugs and self-help, psychiatric diagnoses) also collected.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The 12-week intervention/waiting period includes baseline assessment (T1) and final assessment after 12 weeks (T4) for all arms. The supported intervention group (IG-A) also has interim assessments at 4 weeks (T2) and 8 weeks (T3). After the 12-week observation period, the waitlist control group also receives access to deprexis®. Scheduled completion date is June 2027.
