studies.intervention_and_control_group: IG-A (Supported Intervention Group, n ≥ 100): DiGA deprexis® for 12 weeks plus brief structured check-in sessions at 4, 8, and 12 weeks; IG-B (Unsupported Intervention Group, n ≥ 100): DiGA deprexis® for 12 weeks without adjunct consultations; CG (Waitlist Control Group, n ≥ 100): treatment as usual during the 12-week waiting period, then access to deprexis® after the observation period.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (IG-A): Participants on the waiting list receive access to the digital health application deprexis® for 12 weeks plus brief structured therapeutic check-in sessions after 4, 8, and 12 weeks (not psychotherapy per se). Arm 2 (IG-B): Participants on the waiting list receive access to deprexis® for 12 weeks with no additional consultations. Arm 3 (CG): Participants on the waiting list receive treatment as usual (no DiGA, no additional intervention from the clinic) for 12 weeks; after the observation period they also receive access to deprexis®.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes assessed via standardized self-report questionnaires at T1 (baseline) and T4 (12 weeks), with additional assessments at T2 (4 weeks) and T3 (8 weeks) for IG-A: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) for depressive symptoms; GAD-7 (Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale-7) for anxiety symptoms; SCL-90-R (Symptom Checklist-90-R) for global psychological distress and subjective symptom-related functional impairment. Secondary outcomes: acceptance of the DiGA (user satisfaction, perceived fit, perceived usefulness); usage intensity and adherence (frequency and extent of engagement); dropout rates and reasons for discontinuation; subjectively perceived effects of and burden related to the intervention — assessed via study-specific standardized self-report questionnaires and structured interviews. Exploratory outcomes: comparison of supported vs. unsupported DiGA use regarding efficacy, adherence, and attrition; potential moderators and predictors (sociodemographic characteristics, baseline symptom severity, attitudes toward digital health applications); evaluation of clinical relevance of DiGA as a bridging intervention.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at pretreatment, posttreatment (12 weeks), and follow-up (6 months after treatment). All secondary outcomes also assessed at the same three timepoints.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Psychotherapy plus deprexis web-based program; Control group: Psychotherapy without deprexis. Target enrollment of 800 participants total.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Participants receive regular face-to-face psychotherapy plus access to the deprexis web-based self-help program (10 modules covering psychoeducation, behavioral activation, cognitive restructuring, mindfulness, interpersonal skills, relaxation, physical activity, problem-focused approaches, expressive writing/schema-focused content, and positive psychology). Arm 2 (Control): Participants receive traditional face-to-face psychotherapy as usual without access to deprexis.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depressive symptomatology measured with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) at pretreatment, posttreatment (12 weeks), and follow-up (6 months). Secondary outcomes (assessed at pretreatment, posttreatment, and follow-up): 1) Anxiety symptoms via GAD-7; 2) Somatic symptomatology via PHQ somatic symptom module; 3) Quality of life via SF-12; 4) Psychotherapeutic progress via FEP-2 (well-being, symptoms, interpersonal relationships, incongruence); 5) Common mental disorder screening via Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ, 15-item); 6) Suicidal tendencies via Suicidal Behaviours Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R); 7) Psychological empowerment via adapted Spreitzer scale; 8) Depression diagnosis via licensed assessment.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants are assessed at three time points: baseline (before treatment), after 10 weeks (primary endpoint), and after 6 months. The intervention group starts immediately after consent; the waitlist control group receives access after the 10-week waiting period.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (immediate access to web-based deprexis treatment) vs. waitlist control group (access after 10-week waiting period); target enrollment of 80 participants total.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Immediate access to the web-based psychological treatment 'deprexis', guided by a clinical psychologist, consisting of 10 content modules based on cognitive-behavioral principles. Arm 2 (Control): Waitlist control condition receiving access to deprexis after a waiting period of 10 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Depression levels by BDI-II (Beck Depression Inventory-II), clinical depression by the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ), and clinical interview using the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM Diagnosis (SCID). Secondary outcomes: Stress perception by Perceived Stress Questionnaire (PSQ); Social support by the ENRICHD Social Support Inventory (ESSI); Quality of life by the WHOQOL-BREF; Health behavior; Client satisfaction by the Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ). All assessments conducted at three time points: baseline, 10 weeks, and 6 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes – assessments at 18 weeks, and 6 months after program usage termination; control group also assessed 9 weeks after completing the delayed program
studies.duration_additional_info: The program itself runs for 9 weeks. Data were collected at baseline, 9 weeks, and 18 weeks for both groups. The control group (delayed treatment) also completed questionnaires 9 weeks after finishing the program. Both groups completed questionnaires 6 months after their respective program usage termination.
studies.study_size_participants: 200 (target enrollment; actual enrollment date listed as 30/06/2008 without a specific participant count confirmed)
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Deprexis® online self-help program (add-on to treatment as usual); Control: Waitlist (treatment as usual only, with delayed access to the program). Target enrollment: 200 participants.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Participants received access to the Deprexis® online self-help program for 9 weeks in addition to treatment as usual. Arm 2 (Control/Waitlist): Participants did not receive access to the program during the initial period and continued treatment as usual; they received delayed access to the program after the initial waitlist period.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depressive symptom level measured with the German version of the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) at baseline, 9 weeks, and 18 weeks; also 9 weeks post-program for the control group and 6 months after program usage termination for both groups. Secondary outcomes: (1) Work and Social Adjustment Scale (German translation) at the same timepoints; (2) Subjective usefulness of the program measured by individually designed items.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The study examined an 8-week course of Deprexis compared with a waitlist control.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Deprexis (internet intervention); Control: waitlist. Total N = 295.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Deprexis (8-week internet-based intervention for depression); Arm 2: Waitlist control. Nationally recruited sample from the United States.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depression Symptoms (QIDS) assessed at baseline and after 8 weeks; specific symptoms tracked included sadness, indecision, early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, agitation, and somatic symptoms. Network analysis and mean-level approaches were used to examine symptom-level changes.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Deprexis directly improved sadness and indecision. Changes in these symptoms were associated with changes in early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, and agitation. All of these symptoms except agitation and early insomnia showed significant decreases with Deprexis compared with waitlist after correcting for multiple comparisons. Six additional symptoms, particularly somatic symptoms, were not impacted by Deprexis compared with waitlist. The efficacy of Deprexis was attributed to its direct impact on sadness and indecision.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Patients were followed by structured telephone interviews post-discharge up to the study endpoint at 12 weeks after deprexis-initiation; no follow-up beyond the 12-week primary endpoint is described.
studies.duration_additional_info: There was no fixed time frame for inpatient treatment duration; the study used a pragmatic approach with flexible discharge management. Assessments were carried out at study entry and every 2 weeks. The 12-week period began at deprexis-initiation (during inpatient stay), and post-discharge follow-up via telephone interviews continued until the 12-week endpoint.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: TAU-PLUS (TAU + guided deprexis): N=36; TAU (treatment-as-usual): N=33
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (TAU): Treatment-as-usual for 12 weeks, including routine psychiatric inpatient care with flexible discharge and post-discharge follow-up. Arm 2 (TAU-PLUS): Treatment-as-usual plus guided use of the web-based self-help program deprexis for 12 weeks, started during inpatient treatment and continued post-discharge.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II) assessed at study entry and every 2 weeks up to week 12. Secondary outcomes: Hamilton Depression Scale (HAMD), Clinical Global Impression-Severity (CGI-S), WHO Well-Being Index (WHO-5), Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ). Additional measures: working alliance with deprexis, inpatient treatment duration, daily activity, utilization of post-hospital care after discharge, re-admission rates, prescribed antidepressant medication.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12, modified ITT analyses showed significant between-group differences in BDI-II scores in favor of TAU-PLUS (p=.03), corresponding to a medium effect size (d=-.73, 95% CI -1.4 to .06). TAU-PLUS patients showed greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70, 95% CI -.03 to 1.38) and were discharged significantly earlier from inpatient treatment (p=.003). Post-discharge, the TAU-PLUS group reported a lower rate of post-hospital care (p=.01) and re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcome measures including therapist alliance were not significantly different between groups at study endpoint. The patients' working alliance with deprexis significantly predicted MDD improvement and wellbeing. TAU plus deprexis was superior to TAU in improving subjective depression severity and daily activity.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; three-months' and nine-months' follow-up after post-treatment
studies.duration_additional_info: The 12-week online intervention was followed by assessments at post-treatment, 3-month follow-up, and 9-month follow-up. Primary outcome (statutory health insurance costs) was estimated over the study year using administrative data.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Participants randomized to either a 12-week online intervention (deprexis) or a control condition; specific group sizes not reported in the abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): 12-week internet-based intervention using 'deprexis'. Arm 2 (Control): control condition (details not specified in the abstract).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: statutory health insurance costs estimated using health insurers' administrative data. Secondary outcomes assessed via patient self-report at baseline, post-treatment, 3-month follow-up, and 9-month follow-up: depression severity, health-related quality of life, and impairment in functioning.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: In both groups, total statutory health insurance costs decreased during the study period. The intervention group showed a 32% reduction in total costs (from 3139€/year at baseline to 2119€ in the study year), compared to a 13% mean reduction in the control group; changes from baseline differed significantly between groups. The intervention group also showed a significantly greater reduction in depression severity, significantly greater reduction in impairment in functioning, and a significantly greater increase in health-related quality of life compared to the control group.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; the intervention group had access to Deprexis for 90 days (approximately 13 weeks), and the control group received delayed access after 8 weeks
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed at 8 weeks. The intervention group received immediate access to Deprexis for 90 days (~13 weeks). The control group received delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks. Overall program duration was 90 days.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (immediate access): n=94 (treatment as usual plus immediate access to Deprexis for 90 days); Control (delayed access): n=95 (treatment as usual and delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Treatment as usual plus immediate access to Deprexis (internet-based self-guided CBT program) for 90 days (n=94). Arm 2 (Control): Treatment as usual with delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks (n=95).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptom self-assessment. Secondary outcomes: Clinical Outcome in Routine Evaluation-Outcome Measurement (CORE-OM) for general psychological state; perceived self-efficacy measure. Additional data: login frequency/engagement with Deprexis, remission rates, deterioration rates, dropout rates, demographic and clinical variables, participant satisfaction with Deprexis. Participants also underwent a semistructured interview at screening.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analysis (linear mixed model) showed participants receiving immediate Deprexis access improved significantly more than the delayed access control group on: PHQ-9 (Cohen d=0.80; P<.001); CORE-OM (Cohen d=0.82; P<.001); perceived self-efficacy (Cohen d=0.63; P<.001). Remission was achieved by 21% (20/94) in the intervention group vs 7% (7/95) in the control group (P<.001). Deterioration rates were lower in the immediate access group. Dropout rate was high but no significant differences in demographic and clinical variables between dropouts and completers were found. Participants reported medium to high satisfaction with Deprexis. Average logins in the intervention group: 14.81 (SD 12.16).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were randomized to receive an 8-week course of treatment immediately or after an 8-week delay (waitlist control). No follow-up beyond the primary endpoint is mentioned.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 285 (immediate 8-week Deprexis treatment) and C = 91 (8-week waitlist control, then received treatment)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Immediate access to Deprexis (Internet intervention for depression) for 8 weeks with relatively minimal support (n=285). Arm 2: Waitlist control for 8 weeks, after which they received the Deprexis intervention (n=91).
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms-Self-Report (QIDS-SR) for self-reported depression symptoms; interviewer-rated depression symptoms; well-being measures; depression-related disability; symptoms of social anxiety; panic symptoms; traumatic intrusions. Primary outcome: reduction in self-reported depression symptoms (QIDS-SR) at 8 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analyses showed treatment was associated with greater reduction in self-reported depression symptoms (effect size d = .80) and 12 times greater likelihood of experiencing at least 50% symptom improvement compared with waitlist control. Similar effects were observed for secondary outcomes including interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, and depression-related disability. Treatment effects for symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions were relatively small.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at 12 weeks; secondary outcomes assessed at both 12 weeks and 6 months follow-up. Working alliance assessed after 6 and 12 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Two active treatment conditions: (1) regular psychotherapy alone (control), and (2) psychotherapy plus web-based depression program 'deprexis' (intervention). Exact group sizes not reported separately.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Regular psychotherapy alone. Arm 2: Regular psychotherapy plus adjunctive web-based depression program ('deprexis'). Participants were randomized within therapists.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: depressive symptoms measured with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) at 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: anxiety symptoms, somatic symptoms, and quality of life at 12 weeks and 6 months follow-up. Working alliance assessed after 6 and 12 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The combination of psychotherapy with the web-based program was more effective than psychotherapy alone at 12 weeks, with medium between-group effect sizes on primary depressive symptoms (Cohen's d = .51) and small to medium between-group effect sizes on secondary outcomes (Cohen's d = .07–.55). No negative side effects were observed in the blended format (e.g., working alliance was not lower than in psychotherapy alone). The study had a smaller than planned sample size and a high dropout rate at follow-up.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were assessed at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up. The intervention consisted of 9 weeks of immediate program access.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 320 (immediate program access as add-on to treatment-as-usual) and C = 76 (9-week delayed-access plus treatment-as-usual)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 9 weeks of immediate access to the Deprexis web-based program as an add-on to treatment-as-usual (N=320). Control arm: 9-week delayed-access plus treatment-as-usual (N=76). Randomization was 80:20 weighted.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depression severity measured by the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up. Secondary outcome: Social functioning measured by the Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSA) at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up. Subjective helpfulness of the program was also assessed.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: 55% (216/396) completed post-measurement at 9 weeks. Available case analyses: significant reduction in depression severity (BDI), Cohen's d = 0.64 (95% CI 0.33–0.94); significant improvement in social functioning (WSA), Cohen's d = 0.64 (95% CI 0.33–0.95). Improvements maintained at 6-month follow-up. Intention-to-treat analyses: BDI Cohen's d = 0.30 (95% CI 0.05–0.55); WSA Cohen's d = 0.36 (95% CI 0.10–0.61). Intervention group had significantly higher rate of depression symptom reduction (BDI OR = 6.8, 95% CI 2.90–18.19) and recovery (OR = 17.3, 95% CI 2.3–130). More than 80% of users felt the program was helpful.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months post randomization (secondary endpoint)
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint is at 3 months post randomization; secondary endpoint is at 6 months post randomization.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: regular psychotherapy plus web-based depression program ('deprexis'); Control: regular psychotherapy only. Total N = 800.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (intervention): regular psychotherapy plus the web-based depression program 'deprexis'. Arm 2 (control): regular psychotherapy only (no web-based program).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: depressive symptoms measured with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) at 3 months post randomization. Secondary outcomes: anxiety, somatic symptoms, quality of life, all assessed at 3 months and again at 6 months post randomization. Additional measures: working alliance, feasibility and acceptability of the treatment condition.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants had access to Deprexis (internet-based CBT) for 12 weeks total. After the first 2 weeks, eligible participants were randomized. TILS was administered once a week for 4 weeks following randomization.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: 40 participants were randomized after initial 2-week screening phase: TILS + Deprexis (intervention) vs. sham/placebo + Deprexis (control); exact per-group sizes not specified beyond total of 40 randomized.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (intervention): Deprexis (internet-based CBT) combined with active transcranial infrared laser stimulation (TILS) to the right forehead once a week for 4 weeks. Arm 2 (control): Deprexis combined with sham/placebo stimulation.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology-Self-Report (QIDS-SR) measured at baseline, week 2 (post-screening/pre-randomization), and week 12. Adverse effects were also monitored.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The sham group showed a 43% reduction in QIDS-SR scores from baseline to week 12, while the TILS group showed a 56% reduction, representing an additional 30% reduction attributable to TILS ([56-43]/43 = 30%). Participants who received TILS once a week for 4 weeks showed a significantly greater reduction in depressive symptoms compared to sham/placebo. No adverse effects were reported.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: After 8 weeks, all participants were invited for re-assessment. No follow-up beyond the 8-week primary endpoint is mentioned.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: 140 participants randomly allocated to intervention group (Deprexis online intervention) or wait-list control group; exact per-group sizes not specified in the abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Received access to 'Deprexis', an online intervention for depression, for 8 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Wait-list control group, assessed at the same 8-week timepoint.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms. Secondary outcomes: Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS) and South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS) for problematic gambling symptoms. Moderator variables included baseline gambling-related symptom severity and reasons for gambling (e.g., loneliness). Assessments conducted at baseline and at 8 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: ITT analyses showed the intervention led to a significant reduction in depressive symptoms and gambling-related symptoms compared to the wait-list control group, with moderate to strong effect sizes. PP analyses failed to yield significant results due to high rates of non-completion and limited statistical power. Moderator analyses indicated Deprexis was particularly beneficial in reducing problematic gambling for participants scoring high on baseline gambling-related symptoms and for those who gamble due to loneliness.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, long-term follow-up data collected over 36 months
studies.duration_additional_info: The EVIDENT study conducted assessments at intermediary and long-term time points over 36 months total.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Internet intervention for depression (Deprexis); Control: Care as usual. Total N = 1,013.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Internet-based cognitive-behavioral therapy intervention (Deprexis); Arm 2: Care as usual (control group).
studies.collected_data: Item-level and scale-level depressive symptom scores, quality of life measures (including overall impairment through emotional problems), Network Intervention Analysis variables (symptom-specific effects including worthlessness, fatigue, depressed mood, concentration, activity level). Assessments conducted at baseline, intermediary time points, and long-term follow-up over 36 months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Item-level analysis showed that scale-level effects can be explained by small item-level effects on most depressive symptoms at all assessment points. Higher baseline scores on certain items predicted overall symptom reduction throughout the entire assessment period. Network intervention analysis revealed that Deprexis directly affected certain symptoms (e.g., worthlessness and fatigue) and certain quality of life aspects (e.g., overall impairment through emotional problems), while other domains were affected indirectly (e.g., depressed mood, concentration, and activity level). The configuration of direct and indirect effects replicated findings from a previous study examining the same intervention. The intervention demonstrated both short-term and long-term effects over 36 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months (outcomes assessed at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months)
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasted 12 weeks; outcomes were assessed at baseline, three months (primary endpoint), and six months (follow-up).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 12-week web-based intervention (deprexis) plus care as usual; Control group: care as usual only. Total N = 1013 (individual group sizes not specified in the abstract).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): web-based cognitive behavioral intervention 'deprexis' for 12 weeks in addition to care as usual. Arm 2 (Control): care as usual only.
studies.collected_data: Health-related resource use, use of medication, incapacity to work, direct health care costs (including outpatient treatment costs and psychotherapeutic treatment costs); all collected via patients' self-report at baseline, three months, and six months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Total direct health care costs decreased in both groups during the study period, but changes from baseline did not significantly differ between groups. Costs for psychotherapeutic treatment decreased in the intervention group (-16.8%, €80) while they increased in the control group (+14.7%, €60); this difference was statistically significant (t(df=685) = 2.57; p = 0.008). The study suggests that deprexis over 12 weeks leads to a decrease in outpatient treatment costs, particularly psychotherapeutic treatment costs.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were allocated to either the intervention group or a waitlist control group for 9 weeks. No follow-up beyond the primary endpoint is mentioned.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis) = 45, Waitlist control = 45; 71 completed the trial (35 intervention, 36 control)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received a fully automated, internet-based cognitive behavioural therapy programme called Deprexis for 9 weeks. Control arm was a waitlist control group for the same 9-week period.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) assessed at 9 weeks via intention-to-treat analysis. Additional tracking included worsening of depressive symptoms from below to above clinical cutoff (BDI >13) and occurrence of suicidal ideation (adverse event monitoring).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: 71 patients completed the trial (35 intervention, 36 control). BDI scores decreased in the Deprexis group and increased in the control group; mean group difference -4.02 points (95% CI -7.26 to -0.79), p=0.015, effect size d=0.53. Worsening of depressive symptoms (BDI >13) occurred in 3 (7%) of 45 control patients and 0 Deprexis patients. No adverse events regarding new suicidal ideation were noted.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Depression symptoms were assessed pre and post intervention at 12 weeks.
studies.study_size_participants: 509 (intervention group analyzed; total RCT sample larger)
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (CAU + Deprexis): 509 participants; Control group: care as usual (CAU) - size not specified in this report
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Care as usual (CAU) alone (control). Arm 2: CAU plus the Internet intervention Deprexis (an online program for depression). Some participants in the intervention group also received e-mail guidance support (those with moderate symptom severity at baseline).
studies.collected_data: Depressive symptoms (pre and post intervention at 12 weeks); patient characteristics (age, sex, depressive symptoms); self-rating screening for mental disorders; attitudes towards online interventions; quality of life; adherence measures (usage time in hours, number of sessions); confidence in effectiveness of the program; guidance/e-mail support receipt.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Average adherence was 7 hours of usage time and 8 sessions. Patient characteristics explained 15–25% of variance in number of sessions. Older age (β = .16) and higher depressive symptoms (β = .15) were associated with higher usage duration. Higher adherence predicted greater symptom reduction over 12 weeks (number of sessions: β = .13, usage duration: β = .14), but this was largely explained by receiving guidance (β = .27 and .26). Effects were small overall.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months and 12 months
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 12 weeks post-test; secondary outcomes also assessed at 6 and 12 months follow-up.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 509 (Deprexis + CAU) and C = 504 (CAU alone)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received online intervention (Deprexis) in addition to care as usual (CAU); control group received CAU alone.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) at baseline, 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; sociodemographic variables including sex, age, educational attainment, employment status, relationship status, and lifetime frequency of depressive episodes.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Between-group differences were observed in post-test scores indicating the effectiveness of Deprexis. While effects of the intervention were demonstrated across all subgroups, some showed larger between-group differences than others. However, none of the selected sociodemographic variables (sex, age, educational attainment, employment status, relationship status, lifetime frequency of episodes) was found to moderate treatment outcomes based on linear regression models. The findings suggest Deprexis is equally beneficial across a wide range of participant characteristics.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: This is a reanalysis of data from the EVIDENT trial examining anxiety-related comorbidities as moderators of an Internet-based intervention for depression. The primary endpoint duration is not specified in this publication.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Deprexis + care-as-usual): n = 509; Control group (care-as-usual only): n = 504
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: access to the Internet intervention Deprexis adjunctively to care-as-usual. Control arm: access to care-as-usual only.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depression severity (scores 5–14 at baseline); Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ) for anxiety-related comorbidities at baseline (generalized anxiety disorder, social phobia, panic disorder, agoraphobia, panic disorder with agoraphobia, specific phobia, posttraumatic stress disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder); depression severity as continuous PHQ-9 scores and clinically important PHQ-9 changes as outcomes; multilevel models examining 12 potential moderators (8 anxiety comorbidities, depression severity, and 3 previously identified moderators).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Only social phobia moderated the intervention effect on depression outcome in favor of Deprexis (p < .05), among 12 potential moderators examined. This moderating effect was present for both continuous PHQ-9 scores and clinically important PHQ-9 changes. However, the moderating effect did not reach small effect sizes, accounted for less than 1% of the variance in change in depressive symptoms, and showed limited reproducibility in randomly selected split halves. Deprexis appears most effective for participants with mild to moderate depressive symptoms and comorbid social phobia, but further replications are necessary.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Data collected at baseline and posttreatment; no specific week duration for primary endpoint stated in the abstract.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: 223 patients completed the WAI-I; all received a guided Internet intervention (deprexis) for mild to moderate depression. Specific group sizes not detailed in the abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Multicenter trial examining a guided Internet intervention (deprexis) for patients with mild to moderate depression.
studies.collected_data: Working Alliance Inventory adapted for guided Internet interventions (WAI-I) at posttreatment; Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire (ZUF-8) at posttreatment; Attitudes toward Psychological Online-Interventions Questionnaire (APOI) at baseline. Confirmatory factor analyses (CFA) testing two- and three-factor solutions, Cronbach's α, item-total correlations, and correlations of WAI-I with APOI and ZUF-8.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Results suggested a two-factor solution for the WAI-I with very good model fit and evidence of factor independency, adequate internal consistency, and external validity for the complete scale and sub-scales. The WAI-I was found to be a reliable and valid instrument to capture alliance in guided Internet interventions.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes, 36 months (3-year follow-up)
studies.duration_additional_info: Early change patterns were assessed in the first 4 weeks of the intervention; long-term remission was tracked over 36 months. The study is a follow-up analysis of a previously conducted RCT.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: 409 individuals experiencing mild to moderate depressive symptoms using the web-based intervention deprexis; specific group sizes not detailed in the abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants used the web-based intervention 'deprexis' for mild to moderate depressive symptoms. The RCT involved a web-based intervention arm; control arm details not specified in the abstract.
studies.collected_data: Early change patterns in depressive symptoms during the first four weeks of intervention (used to identify three latent classes: early response after registration, early response after screening, and early deterioration); baseline variables; onset of remission over 36 months assessed via Stepwise Cox Proportional Hazard Multiple Regression.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Three latent classes were identified based on early change in the first four weeks: early response after registration, early response after screening, and early deterioration. Early change class was a significant predictor of remission over 36 months. Compared to early deterioration after screening, both early response after registration and after screening were associated with a higher likelihood of remission. In sensitivity and secondary analyses, only change class consistently emerged as a predictor of long-term outcome. Early improvement in depression symptoms predicted long-term outcome, and those showing early improvement had a higher likelihood of long-term remission.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6-month and 12-month follow-up
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants completed the PHQ-9 biweekly during the 12-week treatment period. Follow-up assessments were conducted at 6 and 12 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: All 483 participants were randomized to a 12-week Internet-based intervention (deprexis); specific control group size not reported in this abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to a 12-week Internet-based intervention (deprexis, NCT01636752). The abstract does not detail a separate control arm but references randomization, suggesting a comparator arm existed in the original trial.
studies.collected_data: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) administered biweekly during the 12-week treatment; dropout indicators; change-dropout patterns assessed at 6-month and 12-month follow-up; reliable change rates.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Four change-dropout patterns were identified using the Muthen-Roy model: C1 (N=198): low impairment, improvement, high dropout probability; C2 (N=32): high impairment, deterioration, high dropout probability; C3 (N=134): high impairment, improvement, low dropout probability — this group had the highest rate of reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up; C4 (N=119): high impairment, no change, low dropout probability. Both deterioration and rapid improvement (perceived 'good enough' dosage) were associated with dropout.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment/primary endpoint), and 6 months (follow-up). The intervention duration was 12 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis POI) vs. Care as Usual (CAU) control; subset of N=577 from the EVIDENT trial, split into Millennials (18-35 years) and Baby Boomers (50-65 years) subgroups.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: 12-week online psychological intervention (Deprexis); Arm 2: Care as usual (CAU). The study also compared two age subgroups (Millennials aged 18-35 and Baby Boomers aged 50-65) within these arms.
studies.collected_data: PHQ-9 (self-rated depression severity) at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment), and 6 months (follow-up); intervention usage frequency and duration; attitudes towards psychological online interventions (POIs).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Boomers used the intervention significantly more often than Millennials (d=0.45) and for a longer duration (d=0.46), and endorsed more positive attitudes towards POIs (d=0.14). There was no significant Age Group by Intervention Group interaction for change in PHQ-9. Post-assessment between-group effect sizes (intervention vs. CAU) for Millennials and Boomers were d=0.26 and d=0.39, respectively, and were stable at 6-month follow-up (d=0.37 and d=0.39). The POI was superior to CAU and comparably effective across both age groups.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The publication date is November 2019; no specific primary endpoint duration or follow-up timing is mentioned in the abstract.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Deprexis®) and control group (care as usual); total N=1013, specific group sizes not reported in the abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received the internet-based intervention Deprexis® in addition to any type of treatment; control arm received care as usual (any type of treatment permitted). Both groups were permitted to use any type of treatment.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) assessing nine depressive symptoms; overall depression severity; treatment condition; changes in all nine PHQ symptoms; network estimation including treatment condition and symptom changes.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Of nine depressive symptoms assessed with the PHQ, seven showed larger improvements in the Deprexis intervention condition relative to care as usual (effect sizes ranging from 0.15 to 0.31). No significant differences were found for the two remaining symptoms. Network analysis indicated that four of the seven symptom-specific effects were direct, while three were indirect (mediated by effects on other symptoms). Exploratory analyses showed the intervention was more effective in improving overall depression severity for participants with higher baseline scores on the four directly affected symptoms.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes (follow-up studies mentioned in MeSH terms)
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention period was 3 months; this study is a subgroup analysis of the EVIDENT trial.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received the Internet intervention 'deprexis'; control group received care as usual. Total N=1,004.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Internet intervention (deprexis, a depression-focused iCBT program); Arm 2: Control group (care as usual).
studies.collected_data: Attitudes toward Internet interventions (pre and post), depression symptoms (mild-to-moderate), usage time of the deprexis program, efficacy of the Internet intervention over a 3-month period. Attitude measures assessed at pre- and post-intervention.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Positive initial attitudes toward Internet interventions were associated with greater efficacy (η2p = .014) independent of usage time. A negative attitude (perceived lack of personal contact) was associated with reduced efficacy (η2p = .012). Users' attitudes changed during the trial, and both the magnitude and direction of attitude change were associated with the efficacy of the program over time (η2p = .030). Conclusions: Internet interventions may be most beneficial for individuals with positive attitudes toward them.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months (follow-up assessment at 6 months after baseline)
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment/primary endpoint), and 6 months (follow-up). The intervention itself was 12 weeks in duration.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Participants randomized to either a 12-week Internet intervention (Deprexis) adjunctive to usual care or care as usual alone (CAU); total N=1013 (specific group sizes not reported in abstract).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: 12-week Internet intervention (Deprexis) in addition to usual care. Arm 2: Care as usual (CAU) alone. Both arms included participants with mild to moderate depressive symptoms (PHQ-9 score 5-14).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-rated depression severity measured by the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment), and 6 months (follow-up). Minimally clinically important change in PHQ-9 was also tracked. The trial used linear mixed models for the main intention-to-treat analysis.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001 for the main effect of group). Post-assessment between-group effect size in favour of the intervention: d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13–0.64). Effect was stable at 6-month follow-up: d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06–0.69). Rate of participants experiencing at least minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%), with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5–10). The Internet intervention was superior to CAU alone in reducing mild to moderate depressive symptoms.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention was a 12-week minimally guided Internet intervention (Deprexis). Outcomes were assessed at baseline, post-treatment (12 weeks), and 6-month follow-up.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Augmented therapy group (Internet intervention + routine psychotherapy) n = 166; Standard psychotherapy group (routine psychotherapy only) n = 174
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (augmented therapy): routine outpatient psychotherapy plus adjunctive 12-week minimally guided Internet intervention (Deprexis) for mild to moderate depression. Arm 2 (standard psychotherapy): routine outpatient psychotherapy alone.
studies.collected_data: Symptoms of depression, therapeutic progress, mental health-related quality of life, physical health-related quality of life, rates of clinical improvement. Assessments conducted at baseline, post-treatment (12 weeks), and 6-month follow-up.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analyses showed combined treatment led to greater reduction in depression symptoms (effect size d = 0.32; p = .002), improved therapeutic progress (d = 0.36; p = .003), and higher mental health-related quality of life (d = 0.34; p = .004). No significant intervention effect on physical health-related quality of life. The same pattern was found at 6-month follow-up, and adjunctive treatment also resulted in increased rates of clinical improvement. Treatment success was independent of the therapeutic orientation of the combined face-to-face therapy.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, up to 12 months (one year) from baseline
studies.duration_additional_info: The 12-week internet intervention was followed by regular PHQ-9 assessments over twelve months total. The between-group effect decreased from d=0.36 at three months to d=0.13 at twelve months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care; Control group: care as usual alone. Total N=1013 randomized.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus care as usual. Arm 2: Care as usual alone (no internet intervention).
studies.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity assessed using PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) measured regularly over twelve months. Remission rates over time were tracked. Subgroup analysis by antidepressant medication use was also conducted.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Remission rates over time were significantly higher in the intervention group (Cox regression: HR 1.31; p=0.009). The intervention was more effective in the subgroup not taking antidepressant medication (Cox regression: HR 1.88; p<0.001). PHQ-9 change from baseline was greater in the intervention group (linear mixed model [LMM]: p<0.001), with the between-group effect gradually decreasing from d=0.36 at three months to d=0.13 at twelve months (LMM: group by time interaction: p<0.001). Conclusion: The internet intervention contributed to achieving remission in people with mild to moderate depressive symptoms, especially those not on antidepressant medication.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at 3 months (end of 12-week intervention) and again at 6 months follow-up.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care; Control: care-as-usual alone. Exact group sizes not reported in this abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): 12-week internet-based intervention (Deprexis) plus care-as-usual. Arm 2 (Control): care-as-usual alone.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-rated depression severity using Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) at 3 months and 6 months. Secondary measures: demographic and clinical parameters, attitudes towards internet interventions measured by the Attitudes towards Psychological Online Interventions Questionnaire (APOI). Recruitment source was also recorded and examined as a moderator.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Recruitment source was associated with very few demographic and clinical characteristics. Participants recruited through insurance companies were more likely to be employed compared to clinically recruited participants. Clinically recruited participants were as severely affected as those from other recruitment sources but more sceptical of internet interventions. The effectiveness of the intervention was not differentially associated with recruitment source (treatment by recruitment source interaction = 0.28, p = 0.84). The intervention was found to be effective across different recruitment sources including clinical settings.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months (T2)
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months (T1) post-randomization; additional follow-up at 6 months (T2).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (IG): n = 304 (lancivis + TAU); Control group (CG): n = 300 (TAU only)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the digital CBT-based program lancivis alongside treatment as usual (TAU); control group received TAU only.
studies.collected_data: Quality of life (QoL), cancer-related fatigue, distress symptoms, and fear of cancer progression. Assessments conducted at baseline, 3 months (T1), and 6 months (T2). Intent-to-treat (ITT) analyses used.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: ITT analyses revealed significant improvements in fatigue in the IG compared to the CG after 3 months (T1; Cohen's d = 0.29) and 6 months (T2; Cohen's d = 0.31). Participants in the IG reported significantly lower fear of cancer progression than the CG at T1 and T2, and lower levels of distress symptoms at T2. No between-group differences were found for health-related QoL. Results suggest that lancivis may present a beneficial addition to psycho-oncological care services.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: No additional follow-up beyond the 12-week observation period is described; assessments occur at baseline (U1), ~3 weeks (U2), ~6 weeks (U3), and ~12 weeks (U4).
studies.duration_additional_info: Data are collected under routine conditions over an observation period of 3 months (approximately 12 weeks), with four assessment time points: baseline (U1), ~3 weeks after U1 (U2), ~6 weeks after U1 (U3), and ~12 weeks after U1 (U4).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single observational group of ambulatory patients using deprexis®24 (with or without antidepressive comedication); no separate control group. Total participants: 104 (planned: 1800).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One observational arm: ambulatory patients with depressive mood or depression using the online psychotherapy program deprexis®24 under routine care, with or without antidepressive comedication.
studies.collected_data: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9), short version Montgomery Asberg Depression Scale (svMADRS), Clinical Global Impression-Severity/Improvement (CGI-S/CGI-I), Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) — all collected at all 4 visits (U1–U4); Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ-4) at U4; standardized questionnaires on compliance with the online psychotherapy program (U2–U4); questionnaire on internet usage behavior (U2–U4); standardized documentation of comedication (U1–U4); standardized questionnaire on safety-related incidents and adverse events under possible comedication (U2–U4).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline and at weeks 3, 6, 9, and 12. Patients received 12-week access to the SMI in addition to usual care.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single intervention group only (n = 104); no control group. Patients received 12-week access to deprexis in addition to usual care.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Uncontrolled observational study with one arm: patients with depressive disorder received access to the internet-based self-management intervention deprexis for 12 weeks in addition to their usual care (76.0% also took concomitant antidepressant medication). Optional guidance by the treating physician was available but only a minority (n = 7, 8.0%) received the guided version.
studies.collected_data: Clinician-rated short version of the Montgomery Åsberg Depression Scale (svMADRS); Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) self-rating for depressive symptoms; usage data (number of sessions, whether intervention was used at least once); receipt of guided vs. unguided intervention; concomitant antidepressant medication use. Assessments at baseline and weeks 3, 6, 9, and 12.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Most patients used the intervention at least once (n = 87, 83.6%); mean number of sessions among users was 18.05 (SD = 11.33). Only 7 patients (8.0%) received the guided version. svMADRS scores decreased significantly from 29.72 (SD = 10.03) at baseline to 15.73 (SD = 9.74) at week 12 (Cohen's d = 1.42, 95% CI 0.08–2.76). PHQ-9 scores decreased from 15.20 (SD = 5.03) to 8.77 (SD = 5.03) (Cohen's d = 1.29, 95% CI 0.60–1.97). The pre-post effect size was comparable to that reported in an RCT using the same intervention in patients with similar symptom severity.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 3 months after training
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention programme lasts 8 weeks (8 modules). Primary outcome is assessed immediately after training and again 3 months later. The study ran from July 2014 until May 2015 (completion date 26/05/2015).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (IG) received access to the online ELEVIDA fatigue management programme; Control group (CG) was a wait-list control group that received the programme after the study completed. Total target enrollment: 204.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention Group): Participants received a personal access code to the ELEVIDA online CBT-based fatigue training tool for 8 weeks. Arm 2 (Wait-list Control Group): Participants were informed about their option to enter the FATIMA programme after the study and received a brief explanation about the need for control groups in RCTs.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Fatigue measured by the Chalder Fatigue Scale (Cella & Chalder et al., 2010) assessed pre- and post-training immediately after and 3 months later. Secondary outcomes: 1. FSMC (Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions, Penner et al., 2009); 2. Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS, Zigmond & Snaith, 1983); 3. Hamburg Quality of Life Questionnaire in MS (HAQUAMS, Gold et al., 2001); 4. Multiple Sclerosis Neuropsychological Screening Questionnaire (MSNQ, Benedict et al., 2003). Exploratory: 5. Cognitive and Behavioural Responses to Symptoms Questionnaire (Skerett & Moss-Morris, 2006); 6. Brief Illness Perception Questionnaire (Broadbent et al., 2006); 7. Coping Self Efficacy Scale (CSES, Chesney et al., 2006); 8. Frenchay Activity Index (Turnbull et al., 2000); 9. Revised NEO Personality Inventory (Costa & McCrae, 1992). A subgroup also underwent clinical examination and MRI (DTI and resting state fMRI connectivity measures). Screening used HAQUAMS fatigue subscale (score ≥2) and FSMC total fatigue score ≥43 for inclusion.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at week 24 (12 weeks after primary endpoint)
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasted 12 weeks; outcomes were assessed at baseline, week 12 (post-intervention/primary endpoint), and week 24 (follow-up).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 139 (ELEVIDA online intervention) and C = 136 (waitlist control group)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 12-week self-guided interactive online fatigue management programme (ELEVIDA) based on CBT and related psychotherapeutic approaches (e.g., mindfulness), n=139, 82% female, mean age 40.8, median PDDS 3.0. Control arm: waitlist control group, n=136, 79% female, mean age 41.9, median PDDS 3.0.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Chalder Fatigue Scale (assessed at baseline, week 12, and week 24). Secondary outcomes not explicitly listed in the abstract. Patient Determined Disease Steps (PDDS) used as a measure of disability at baseline. Randomisation was 1:1 with no blocking or stratification.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12 (primary endpoint), the ELEVIDA group showed significantly greater reductions in Chalder Fatigue Scale scores compared to control (intention-to-treat: between-group mean difference 2.74 points; 95% CI 1.16 to 4.32; p=0.0007; effect size d=0.53). Effects were sustained at week 24 (intention-to-treat: between-group mean difference 2.19 points; 95% CI 0.57 to 3.82; p=0.0080). The trial provides evidence for the effectiveness of a self-guided internet-based intervention to reduce fatigue in MS.

studies.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH
studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; uncontrolled follow-up at 6 and 12 months after randomisation
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months (post-treatment) after randomisation. Uncontrolled follow-up assessments for both intervention and control groups at 6 and 12 months after randomisation. Control group participants receive free access to the online programme after the 3-month data collection period if they wish.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (IG): direct access to HelloBetter ratiopharm chronic pain digital therapy programme (7 units, ACT-based); Control group (CG): care-as-usual (CAU) waiting control group with unrestricted access to routine care. Total planned and enrolled: 360 participants.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Immediate access to the web-based ACT programme HelloBetter ratiopharm chronic pain (7 units), assessed at baseline, post (3 months), and follow-up at 6 and 12 months after randomisation. Arm 2 (Control): Care-as-usual (CAU) waiting control group with unrestricted access to all routine care options (GP, pain physicians, psychotherapy, medication, physical therapy, exercise therapy); assessed at baseline, 3, 6, and 12 months after randomisation. After 3-month data collection, control participants may receive free access to the programme on request.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain interference measured by the West Haven-Yale Multidimensional Pain Inventory (MPI) interference scale at post-treatment (3 months after randomisation). Secondary outcomes (collected at baseline, post/3 months, 6-month and 12-month follow-up unless noted): Emotional functioning (PHQ-9); Health-related quality of life (AQoL-8D); Pain intensity and interference (Brief Pain Inventory, BPI); Multidimensional pain interference including pain intensity, self-control, negative mood (MPI); Pain impairment in everyday life (Pain Disability Index, PDI); Anxiety (GAD-7); Pain processing (FESV); Pain acceptance (Chronic Pain Acceptance Questionnaire, CPAQ); Psychological flexibility (FAH-II / AAQ-II; Psychological Inflexibility in Pain Scale, PIPS); Pain-specific self-efficacy (Pain Self-Efficacy Questionnaire, PSEQ); Patient satisfaction (CSQ-8/ZUF-8, post, IG only); Negative effects (NEQ, post, IG only); Subjective global assessment of change (PGIC, post).

studies.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH
studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The study includes 3 visits per patient: baseline (V1), telephone visit after 4 weeks (V2, intervention group only), and a visit at month 3 (V3, all patients). The primary endpoint is assessed at month 3 (approximately 12 weeks). The online course (HelloBetter app) consists of seven weekly units of 45-60 minutes each.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: 1:1 randomization; total enrolled: 136 (planned: 250). Group 1 Intervention: axSpA patients prescribed the HelloBetter ACT-app in addition to standard therapy. Group 2 Control: axSpA patients receiving standard therapy without the HelloBetter app. Exact per-group sizes not reported.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients with axSpA who continue to suffer from pain despite adequate anti-inflammatory medication are prescribed the digital health application (DiGA) 'HelloBetter ratiopharm chronic pain®' (HelloBetter ACT-app), an interactive online behavioral therapy/ACT program consisting of seven weekly units of 45-60 minutes. Visits: V1 (baseline), V2 (telephone visit at 4 weeks to verify app activation), V3 (month 3). Arm 2 (Control): Patients with axSpA who continue to suffer from pain despite adequate anti-inflammatory medication receive standard therapy without the HelloBetter app. Visits: V1 (baseline) and V3 (month 3).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Change in the pain interference scale of the West Haven-Yale Multidimensional Pain Inventory (MPI-D) at month 3. Secondary outcomes and instruments collected at baseline (V1) and month 3 (V3): NRS global pain (0-10), back pain (BASDAI question 2), nocturnal back pain, inflammatory back pain (BASDAI questions 5 and 6), morning stiffness (BASDAI questions 5 and 6), West Haven-Yale Multidimensional Pain Inventory (MPI-D), neuropathic pain (painDetect questionnaire), PASS (patient acceptable symptom state), Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS), Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Score (ASDAS), Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Index (BASDAI), Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Functional Index (BASFI), Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index (BASMI), modified Short Questionnaire to Assess Health-enhancing Physical Activity (mSQUASH), ASAS Health Index (ASAS HI), Fibromyalgia Impact Questionnaire (FIQ), Short Form 36 (SF36), patient and physician global assessment, Patient Activation Measure (PAM12), patient global impression of change (V3 only). Intervention group only at V3: benefit from app use (yes/no), recommendation likelihood (NRS 0-10), Mobile Application Rating Scale (MARS), system usability questionnaire. V2 (intervention group only): app activation status (dichotomous yes/no). Demographic characteristics including educational and employment status, current pharmacological and non-pharmacological therapy.

studies.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH
studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months after randomisation (T2)
studies.duration_additional_info: T0 = before randomisation, T1 = 9 weeks after randomisation (post-intervention), T2 = 6 months after randomisation (follow-up). The intervention consists of 7 lessons to be processed weekly.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Three arms: (1) GET.ON chronic pain guided (with e-coach feedback emails after each lesson), (2) GET.ON chronic pain unguided (no e-coach feedback, self-directed), (3) waitlist control group. Total planned: 300, total enrolled: 302.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Guided internet-based ACT intervention (GET.ON chronic pain guided) - 7 weekly lessons with feedback emails from an e-coach after each lesson, plus optional SMS coach. Arm 2: Unguided internet-based ACT intervention (GET.ON chronic pain unguided) - same 7 weekly lessons but without e-coach feedback, plus optional SMS coach. Arm 3: Waitlist control group - receives no active intervention during the study period.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain interference measured by the MPI Interference Scale at T0 (pre), T1 (9 weeks post-randomisation), and T2 (6 months post-randomisation). Secondary outcomes: At screening - demographic variables (sex, age, education, social support), prior pain treatments, pain type, chronicity, comorbidities (self-report). At T0, T1, T2 - Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), Generalised Anxiety Disorder Screener (GAD-7), Pain intensity (11-point NRS), Quality of life: SF-12 and EQ-5D, Acceptance and Action Questionnaire-II (AAQ-II / FAH-II), Chronic Pain Acceptance Questionnaire (CPAQ-D), adapted TiC-P (costs associated with psychiatric illness). Treatment process (after lessons 2, 4, 6): FAH-II and CPAQ-D. At T1 and T2: Patient Global Impression of Change scale (PGIC). Active groups only at T1: intervention adherence (attrition rate), satisfaction with intervention (CSQ-8 / ZUF-8).

studies.company_provider: GET.ON Institut für Online Gesundheitstrainings GmbH
studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months and 12 months after baseline
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention consists of a 6-week online training (6 lessons, one per week), followed by a 7th lesson 4 weeks after the final lesson. Primary endpoint is assessed at 8 weeks (post-intervention), with follow-ups at 6 and 12 months after baseline.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (N=130): access to online program 'Get.On Diabetes and depressive symptoms'; Control group (N=130): psychoeducation materials about depression
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention, N=130): Six-week online training with 6 lessons (one per week, ~45 min each), guided self-help via weekly email contact, optional SMS support, and a 7th lesson 4 weeks after the final lesson. Arm 2 (Control, N=130): Receives psychoeducation materials on depression; after the last measurement point (12 months), the control group also gets access to the online training.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depressive symptoms measured by the Center for Epidemiological Studies Depression Scale (CES-D / Allgemeine Depressionsskala, ADS): Pre, Post (8 weeks), 6 and 12 months follow-up. Secondary outcomes: Demographic questionnaire (Pre); Worry (Penn State Worry Questionnaire, PSWQ, short version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Activation (Behavioural Activation Depression Scale, BA-DS, short version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Problem solving (Social Problem Solving Inventory, short version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Long-term blood sugar level (HbA1c): Pre, 6 and 12 months; Problem areas in diabetes (PAID, short version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Acceptance and Action Diabetes Questionnaire (AADQ, German version): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Diabetes Self-Management Questionnaire (DSMQ): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Depression (HADS-D): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Therapy expectation (Pathev): Pre (intervention group only); Negative effects of online health trainings (based on INEP): Post (intervention group only); Attitude toward seeking professional help (ATSPHS): Pre, Post; Satisfaction with the program (adaptation ZUF-8): Post (intervention group only); Quality of life (EuroQol): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Quality of life (SF-12): Pre, Post, 6 and 12 months; Costs associated with psychiatric illness (German adaptation of TiC-P): Pre, 6 and 12 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, secondary outcome on movement and health competence (PAHCO) assessed at 24 weeks
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention group uses the DiGA Kaia Health app for 12 weeks (10-30 minutes daily). One secondary outcome (PAHCO) is assessed at both 12 and 24 weeks, indicating a follow-up period extending to 24 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Planned 120 participants total; Intervention group receives DiGA Kaia Health app over 12 weeks; Control group receives standard prevention for back pain. Exact group sizes not specified.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Subjects receive the DiGA Kaia Health app over 12 weeks, with exercises performed daily for 10-30 minutes. Arm 2 (Control): Standard prevention for back pain.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes (at 12 weeks): Physical function (IPAQ, HAQ); Quality of life (SF-36); Sleep behavior (PSQI); Depressive moods (BDI). Secondary outcomes: Movement and health competence (PAHCO) at 12 and 24 weeks; Gait pattern via sensor-based gait analysis at 12 weeks; Mobility (BASMI) at 12 weeks; Compliance with DiGA Kaia use (Compliance Questionnaire).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed at day 90 (approximately 13 weeks) from baseline. Secondary outcomes were also assessed at 30 and 60 days. The study was prematurely terminated on 20.03.2023 by the sponsor due to: (1) recruitment challenges, (2) 'Kaia Back Pain' having already achieved permanent DiGA listing on 3 February 2023, and (3) ethical concerns about withholding a proven effective therapy from the control group.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Two parallel arms: Intervention group uses Kaia Back Pain app in addition to usual care for 90 days; Control group receives usual care only (with access to Kaia Back Pain app at end of study). Total enrolled: 87 participants (planned 210).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Control): Usual care only, with access to Kaia Back Pain app provided at end of study. Arm 2 (Intervention): Usual care plus use of the Kaia Back Pain smartphone app for 90 days.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Mean change in Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) score from baseline to day 90. Secondary outcomes: (1) Percentage of patients with ≥30% reduction in ODI from baseline to study end; (2) Health-related Quality of Life via Short Form Health Survey 12 (SF-12); (3) Pain intensity on an 11-point NRS (average pain in previous week) from baseline to study end; (4) Depression, anxiety, and stress via Depression-Anxiety-Stress-Scale (DASS); (5) Percentage of partial responders (average pain ≤2 on 11-point NRS); (6) Percentage of complete responders (average pain of 0 on 11-point NRS); (7) Physical function via ODI version 2.1b at 30 and 60 days; (8) Pain intensity on 11-point NRS at 30 and 60 days.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 12 and 24 weeks
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint (pain index on NRS) is assessed at enrollment and at 6-, 12-, and 24-week follow-up. The total trial duration is 6 months. Control group patients receive standard physical therapy once a week for 6 weeks, then gain access to the kaia app from week 12 until trial end.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: kaia LBP app used self-administered on own mobile device throughout the 6-month trial period (at least 4 times per week). Control group: standard physical therapy once a week for 6 sessions plus weekly online education links; after week 12, control group also receives access to kaia app until trial end. Total N = 101.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Treatment with kaia back pain app for the entire 6-month study duration, self-administered by patients on their own smartphone/tablet, encouraged to use app at least four times a week. Arm 2 (Control): Standard physiotherapy at an affiliated center once a week for 6 sessions total, plus weekly email with links to online LBP education resources (6 links total); after week 12, control group patients are granted access to the kaia app until trial end at 6 months.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain index calculated as the mean of current pain, maximum pain, and average pain over the last 4 weeks, each measured on an 11-point Numeric Rating Scale (NRS; 0 = no pain, 10 = unbearable pain), assessed at enrollment and at 6-, 12-, and 24-week follow-up. Secondary outcomes: Functional capability (Hannover Functional Ability Questionnaire for back pain, HFAQ/FFbH-R), health-related quality of life (VR-12), Graded Chronic Pain Scale (GCPS/von Korff questionnaire), and pain self-efficacy (FESS/pain self-efficacy questionnaire), all assessed at enrollment and at 6-, 12-, and 24-week follow-up.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 12 months post-baseline
studies.duration_additional_info: Measurements collected at pre-treatment (T0), 3 months (T1), 6 months (T2), 9 months (T3), and 12 months (T4). The trial was terminated early due to the COVID-19 pandemic; participant recruitment was stopped. Primary completion date was 30 September 2021 and full completion date was 30 April 2022.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Optimune): immediate 12-month access to web-based CBT program Optimune plus care-as-usual; Control group (Care-as-Usual/waitlist): care-as-usual with access to Optimune delayed by 6 months. Exact group sizes not reported.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental - Optimune): Participants receive immediate 12-month access to the web-based psychological intervention Optimune (CBT-based, including sleep/stress management, mindfulness, dietary and physical activity advice) plus care-as-usual. Arm 2 (Active Comparator - Care-as-Usual): Participants continue with any treatment they require (care-as-usual) and receive access to Optimune only after a 6-month delay (waitlist control).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes (change from baseline to 6 months, also at 12 months): plasma CRP concentration, circulating IL-6, circulating TNF-α, stimulated IL-6 (LPS-stimulated PBMCs), stimulated TNF-α (LPS-stimulated PBMCs). Secondary outcomes: plasma cytokine concentrations (IL-1β, IFN-α, IFN-γ, MCP-1, IL-8, IL-10, IL-12p70, IL-17A, IL-18, IL-23, IL-33 via CBA); secreted cytokines after PMA stimulation of PBMCs; secreted cytokines after LPS stimulation of PBMCs; circulating lymphocytes, monocytes, granulocytes (TruCount Flow Cytometry); T and NK cell subset phenotyping (11-color Flow Cytometry); cortisol awakening response (CAR) from saliva; Brief Fatigue Inventory (BFI-9); Impact of Event Scale-Revised (IES-R); Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9); Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD-7); Fear of Progression questionnaire (PA-F12); program usefulness questionnaire; Inventory for the Assessment of Negative Effects of Psychotherapy (INEP). Other outcomes: recurrence of breast cancer, frequency of common cold/virus flu, frequency of unscheduled medical encounters.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months post-baseline (and for some secondary outcomes at 9 and 12 months post-baseline)
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 3 months post-baseline (T1); additional assessments at 6 months post-baseline (T2). Some secondary outcomes (e.g., IES-R) also assessed at 9 and 12 months post-baseline. The intervention (Optimune) is accessible for 365 days after registration. The control group receives access to Optimune after a delay of 6 months (wait list). Primary completion date: June 2020; study completion date: September 2020.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Optimune): immediate 12-month access to web-based intervention plus care as usual; Control group (Care-as-Usual/wait list): care as usual with access to Optimune delayed by 6 months post-baseline. Total N = 360.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental - Optimune): Participants receive immediate 12-month access to Optimune, a web-based CBT-based psychological intervention including techniques targeting depression, anxiety, fatigue, sleep, stress management (mindfulness), and lifestyle optimization (diet and physical activity), plus care as usual. Arm 2 (Active Comparator - Care-as-Usual): Participants continue with any treatment they require (care as usual) and receive access to Optimune after a 6-month delay (wait list control).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes (assessed at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months post-baseline): Quality of Life (WHOQOL-BREF, 26-item, 5-point scale, range 26-130); Physical Activity (IPAQ-short, 7-item questionnaire, min/week and sitting time); Diet Quality (Food Quality Questionnaire/FQQ, 10-item, 5-point scale, range 0-39). Secondary outcomes: Cancer-related fatigue (Brief Fatigue Inventory/BFI-9, 9-item, 11-point scale, range 0-90; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Cancer-related emotional stress (IES-R, 22-item, 5-point scale, range 0-88; assessed at baseline, 3, 6, 9, and 12 months); Depression (PHQ-9, range 0-27; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Anxiety (GAD-7, range 0-21; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Fear of progression (PA-F12, 12-item, 5-point Likert scale, range 12-60; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Insomnia (ISI/Insomnia Severity Index, range 0-28; assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months); Usefulness of the program (questionnaire; assessed at 3 and 6 months); Negative effects (INEP/Inventory for the Assessment of Negative Effects of Psychotherapy; assessed at 3 and 6 months). Other outcomes (assessed at baseline, 3 and 6 months): Recurrence of breast cancer (questionnaire); Frequency of common cold or virus flu (questionnaire); Frequency of unscheduled medical encounters (questionnaire); Body Mass Index (height and weight via questionnaire); Psychotherapy initiation during trial (questionnaire).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes, follow-up surveys ongoing after primary endpoint at 3 months
studies.duration_additional_info: The online program optimune is used for 3 months in addition to usual treatment. Intervention group has access to optimune for 365 days. The recruitment target (680 participants) was not achieved within the designated timeframe; follow-up surveys are still ongoing.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: access to online program optimune for 365 days in addition to routine treatment (care as usual).
