Control group: routine treatment (care as usual) only, with access to optimune for 365 days after study completion. Total enrolled: 405 (planned: 680).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Access to the internet-based self-help program optimune for 365 days in addition to routine treatment (care as usual). Arm 2 (Control): Routine treatment (care as usual) only during the study; access to optimune for 365 days granted after study completion.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Quality of life measured by the total score of the Assessment of Quality of Life (AQoL-8D) at T1 (3 months after baseline). Secondary outcomes: General distress (Depression-Anxiety-Stress-Scales, DASS-21); Work and social ability (Work and Social Adjustment Scale, WSAS); Fatigue (Brief Fatigue Inventory, BFI); Responder rate of AQoL-8D; Subscales and dimensions of AQoL-8D.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; control group received Optimune after a 3-month delay, and intervention group had 12-month access to Optimune, implying follow-up beyond the 3-month primary endpoint
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoints assessed at 3 months post-randomization. Participants in the intervention group received 12-month access to Optimune. The control group received Optimune after a delay of 3 months (waitlist control).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (n = 181): care as usual (CAU) plus immediate 12-month access to Optimune; Control group (n = 182): CAU plus Optimune after a 3-month delay (waitlist control)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): CAU + immediate access to Optimune (CBT-based Internet intervention) for 12 months. Arm 2 (Control): CAU only for 3 months, then access to Optimune after the 3-month delay.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Quality of life (QoL), physical activity, and dietary habits assessed at 3 months. Measures included patient-reported outcomes via questionnaires on QoL, physical exercise, and dietary habits. Intention-to-treat (ITT) analyses were conducted.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: ITT analyses at 3 months showed significant effects on QoL (d = 0.27, 95% CI: 0.07–0.48) and dietary habits (d = 0.36, 95% CI: 0.15–0.56). The effect on physical exercise was not statistically significant (d = 0.30; 95% CI: 0.10–0.51). The authors concluded that Optimune is effective in improving quality of life and dietary habits in breast cancer survivors.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes – the control group received Optimune after a 3-month delay, implying continued observation; intervention group had 12-month access to Optimune
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoints were assessed at 3 months (approximately 12 weeks). Participants in the intervention group received 12-month access to Optimune immediately after randomization. The control group received care as usual and then gained access to Optimune after a 3-month delay.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (n = 181): care as usual (CAU) plus immediate 12-month access to Optimune; Control group (n = 182): CAU plus access to Optimune after a 3-month delay
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): CAU + immediate access to Optimune (CBT-based Internet intervention) for 12 months. Arm 2 (Control): CAU only for the first 3 months, then access to Optimune after the 3-month delay (waitlist-controlled design).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: quality of life (QoL) assessed with WHOQOL-BREF, physical activity assessed with IPAQ-SF (International Physical Activity Questionnaire – Short Form), and dietary habits – all measured at 3 months. Secondary outcomes/measures included: fatigue (Brief Fatigue Inventory), depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), fear of progression (Herschbach et al. instrument), insomnia (Insomnia Severity Index), post-traumatic stress (Impact of Event Scale – Revised), net promoter score, negative effects of psychotherapy (INEP inventory), and immune/inflammatory biomarkers (referenced in background). Adherence and usage logs of the Optimune platform were also tracked.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat (ITT) analyses at 3 months revealed: significant effect on quality of life (d = 0.27, 95% CI: 0.07–0.48); significant effect on dietary habits (d = 0.36, 95% CI: 0.15–0.56); non-significant effect on physical exercise (d = 0.30, 95% CI: 0.10–0.51). The authors concluded that Optimune was effective in improving quality of life and dietary habits in breast cancer survivors.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary outcome (KOOS) was collected at enrollment, after 3 weeks, 1 day pre-surgery, 2 weeks post-surgery, 6 weeks post-surgery, 10 weeks post-surgery, and 14 weeks post-surgery. The study covered both preoperative and postoperative periods.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Arm 1 (Intervention): Standard of care + Use of the Orthopy App; Arm 2 (Control): Standard of care only. Total planned and enrolled: 80 participants.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients received standard of care plus access to the Orthopy smartphone application, which provided individually tailored training content and exercises as an add-on to conventional therapy. Arm 2 (Control): Patients received standard of care only (conventional treatment without the app).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: KOOS (Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score) – collected at enrollment, after 3 weeks, 1 day pre-surgery, 2 weeks post-surgery, 6 weeks post-surgery, 10 weeks post-surgery, and 14 weeks post-surgery. Secondary outcomes: Kinesiophobia (Tampa Scale); subjective pain level (numeric VAS); subjective function (numeric VAS); subjective patient satisfaction (numeric VAS); physical therapy/lymphatic drainage needed (yes/no); ability to work (yes/no); patient-relevant structural and procedural improvements (pSVV). Exploratory medical endpoints: thigh circumference, lower leg circumference, patellar center; angular measurements (range of motion); stability test (anteroposterior stability of the knee joint); coordination, quick strength, and agility (end of study only). Exploratory endpoints on app use (intervention group only) via custom-developed questions.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The clinical trial showed positive outcomes on the KOOS Pain and Symptoms subscales in the interventional group compared to the standard of care group. A statistically significant pain reduction was seen at t1 (p = 0.0005), t2 (p = 0.0068), and t4 (approximately 6 weeks after surgery; p = 0.01). A significant reduction of symptoms was found at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036), and t6 (p = 0.0377). These findings were further supported by significant benefit on the pain NRS at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5, and t6. The KOOS subscales quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009), and Sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The total intervention period is at least 15 weeks, consisting of a preoperative phase of at least 1 week and a postoperative phase of 14 weeks. The primary endpoint (VAS pain change) is assessed at 14 weeks postoperatively (T6). Data collection occurs at T0 (baseline, 7–30 days pre-op), T1 (1 day pre-op), T2 (3 days post-op), T3 (2 weeks post-op), T4 (6 weeks post-op), T5 (10 weeks post-op), and T6 (14 weeks post-op).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (I = 164): Orthopy app (video-based home training and educational content, used 3x/week) in addition to usual care. Control group (C = 164): Usual care alone (routine medical treatment and 6 sessions of physiotherapy).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Postoperative use of the Orthopy smartphone app in addition to standard orthopedic therapy (usual care) in patients with meniscus injuries; app used 3 times per week. Arm 2 (Control): Standard therapy in Germany (regular physical therapy / usual care), essentially 6 sessions of physiotherapy.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: VAS Pain (subjective pain intensity) assessed at T0, T1, T2, T3, T4, T5, T6 via patient questionnaire. Secondary outcomes: Health-related quality of life and functional limitations (WOMET); subjective pain intensity (KOOS subscale pain); subjective perception of symptoms and functional deficits (KOOS subscale symptoms and function); objective assessment of proprioceptive abilities (angle reproduction/position sense test) assessed at on-site visits T4 and T6; range of motion (ROM via goniometer) assessed at T4 and T6. All patient-reported outcomes collected digitally via online questionnaires at T1, T2, T3, T5 and in-person at T0, T4, T6.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The total intervention period is at least 18 weeks, consisting of a preoperative phase of at least 4 weeks and a postoperative phase of 14 weeks. The primary endpoint (KOOS Symptoms score change) is assessed at 14 weeks postoperatively (T6). Data collection occurs at T0 (28–36 days before surgery), T1 (1 day before surgery), T2 (3 days post-op), T3 (2 weeks post-op), T4 (6 weeks post-op), T5 (10 weeks post-op), and T6 (14 weeks post-op).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (n=114): Orthopy app (video-based home training and educational content) in addition to usual care; Control group (n=114): Usual care alone (18 sessions of physical therapy and 6 sessions of manual lymphatic drainage).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients use the Orthopy smartphone app 3 times per week in addition to standard care (usual care) for at least 18 weeks (pre- and postoperative phases). Arm 2 (Control): Patients receive usual care in Germany, consisting of routine medical treatment and physiotherapy prescriptions (18 sessions of physical therapy and 6 sessions of manual lymphatic drainage).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: KOOS subscale – Symptoms and Function (change over 14 weeks postoperatively). Secondary outcomes: KOOS-ACL subscale – Function; VAS Pain (subjective pain intensity); LERAS-13 (everyday functional recovery); KOOS subscale – QoL (knee-related quality of life); Angle Reproduction Test (proprioceptive ability of the knee joint); Range of Motion via goniometer. Assessment time points: T0 (≥28 days before surgery), T1 (1 day before surgery), T2 (3 days post-op, online), T3 (2 weeks post-op, online), T4 (6 weeks post-op, on-site), T5 (10 weeks post-op, online), T6 (14 weeks post-op, on-site). Patient-reported outcomes collected digitally; objective functional endpoints (angle reproduction test, range of motion) collected by study staff at on-site visits (T0, T1, T4, T6). Primary analysis: Intention-to-Treat (ITT) using Mixed Model for Repeated Measures (MMRM). Randomization stratified by study center, gender, and preoperative functional status.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is at least 16 weeks, consisting of a preoperative phase of at least 2 weeks and a postoperative phase of 14 weeks. Data is collected at T0 (baseline), T1 (preoperative, end of preoperative phase), T2 (2 weeks post-op), T3 (6 weeks post-op), T4 (10 weeks post-op), and T5 (end of intervention period, 14 weeks post-op). Inclusion occurs at least 2 weeks before surgery.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: pre- and postoperative use of Orthopy smartphone app in addition to standard orthopedic therapy. Control group: standard therapy in Germany (regular physiotherapy). Planned 200 patients per cohort (400 total); 419 actually enrolled. Two cohorts: ACL rupture (with or without meniscal damage) and meniscal damage alone, each split 1:1 into intervention and control.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention group): Pre- and postoperative use of the Orthopy smartphone app in addition to the standard orthopedic therapy standard for anterior cruciate ligament tears (with or without meniscal damage) or meniscal damage alone. Arm 2 (Control group): Standard therapy in Germany (regular physiotherapy / standard of care), including physiotherapy per therapeutic products catalog, with possible supplementary analgesics, lymphatic drainage, orthoses, and motion splints.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: KOOS (Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score) Subscale Symptoms and functional limitations. Secondary outcomes: a) KOOS Pain subscale; b) Subjective pain level (numerical VAS); c) Subjective function (numerical VAS); d) KOOS Activities of Daily Living subscale; e) KOOS Quality of Life subscale; f) KOOS Sport subscale. Exploratory endpoints (collected by study personnel): thigh circumference, range of motion (extension, neutral position, and flexion via angle measure). All primary and secondary endpoints collected digitally at T0, T1, T2, T3, T4, and T5. Prescribed and utilized standard therapy services also recorded.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: No follow-up beyond 12 weeks is mentioned; assessments occur at 1, 3, 6, and 12 weeks postoperatively.
studies.duration_additional_info: The study duration per participant is approximately 12 weeks, corresponding to the usual follow-up period after ACL surgery. Secondary outcomes are assessed at 1, 3, 6, and 12 weeks postoperatively. Cryotherapy starts on the day of surgery and is applied 5 times within the first 48 hours; shockwave therapy is administered once weekly for 5 weeks starting on postoperative day 2. The Orthopy app is used from the third postoperative week onward.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: 4 groups of 15 participants each: Group 1 (combination rESWT + cryotherapy, n=15), Group 2 (rESWT alone, n=15), Group 3 (cryotherapy alone, n=15), Group 4 (control: standardized physiotherapy only, n=15).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Postoperative application of rESWT and hyperbaric cryotherapy (HCT) combined with standardized physiotherapy. Arm 2: Postoperative application of rESWT alone with standardized physiotherapy. Arm 3: Postoperative application of HCT alone with standardized physiotherapy. Arm 4: Control group receiving standardized physiotherapy only. All arms also use the Orthopy app from week 3 postoperatively.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: IKDC score at 12 weeks postoperatively. Secondary outcomes: Tegner activity scores, Lysholm scores, VAS pain scores, swelling status, and range of motion at 1, 3, 6, and 12 weeks postoperatively; IKDC score at 1, 3, and 6 weeks postoperatively; patient satisfaction; rehabilitation progress documented via the Orthopy mobile application.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: No follow-up beyond the primary endpoint (t6 at 14 weeks postsurgery was the final assessment)
studies.duration_additional_info: The study included a presurgery period of 2-6 weeks and a postsurgery period of 14 weeks. Interim assessments were conducted at t1 (if more than 3 weeks before surgery), t3, t4, and t5 (postsurgery). Clinical assessments were conducted at t2 (presurgery) and t6 (14 weeks postsurgery), which was the primary endpoint.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 40 (app-assisted therapy plus standard of care) and C = 40 (standard of care alone); 1:1 randomization
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Orthopy digital health app combined with standard of care (conventional orthopaedic and physiotherapeutic measures). Arm 2 (Control): Isolated standard of care (conventional orthopaedic and physiotherapeutic measures only).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS) including subscales for Pain, Symptoms, Quality of Life, Activities of Daily Living, and Sport. Secondary outcomes: Subjective pain levels via Numeric Rating Scale (NRS) and function levels via NRS. Assessments collected at baseline (t0), t1 (if >3 weeks before surgery), t2 (presurgery clinical assessment), t3, t4, t5 (postsurgery online questionnaires), and t6 (14 weeks postsurgery clinical assessment).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed statistically significant improvements in KOOS Pain subscale at t1 (p=0.0005), t2 (p=0.0068), and t4 (~6 weeks postsurgery; p=0.01). Significant reduction in KOOS Symptoms at t1 (p=0.0001), t2 (p=0.0036), and t6 (p=0.0377). Pain NRS showed significant benefit at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5, and t6. KOOS subscales Quality of Life (p=0.0066), Activities of Daily Living (p=0.0009), and Sport (p=0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery. Conclusion: Combining the Orthopy app with standard of care significantly improves prehabilitation and rehabilitation outcomes compared to standard of care alone.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoints are assessed directly after the 12-week intervention phase. The study is monocentric, conducted at the Department of Sports Medicine in Tübingen, Germany. Recruitment status is listed as complete with follow-up complete.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 12-week app-guided exercise intervention; Control group: no intervention. Randomized 1:1. Total enrolled: 195.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention group): 12-week app-guided home exercise program including progressive video-supported exercises for mobility, flexibility, strength and balance, with movement quality and quantity controlled by accelerometers providing biofeedback. Arm 2 (Control group): no study intervention for 12 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Knee Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS) Subscale Pain and KOOS Subscale Activities of Daily Living (ADL), assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: health-related quality of life (VR12), fear of movement (kinesiophobia), other KOOS subscales (symptoms, sport/recreation, quality of life), Patient's Global Assessment, leg strength and strength endurance (30-Second Chair Test). During intervention: perceived exertion during exercise, exercise adherence, safety aspects (pain, unexpected events/side effects), usability and patient satisfaction.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes assessed at 3 months (12 weeks). The intervention consisted of 3 exercise sessions per week over 12 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 98 (re.flex + usual care) and C = 97 (usual care only); primary analysis included 194 participants
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 12-week self-directed app-based and sensor-assisted exercise program (re.flex) with 3 sessions per week in addition to usual care. Control arm: usual care only.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: OA-specific knee pain and physical function measured using the Knee Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS) subscales for pain and activities of daily living (ADL) (0-100 scale) at 3 months. Secondary outcomes: adherence (percentage of scheduled exercise sessions completed) and safety (adverse events). Statistical methods: multiple imputation for missing data, baseline-adjusted ANCOVA, Bonferroni correction (alpha=0.025).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Pain reduction was significantly larger in re.flex than in usual care: adjusted mean difference of 4.8 points (95% CI 0.7–8.9; P=.02; Cohen d=0.35). Improvement in physical function was not statistically significant (β=3.9 points, 95% CI 0.0–7.9; P=.049, did not meet Bonferroni-corrected threshold of .025). Adherence: 77% of scheduled sessions completed (2705/3528). Twelve adverse events were linked to re.flex, none serious. Conclusion: re.flex demonstrated superiority over usual care for pain reduction.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention included a 12-week home exercise program with 3 sessions per week. Outcomes were assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks. No follow-up beyond the 12-week primary endpoint is mentioned.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (IG): n=30 (split into IG A: app-based training, and IG AB: app-based training + supportive knee brace); Control group (CG): n=31
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Randomization was 1:1:2 into three arms: (1) IG A – app-based exercise training only; (2) IG AB – app-based exercise training plus a supportive knee brace; (3) CG – control group receiving no study intervention but allowed usual care. The intervention arms used a 12-week home exercise program (3 sessions/week) guided via app and monitored with 2 accelerometers placed above and below the affected knee.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS) pain subscale. Secondary outcomes: all other KOOS subscales (physical function, symptoms, sport and recreation activities, knee-related quality of life); general health-related quality of life (Veterans RAND 12-item Health Survey [VR-12]); psychological measures (e.g., exercise self-efficacy); performance measures (strength and postural control); adherence monitoring (percentage of scheduled exercise sessions completed); safety monitoring. All outcomes assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: 61 participants were analyzed (IG: n=30; CG: n=31). Statistically significant effects in favor of the IG were found for: pain reduction (P<.001; ES=0.76), improvements in physical function (P<.001; ES=0.64), improvements in symptoms (P=.01; ES=0.53), improvements in sport and recreation activities (P=.02; ES=0.47), improvements in knee-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.76), and improvements in the physical component of general health-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.74). Mean differences ranged from 6.0 to 13.2 points (scale range 0–100). Effect sizes indicated small to medium effects. No significant effects were found for psychological or performance measures. Adherence was 92.5% (899/972 scheduled sessions completed).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes measured at individual starting point (T0) and after 3 months (T1). Recruitment status listed as COMPLETE_FOLLOW_UP_COMPLETE.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Planned N=164; actual total participants=282. Intervention group: Rehappy app users. Control group: Waiting control group (receives no treatment).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Patients use the Rehappy app in daily living, which provides feedback on physical activity (via activity tracker), individually tailored motivational messages, medical background information, hints on activities of daily living, goal setting, and checklists. Arm 2 (Control): Waiting control group receiving no treatment.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes collected at T0 (individual start) and T1 (after 3 months): post-stroke depression (PHQ-2), activity and participation (IMET), therapy adherence, medication adherence (MMAS-8), health literacy (HLS-EU-Q16), self-efficacy (ASKU), health-related quality of life (SOEP-SF12v2), and rate of recurrent strokes. Additional data collected: physical and mental health, activity and mobility, medication compliance, age, gender, and health behavior.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, 24 weeks post-randomization
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes collected at baseline, 12, and 24 weeks post-randomization. Group 1 receives dCBT-I for 3 months (alongside CAU); Group 2 is a waitlist control receiving dCBT-I after 6 months. The primary endpoint is at 12 weeks; the 24-week assessment serves as a follow-up.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Group 1 (intervention): access to somnio (dCBT-I) plus care-as-usual (CAU); Group 2 (waitlist control): CAU only, with access to somnio after 6 months. 1:1 randomization; total enrolled = 137 (planned = 122).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Receives access to somnio (mementor DE GmbH), a digital health application delivering cognitive-behavioral psychotherapy for insomnia (dCBT-I), in addition to care-as-usual (CAU), for 24 weeks. Arm 2: Waitlist control group that continues CAU only and receives access to somnio after 24 weeks (6 months).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depression severity measured with the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) at 12 weeks post-randomization. Secondary outcomes: Insomnia severity (ISI; 12 & 24 weeks), Daytime sleepiness (ESS; 12 & 24 weeks), Fatigue (FSS; 12 & 24 weeks), Quality of life (WHOQOL-BREF; 12 & 24 weeks), Emotional state (PANAS; 12 & 24 weeks), Sleep continuity (sleep diary; 12 weeks), Self-efficacy (PCS; 12 & 24 weeks), Emotion regulation (RSQ; 12 & 24 weeks). Inclusion screening also used Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) and Insomnia Severity Index (ISI > 10).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, 16 weeks post-randomization (i.e., 8 weeks after the primary endpoint)
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasted 8 weeks. Outcomes were collected at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomization. Both groups wore an activity tracker and used a digital app for 8 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Group 1): digital CBT-I via somnio app (n = 29); Control (Group 2): digital sleep monitoring via malio app/sleep diary only (n = 27)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Group 1 received access to somnio (mementor DE GmbH), a digital health application delivering cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) plus a digital sleep diary, for 8 weeks. Group 2 received access to malio (mementor DE GmbH), a digital health application enabling daily sleep tracking via a digital sleep diary only, for 8 weeks. Both groups also wore an actigraphy/activity tracker for the 8-week intervention period.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomization. Secondary outcomes: Sleep continuity measured via application-integrated sleep diary and actigraphy (continuously for 8 weeks); Dysfunctional Beliefs and Attitudes about Sleep (DBAS) at 8 & 16 weeks; Pre-Sleep Arousal Scale (PSAS) at 8 & 16 weeks; Daytime well-being (WHO-5) at 8 & 16 weeks; Quality of life (WHOQOL-BREF) at 8 & 16 weeks; Symptoms of anxiety and depression (HADS) at 8 & 16 weeks. Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) suicide item used at screening.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: 56 participants (48 females, mean age 45.55 ± 13.70 years) were randomized: dCBT-I (n=29) vs. digital sleep monitoring (n=27). At 8 weeks post-randomization, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions in insomnia severity in the dCBT-I arm relative to digital sleep monitoring (-3.70 points on ISI; p ≤ 0.003). At 16 weeks post-randomization, reductions were -2.97 points on ISI (p ≤ 0.003). Treatment effects in favour of dCBT-I were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables, indicating sleep improved throughout the 8-week intervention period. Linear mixed models were used to assess between-group differences. The study concluded that dCBT-I achieves clinical improvements for patients with insomnia, supporting the specific effects of CBT-I.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months after study inclusion
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments take place prior to the intervention, at 10 weeks after study inclusion (primary endpoint), and at 6 months after study inclusion (follow-up).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: CBT-I delivered via the 'Somnio' app in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic. Control: Sleep diary in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic. Specific group sizes not reported.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) delivered via the 'Somnio' app as add-on to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic. Arm 2: Filling in a sleep diary in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic (active control).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Posttraumatic symptoms assessed via PCL-5 (Weathers et al., 2013) and PTCI (Foa et al., 1999); sleep quality assessed via sleep diary and PSQI (Buysse et al., 1989). Secondary outcomes: Anxiety (GAD-7, Spitzer et al., 2006), depression (QIDS-SR16, Rush et al., 2003), and quality of life (WHODAS 2.0). All measures collected at baseline (pre-intervention), 10 weeks post-inclusion, and 6 months post-inclusion.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 24 weeks (6 months follow-up)
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention (somnio dCBT-I) consists of 11 modules completed over 8 weeks. Primary outcome is assessed at baseline, 8 weeks (post), and 24 weeks (6-month follow-up). Secondary outcomes are also assessed at baseline, 8 and 24 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Digital CBT-I (dCBT-I) via somnio programme (11 modules over 8 weeks); Waitlist control group. Total enrolled: 207 (planned: 204).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Digital cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) using the somnio therapy programme, consisting of 11 modules (approx. 20 minutes each on average, approx. 3.5 hours total) completed over 8 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Waitlist control group – no digital therapy but can continue all usual therapy measures.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at baseline, 8 weeks, and 24 weeks. Secondary outcomes (all at baseline, 8 and 24 weeks unless noted): Pain symptoms (German Pain Questionnaire [DSF], Multidimensional Pain Inventory [MPI]); Daytime sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale [ESS]); Fatigue (Fatigue Severity Scale [FSS]); Dysfunctional attitudes to sleep (Dysfunctional Beliefs and Attitudes about Sleep [DBAS]); Well-being (WHO-5 Well-Being Index); Quality of life (WHOQOL-BREF); Psychological need satisfaction (Basic Psychological Need Satisfaction and Frustration Scale [BPNSFS]); Dreams and nightmares (Mannheim Dream Questionnaire [MADRE], Nightmare Distress Questionnaire [NDQ]); Depression and anxiety (General Depression Scale [ADS-K], State-Trait Anxiety Inventory [STAI]); Motivational schemas (FAMOS at baseline only; Incongruence Questionnaire [K-INK] at baseline, 8 and 24 weeks).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 6 months and 12 months post-treatment (intervention group only)
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention consists of 11 modules completed over 6-8 weeks. The primary endpoint is assessed at 8 weeks post-randomization. Follow-ups at 6 and 12 months were conducted for the intervention group only.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (dCBT-I + care-as-usual): n = 118; Waitlist control group (+ care-as-usual): n = 120
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Unguided digital cognitive-behavior therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) via somnio (www.somn.io), consisting of 11 modules (~20 minutes each, ~3.5 hours total) completed over 6-8 weeks, plus care-as-usual. Arm 2 (Control): Waiting list control group plus care-as-usual.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: ISI (Insomnia Severity Index) assessed at pre, post (8 weeks), follow-up 6 months, follow-up 12 months. Secondary outcomes: DBAS-16 (dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep); FSS (Fatigue Severity Scale); ESS (Epworth Sleepiness Scale); CES-D/ADS-K (depression scale, short version); STAI-T (State-Trait Anxiety Inventory, trait version); WHOQOL-BREF (WHO Quality of Life Questionnaire, brief version); WHO-5 (WHO-Five Well-Being Index); MADRE (Mannheim Dream Questionnaire); DTI (Düsseldorf Dream Inventory); NDQ (Nightmare Distress Questionnaire); CAN (Cognitive Appraisal of Nightmares); nightmare frequency. All secondary outcomes assessed at pre, post, follow-up 6 months, and follow-up 12 months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: N = 238 participants (67.6% female, age range 19–81 years); n = 118 randomized to dCBT-I, n = 120 to waitlist control. At post-treatment (8 weeks), dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in ISI (Diffadj = –7.60) compared to waitlist control (effect size d = –2.08). Clinical improvement was reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were also observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, depression, and anxiety symptoms (ds = 0.26–1.02). Long-term follow-up effects (intervention group only) were sustained (ds = 0.18–1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency. Conclusion: dCBT-I reduces insomnia symptoms and improves daytime functioning in a heterogeneous insomnia population in Germany with sustained long-term effects.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 24 weeks post randomization
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments took place at baseline, 12-weeks, and 24-weeks post randomization. The primary endpoint was at 12 weeks, with a follow-up assessment at 24 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I (dCBT-I) = 70 and C (WLC, waiting list control) = 70
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: fully automated digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) added to care-as-usual (CAU), delivered via a mobile app or web platform consisting of 10 sequential core modules based on evidence-based CBT-I techniques. Control arm: waiting list control (WLC), receiving care-as-usual only.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: severity of depressive symptoms measured by the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) at baseline, 12 weeks, and 24 weeks. Secondary outcomes: insomnia severity, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, well-being, and mechanistic effect measures, assessed at baseline, 12 weeks, and 24 weeks. Linear mixed models were used to determine between-group effects.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: A total of 140 participants were randomized (dCBT-I n=70, WLC n=70; 120 women, mean age 39.76 ± 11.65 years). Large treatment effects at 12 and 24 weeks showed significant reductions in depressive symptoms (PHQ-9) in the dCBT-I group compared to WLC (-3.34 and -2.83 respectively; ps <0.001; Cohen's d = 0.66–0.78). Treatment effects in favor of dCBT-I were also found for insomnia severity (ds = 1.46–1.94) and most secondary outcomes (ds = 0.33–1.14). Effects remained stable across the heterogeneous sample.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 24 weeks post-randomization
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments took place at baseline, 8 weeks, and 24 weeks post-randomization. The primary endpoint was at 8 weeks, with a follow-up assessment at 24 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 103 (dCBT-I) and C = 104 (waitlist control)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) added to regular chronic pain treatment. Arm 2: Waitlist control (WLC) condition receiving regular treatment without dCBT-I. Randomization was 1:1.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia severity measured by the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at baseline, 8 weeks, and 24 weeks post-randomization. Secondary outcomes: sleep-related symptoms, pain-related symptoms (including pain-related impairments and life control), measures of well-being, dreams, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (DBAS). Linear mixed models were used to determine between-group differences.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: 207 participants were randomized (dCBT-I n=103, WLC n=104; 182 women, mean age 51.96 ± 12.97 years). The dCBT-I group showed large improvements in insomnia severity compared to WLC at 8 weeks (difference = -4.36, p < 0.001, d = -1.18) and 24 weeks (difference = -4.88, p < 0.001, d = -1.32). Pain-related impairments and life control also improved favoring dCBT-I (effect sizes ds = 0.22–0.35). Moderate-to-large treatment effects were observed for daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (ds = 0.47–1.12). The study concluded that dCBT-I effectively reduces insomnia severity and improves chronic pain symptoms in individuals with comorbid chronic pain and insomnia.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 16 weeks post-randomisation (i.e., 8 weeks after the primary endpoint)
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomisation. The intervention itself lasted 8 weeks, with a follow-up assessment at 16 weeks post-randomisation.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I (digital CBT) = 29 and C (digital sleep monitoring) = 27
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: 8 weeks of digital cognitive behavioural therapy (dCBT) for insomnia. Arm 2: 8 weeks of digital sleep monitoring (control application).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia severity (assessed at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomisation). Secondary outcomes: Sleep assessed via application-integrated sleep diaries and actigraphy (sleep continuity variables, both self-reported and actigraphy-derived). Linear mixed models used for between-group differences.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions in insomnia severity in the dCBT arm relative to digital sleep monitoring (reductions of -3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003). Treatment effects in favour of dCBT were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables, indicating sleep improved throughout the 8-week intervention period.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The study ran for 12 weeks with measurements collected at baseline and after 12 weeks. Patients in the intervention group exercised with the ViViRA app at least 3 times per week for 20 minutes over 12 weeks. The control group attended physiotherapy at least once a week.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Group 1): received a prescription for the ViViRA digital health app and exercised for 12 weeks (3x/week, 20 min), plus an activity sensor wristband. Control group (Group 2): underwent physiotherapy at least once a week as standard of care. Total enrolled: 70 (planned: 75).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Group 1 (Intervention): Patients with axial spondyloarthritis or axial psoriatic arthritis (per ACR/ASAS criteria) received the ViViRA digital health app as a prescription, exercising 3x/week for 20 minutes over 12 weeks, plus an activity sensor wristband. Group 2 (Control): Patients with the same diagnoses underwent standard physiotherapy at least once a week. Both groups had clinical measurements collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: pain reduction, change in range of motion (BASMI score), change in gait pattern, change in clinical parameters. Secondary outcomes: differences in gait pattern and disease activity between groups; influence of ViViRA on movement competence, sleep quality, hand strength, work productivity, pain, fear of movement, and quality of life. Instruments/measures collected at baseline and after 12 weeks: BASMI (mobility), BASFI (function), BASDAI, ASDAS, ASAS 20 (disease activity); BSA, WPAI, PSQI, ASqL, SF-36, HAQ, PAIN, TSK, IPAQ, PAHCO; gait and movement analysis with sensors; physical activity evaluation via activity sensor (intervention group); clinical routine blood tests (CRP, blood sedimentation, biochemical markers, bone resorption markers: CTX, osteocalcin, BAP); hand strength measurement.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Assessments also at 2 and 6 weeks (intermediate timepoints)
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome (pain reduction via VNRS) measured at 2, 6, and 12 weeks. Trial ran from November 2020 to March 2021.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Arm 1 (Vivira app-based personalized exercise therapy) vs Arm 2 (conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists per German statutory health insurance agreements); total N=213
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Personalized exercise therapy with the Vivira app — digitally adapted guideline-based exercises and patient education. Arm 2: Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists according to agreements with the German statutory health insurance system.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain reduction measured by VNRS (Verbal Numeric Rating Scale) at 2, 6, and 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Function improvement of mobility.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Results published in a peer-reviewed journal (J Med Internet Res. 2022 Oct 28;24(10):e41899). Specific effect sizes, p-values, and statistical results are not detailed in the registry record.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at baseline and after 12 weeks as the primary endpoint timepoint.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received ViViRA DHA (digital health application); control group received standard physiotherapy. Total analyzed: 59 participants.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): SpAs patients received the ViViRA digital health application for home-based physiotherapy. Arm 2 (Control): SpAs patients received standard physiotherapy.
studies.collected_data: Pain assessed via verbal rating scale (VRS) and PAIN-Detect questionnaire; quality of life assessed via SF-36; spinal mobility assessed via BASMI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index). All measures collected at baseline and after 12 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Data from 59 participants (71.2% female, mean age 45.2 years) were analyzed. The intervention group showed a significant improvement in mobility (average BASMI score: baseline 1.1 [range 0.7–1.5]; follow-up 1.0 [range 0.5–1.4]; p = 0.05), whereas the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline 1.5 [range 1.1–1.9]; follow-up 1.8 [range 1.4–2.2]; p = 0.00). The intervention group demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level 3.5 ± 2.8) than the control group (VRS pain level 4.5 ± 2.0) after 12 weeks. The authors concluded that DHA use resulted in superior outcomes compared with standard physiotherapy for lower back pain in SpAs patients.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Assessments at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months (primary endpoint at 6 months, with interim assessment at 3 months)
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were instructed to exercise at least 2-3 times per week for 6 months. Assessments occurred at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Three groups: ViViRA with personal coaching (intervention), Kaia Health with AI-based coaching (intervention), ViViRA without coaching (control). Total N=78.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: ViViRA DHA with personal coaching (one-time 30-min online coaching session prior to DHA use); Arm 2: Kaia Health DHA with AI-based coaching (video-based AI integrated into DHA providing movement guidance during each session); Arm 3: ViViRA DHA without coaching (control group). All groups instructed to exercise at least 2-3 times per week for 6 months.
studies.collected_data: Sociodemographic data; Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index (BASMI, range 0-10, lower = better mobility); PainDETECT (neuropathic pain, range 0-38, higher = more severe pain); PAHCO (Physical Activity-related Health Competence, higher = better competence); mobility assessments; adherence (weekly use of DHA). Collected at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: All three digital interventions showed significant improvement in mobility (BASMI): baseline to 3 months mean change -0.6 to -0.7, all p < 0.001. Pain intensity (PainDETECT) decreased substantially in all arms: baseline to 6 months mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6 points, all p ≤ 0.006. PAHCO control competence increased over time and reached statistical significance only in the ViViRA + coaching group (baseline to 6 months: +1.02, p = 0.013), but did not exceed other interventions in direct comparison. None of the coaching strategies showed significant superiority over stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same in all groups after 3 months (2-3 weekly use of DHA).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary outcome was assessed at 12 weeks, corresponding to the full duration of the digital home exercise program. No follow-up beyond 12 weeks is mentioned.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (digital home exercise program) = 108; Control (physiotherapy standard of care) = 105
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 12-week stand-alone app-based digital home exercise program with 4 exercises daily. Control arm: standard physiotherapy consisting of 6 to 12 sessions depending on symptom severity.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-reported pain intensity measured using a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS) at baseline and 12 weeks. Clinical relevance thresholds: improvement of at least 1.4 points on the VNRS and a pain reduction of at least 30%. Retention rates were also tracked.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Mean difference in pain scores between groups at 12 weeks: -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group. Intervention group achieved a clinically relevant pain reduction: mean change of -3.35 (SD 2.05) points or -53.1% (SD 29.5) from baseline. Control group did not reach clinical relevance: mean change of -0.91 (SD 1.5) or -14.6% (SD 25.3). Retention rates: 89.9% in the intervention group and 97.3% in the control group. The app-based home exercise program led to a significant and clinically relevant reduction in pain intensity superior to standard physiotherapy.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Retrospective, intraindividually controlled study. The study is single-arm with intra-individual control (baseline/historical findings of the same patient serve as control). Data collection period: June 1–15, 2020. Patients included from January 9, 2018 onward.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single arm (N=179); intra-individual control design where each patient's baseline (historical) findings serve as the control. Intervention: use of the Vivira digital home exercise app.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm study. All participants used the Vivira app (CE-certified digital therapeutic exercise program for home use on smartphones/tablets). Control is the intra-individual baseline (pre-treatment) pain score of the same patient.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Pain assessed using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS/VNRS) after 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Proprietary functional test scores. Additional descriptive variables collected include measures of location (means, medians), dispersion (standard deviations), changes from baseline, 95% confidence intervals for changes from baseline, Cohen's effect sizes, and absolute/relative frequencies for qualitative variables.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Retrospective, intraindividual controlled study. The study is single-armed with the control being the pre-finding (historical finding) of the same patient. Data collected from patients using the Vivira app from 01/01/2020 onwards.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single arm: retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app (N=157 actual participants; 150 planned). Intraindividual control: the control is the pre-finding (historical baseline) of the same patient.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app (CE-marked digital therapeutic exercise program for home use on smartphones/tablets). The study is single-armed; the control is the intraindividual pre-finding (historical baseline) of the same patient.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: VNRS (verbal-numerical rating scale) of pain after 12 weeks. Secondary outcome: Proprietary functional test (series of functional scores). Quantitative variables described by measures of location (means, medians) and dispersion (standard deviations); changes from baseline; 95% confidence intervals for changes from baseline; Cohen's effect sizes. Qualitative variables: absolute and relative frequencies.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The study involves a twelve-week individualized home exercise program (digital therapeutic). The study is a single-arm observational study with pre-post comparison. Actual data collection ran from 06 April 2022 to 10 May 2022.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study; no control group. 7842 patients with non-specific and degenerative back, hip, and knee pain using a digital therapeutic (DiGA 'Vivira') consisting of an adaptive home exercise program with individualized exercise progressions.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm: intra-individual pre-post comparison of patients with non-specific and degenerative musculoskeletal conditions undergoing a twelve-week individualized home exercise program (digital therapeutic 'Vivira').
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Self-reported pain intensity assessed with a verbal-numeric rating scale (VNRS, 0-10). Secondary outcomes: (1) Clinical relevance of pain intensity improvement assessed via responder analysis using three criteria established in the literature (criterion met / not met); (2) Physical functional capacity assessed in the dimensions of mobility, strength, and coordination on a discrete scale; (3) Patient characteristics associated with good treatment adherence.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Pain scores were assessed at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks. The total observation period was 12 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm observational study; 3629 patients using the Vivira digital home exercise program (no separate control group).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: All participants received the smartphone-based home exercise program 'Vivira' (a fully approved DiGA) for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee. There was no comparator arm.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: self-reported pain intensity measured with a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS) at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: self-reported function scores for back, hip, and knee pain areas, assessed using matched-pair analysis (Wilcoxon signed-rank test). Demographic data including sex and age were also collected.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity were observed after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (Skillings-Mack test: T3628=5308; P<.001), with changes within the range of clinically relevant improvement. Function scores showed a generally positive yet more variable response across the pain areas (back, hip, and knee). The study noted relevant attrition at follow-up.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The study assessed pain score changes over the intended 12-week duration of the program. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single group of 2517 participants using a smartphone-based home exercise program (Vivira) for unspecific and degenerative back, hip, and knee pain; an incomplete matched block design was used for comparison.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm observational study. All participants used the Vivira smartphone-based exercise program for back, hip, and knee pain. An incomplete matched block design and matched comparison were employed for internal analyses rather than a separate control group.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores (0-10 scale) at baseline and 12 weeks; self-reported functional scores including strength, mobility, and coordination scores; adherence rates. An incomplete matched block design was used to assess pain score changes over 12 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: 2517 participants met inclusion criteria. Overall initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19/10 (SD 1.96) to 3.35/10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over early use phases. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores; participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in coordination scores. A high yet expected attrition rate was observed across all pain areas and pain durations.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The study does not specify the duration of the program or when the primary endpoint was assessed.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Participants were grouped by use profiles: high use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), and sub-low use (sub-LU). Specific group sizes are not reported in the abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Four use profile groups were compared: high use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), and sub-low use (sub-LU) of a digital home exercise program for nonspecific back pain (NSBP). Propensity score matching (PSM) was used to comparatively assess outcomes across these groups.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity (clinically relevant pain improvement as primary measure); use/adherence profiles (HU, IU, LU, sub-LU); sensitivity analyses using commonly accepted thresholds for clinically relevant improvements.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles were associated with a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement in self-reported pain intensities. Achieving any higher use level was associated with a statistically significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement. The study concluded that a high use (HU) profile should be pursued to enable optimal effectiveness of DTx home exercise programs.

studies.duration_additional_info: The Caterna visual training games are played for 90 days daily for 30-45 minutes during occlusion. The prospective data analysis will take place over a period of 5 years. Occlusion therapy continues until the worse eye reaches the same visual acuity as the better eye or no further improvement occurs.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single observational group: amblyopic patients receiving Caterna online visual training (app-based therapy) as an addition to standard occlusion therapy, played on a computer for 90 days daily for 30-45 minutes during occlusion.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One observational arm: amblyopia therapy using Caterna online visual training (CE-certified medical device) additive to standard occlusion therapy. No separate control arm described.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: visual acuity of the worse eye (improvement until equal to the better eye or no further improvement); regular check-ups by orthoptists and ophthalmologists. Secondary outcome: optimization of amblyopia therapy through data analysis of a Germany-wide network. Data includes retrospective and prospective treatment course data from practices using Caterna visual training combined with occlusion therapy.

studies.duration_additional_info: Children had stagnating BCVA despite at least 90 days of prior occlusion therapy before being included. The study does not explicitly state the duration of the FAVAS intervention or the time to primary endpoint assessment.
studies.study_size_participants: 227 children analyzed initially; after propensity score matching, 105 patients (114 eyes; 57 eyes per group) remained for analysis.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: FAVAS group (57 eyes): occlusion therapy plus focal ambient visual acuity stimulation (FAVAS, Caterna Vision GmbH); Control group (57 eyes): occlusion therapy alone.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (FAVAS group): amblyopic children received FAVAS integrated into a computer game alongside occlusion therapy. Arm 2 (Control group): amblyopic children received occlusion therapy alone. Post hoc propensity score matching was applied to balance the groups.
studies.collected_data: Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA, logMAR) as primary endpoint; stereo vision as secondary endpoint. Clinical records from a retrospective multicentre study were analyzed.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: FAVAS group: significant improvement in BCVA with a mean gain of 0.15 logMAR (95% CI [0.11; 0.18], p < 0.001). Control group: non-significant improvement of 0.01 logMAR (95% CI [-0.02; 0.04], p = 0.356). No significant changes in stereo vision were observed in either group. The combination of occlusion therapy and FAVAS was superior to occlusion alone in amblyopic children with stagnating BCVA.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed during a single dental appointment. Heart rate was measured at baseline (patient sitting in chair prior to treatment), during sealant placement with VR, during sealant placement with basic behavior guidance techniques, and post-operatively (5 minutes after procedure). The VR system was worn for approximately 5-10 minutes during dental sealant application. This was a split-mouth crossover design conducted within a single visit.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Crossover design with 4 sequence groups: VR with right side first then BBG with left side (n=4); VR with left side first then BBG with right side (n=4); BBG with right side first then VR with left side (n=8); BBG with left side first then VR with right side (n=4). Each participant received both VR and BBG (split-mouth design).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Virtual Reality (VR) using the RelievRx by AppliedVR goggle headset, worn for approximately 5-10 minutes during dental sealant application, providing an immersive game experience with bright colors and cartoon-like characters. Arm 2 (Active Comparator): Basic Behavior Guidance Techniques (BBG), the standard of care, including tell-show-do, ask-tell-ask, memory restructuring, positive pre-visit imagery, and distraction. Each participant received both interventions on opposite sides of the mouth in a crossover/split-mouth design.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: (1) Change in heart rate (beats per minute) from baseline to during sealant placement with VR; (2) Change in heart rate (beats per minute) from baseline to during sealant placement with BBG. Additional measures: Frankl behavior score (1-5) assigned by provider at end of each appointment; Healthcare Provider Questionnaire (via Qualtrics); parent-reported weekly screen time hours; parent and patient satisfaction survey post-treatment; demographic factors from medical chart. Heart rate measured at: baseline (patient sitting in chair prior to treatment), during sealant placement with VR, during sealant placement with BBG, and post-operatively (5 minutes after procedure).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: This was a pilot study; only means and ranges were reported (no formal statistical analysis). Baseline mean heart rate across all 20 participants: 82.575 bpm (range 61-108). Change in heart rate (beats per minute) with VR: Group VR-right-first: mean -2 (range -9 to 2); Group VR-left-first: mean -6.25 (range -9 to -4); Group BBG-right-first then VR-left: mean -8.8 (range -18 to 4); Group BBG-left-first then VR-right: mean -6.25 (range -12 to -3). Change in heart rate with BBG: Group VR-right-first: mean -2 (range -13 to 8); Group VR-left-first: mean -2.75 (range -10 to 1); Group BBG-right-first: mean -3 (range -14 to 15); Group BBG-left-first: mean -0.5 (range -16 to 7). Heart rate tended to decrease from baseline in both VR and BBG conditions across all groups.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; study completion date was 12 months after primary completion (August 2023 vs August 2022), suggesting follow-up beyond the 90-day primary endpoint period.
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoints assessed at 90 days post-surgery. Adverse events monitored for 4 months per patient. Primary completion date was August 2022; overall study completion date was August 2023.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Standard of Care (control) group: n=32; Standard of Care + RelieVRx (intervention) group: n=31
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Active Comparator): Standard of care group – received multi-modality pain management (local anesthesia, ibuprofen, acetaminophen, gabapentin, ketamine, lidocaine, and/or opioids) without RelieVRx. Arm 2 (Experimental): Standard of care + RelieVRx group – received the same multi-modality pain management plus the RelieVRx virtual reality headset as adjunctive treatment for acute surgical pain.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: (1) Acute Postoperative Pain measured via Visual Analog Pain Scale (0–10) at 90 days; (2) Opioid Consumption measured in Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME) over 90 days (pre-op to post-op change). Secondary outcomes: (1) KOOS Jr. Score (Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score short form; 0–100) at 90 days; (2) Veterans RAND 12 Health Survey (VR-12) Physical Component Score (T-score metric, mean 50, SD 10) at 90 days. Baseline: Numerical Pain Rating Scale (0–10). Adverse events tracked over 4 months per patient.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Acute Postoperative Pain (Visual Analog Scale, 0–10): SOC group mean 5.13 (SD 1.79) vs SOC+RelieVRx group mean 4.42 (SD 1.17) over 90 days. Opioid Consumption (median change in MME from pre-op to post-op, n=24 SOC and n=18 RelieVRx completers): SOC group median −28.0 MME (IQR −35.0 to −23.5); SOC+RelieVRx group median −34.8 MME (IQR −37.0 to −23.5). KOOS Jr. Score (median change pre- to post-surgery): SOC group 27.6 (IQR 17.5–38.0); SOC+RelieVRx group 32.2 (IQR 21.2–42.0). VR-12 Physical Component Score (median change): SOC group 7.2 (IQR 2.0–11.4); SOC+RelieVRx group 9.5 (IQR 4.0–12.8). No deaths or serious adverse events were reported in either group.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary endpoint was assessed at day 56 (end of 8-week treatment). Data were collected from January 31, 2022, to October 31, 2022.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I (RelieVRx) = 536 and C (Sham) = 531
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received RelieVRx, an 8-week self-administered in-home behavioral skills virtual reality program training diaphragmatic breathing, biofeedback, cognition and emotion regulation, mindfulness, and pain education. Control arm received an active Sham VR program. Randomization was 1:1.
studies.collected_data: Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), pain intensity (0-10 scale), pain interference (0-10 scale), assessed from pretreatment to day 56 (end of treatment). Participants had self-reported nonmalignant chronic low back pain ≥3 months duration with average pain intensity and pain interference ≥4/10.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: RelieVRx was superior to Sham for pain intensity reduction (difference from Sham: 0.406 [95% CI: 0.170–0.642]) and pain interference reduction (difference from Sham: 0.523 [95% CI: 0.285–0.760]) from pretreatment to day 56. Clinically meaningful reductions were observed for RelieVRx: pain intensity reduced by 2.0 points out of 10 [1.73–2.06] and pain interference reduced by 2.3 points [1.99–2.33]. The 8-week self-administered behavioral skills VR program imparted clinically meaningful improvements above the active control on both pain intensity and pain interference in clinically severe and diverse adults with chronic low back pain.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 12 months posttreatment
studies.duration_additional_info: The study involved a 56-session self-administered in-home VR program. Data were collected from January 31, 2022 to October 31, 2023. The primary analyses compared pretreatment to 12 months posttreatment outcomes.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Participants randomized to Skills-Based VR (RelieVRx) or active Sham; total N=1,093 (individual group sizes not reported in abstract)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Skills-Based VR (RelieVRx) – a 56-session, self-administered in-home virtual reality program incorporating pain education and cognitive behavioral therapy. Arm 2: Active Sham – a sham/control VR condition.
studies.collected_data: Average pain intensity (0-10 scale), pain interference (0-10 scale), responder rates (≥2-point reduction in pain intensity, pain interference, or both) assessed at baseline and 12 months posttreatment. Participants had self-reported nonmalignant chronic low back pain >3 months duration with average pain intensity and interference scores >4/10.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: From baseline to 12 months posttreatment, Skills-Based VR showed reductions in average pain intensity (1.7 ± 2.1) and pain interference (1.9 ± 2.3) that were robust and significantly greater than those found for Sham. More than half of Skills-Based VR participants reported at least a 2-point reduction in pain intensity, pain interference, or both at 12 months posttreatment.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 6 months post-intervention
studies.duration_additional_info: The 9-week intervention period is the primary endpoint; a second primary endpoint assessment occurs at 6 months post-intervention. The overall study completion date was July 15, 2024. Adverse events were monitored up to 6 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I (Culturally adapted digital CBT-I / Somryst) = 3 and C (Minimally Enhanced Usual Care / mEUC) = 4
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Experimental): Culturally adapted digital CBT-I program (Somryst) – FDA-authorized, translated and culturally adapted for Spanish speakers, self-guided mobile-based program with six cores over 9 weeks covering behavioral (sleep restriction, stimulus control), cognitive (cognitive restructuring), educational (sleep hygiene), overview, and consolidation/relapse prevention domains, plus usual primary care and a sleep hygiene brochure. Arm 2 (Active Comparator): Minimally Enhanced Usual Care (mEUC) – continued standard care with primary care provider (which may include pharmacotherapy) plus a sleep hygiene brochure.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Change in mean Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) score (7-item self-report, range 0-28) from baseline to post-intervention (9 weeks) and from baseline to 6 months post-intervention. Secondary outcomes: Change in mean SF-12 Mental Component Score (MCS) quality of life (12-item, 0-100 scale) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS) daytime sleepiness (8-item, range 0-24) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean Patient Assessment of Chronic Illness Care (PACIC) satisfaction with care (20-item, range 20-100) from baseline to 9 weeks. Other pre-specified outcomes: Change in mean Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) sleep quality (18-item, range 0-21) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean self-reported wake after sleep onset (14-day sleep diary, minutes) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean actigraphy-assessed wake after sleep onset (14-day wrist actigraphy, minutes) from baseline to 9 weeks; Change in mean self-reported sleep onset latency (14-day sleep diary, minutes) from baseline to 9 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Only 7 participants were enrolled (3 in CBT-I arm, 4 in mEUC arm); all completed the study. Primary outcome (ISI, baseline to 9 weeks): CBT-I group mean change = -6.7 (95% CI: -15.4 to 2.0); mEUC group mean change = -3.9 (95% CI: -12.5 to 4.7). Primary outcome (ISI, baseline to 6 months post-intervention): CBT-I group mean change = -5.7 (95% CI: -17.1 to 5.8); mEUC group mean change = -1.6 (95% CI: -13.0 to 9.9). Secondary outcome (SF-12 MCS, 9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = +10.8 (95% CI: 5.9 to 15.7); mEUC mean change = +0.8 (95% CI: -4.0 to 5.6). Secondary outcome (ESS, 9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = -2.3 (95% CI: -6.5 to 1.9); mEUC mean change = +0.5 (95% CI: -3.6 to 4.6). Secondary outcome (PACIC, 9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = +17.4 (95% CI: -20.5 to 55.4); mEUC mean change = -8.2 (95% CI: -41.9 to 25.6). Other outcomes (PSQI, 9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = -1.0 (95% CI: -2.6 to 0.6); mEUC mean change = -0.7 (95% CI: -2.2 to 0.8). Actigraphy-assessed WASO (9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = -2.5 min (95% CI: -38.5 to 33.6); mEUC mean change = -4.8 min (95% CI: -10.2 to 0.6). Self-reported sleep onset latency (9 weeks): CBT-I mean change = -53.3 min (95% CI: -128.6 to 21.9); mEUC mean change = -16.9 min (95% CI: -84.7 to 51.0). Self-reported WASO data were not collected and could not be analyzed. No adverse events (deaths, serious, or other) were reported in either arm. Note: The very small sample size (n=7 total) severely limits the interpretability of these results.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; secondary outcomes and follow-up are assessed beyond the 9-week primary endpoint (specific duration not stated beyond 9 weeks)
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at 9 weeks post-randomisation. The trial is prospective and multicentre. No explicit total study duration beyond the 9-week primary endpoint is stated in the protocol.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 50 (PDT/Somryst for mobile-delivered CBT-I) and C = 50 (no PDT); randomised 1:1
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Participants receive the prescription digital therapeutic (PDT) PEAR-003b (Somryst), an FDA-authorised mobile app delivering CBT-I, plus a linked Fitbit Inspire 2. Arm 2 (Control): Participants do not receive the PDT but are enrolled on the Hugo Health platform and provided with a linked Fitbit Inspire 2.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Change in Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) score from baseline to 9-week post-randomisation. Secondary outcomes: Healthcare utilisation; health utility scores; clinical outcomes; change in sleep outcomes measured via sleep diaries; depressive symptoms; daytime sleepiness; health status; stress; anxiety; engagement with the PDT (for intervention arm only). Data collected via the Hugo Health patient-centred health data-aggregating platform and Fitbit Inspire 2 activity tracker.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; at 6, 12, and 18 months after posttest
studies.duration_additional_info: Posttest (primary endpoint) at 9 weeks; follow-ups at 6-, 12-, and 18-month intervals after baseline.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: SHUTi (digital CBT-I); Control: HealthWatch (attention-matched control internet program); total N = 1149
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: SHUTi – a self-guided, digital Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) program. Arm 2: HealthWatch – an attention-matched control internet program.
studies.collected_data: Online sleep diaries used to derive: sleep-onset latency (SOL), wake after sleep onset (WASO), sleep efficiency (SE), number of awakenings, sleep quality, and total sleep time (TST). Assessments conducted at baseline, posttest (9 weeks), and at 6-, 12-, and 18-month follow-ups. Also measured: insomnia severity, depression symptoms, anxiety symptoms, and suicidal ideation (referenced from prior RCTs context).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the SHUTi condition had greater improvements at posttest (9 weeks) compared with control for SOL, WASO, SE, number of awakenings, and sleep quality. These improvements were sustained at every follow-up (p < .02 for all outcomes except TST). Statistically significant increases in TST were observed only at 12- and 18-month follow-ups. Digitally delivered CBT-I produced lasting improvements in sleep outcomes among adults with insomnia and depressive symptoms.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: Retrospective pharmacovigilance study of the MAUDE database through April 30, 2025. No specific primary endpoint duration or follow-up period defined; the study reviewed postmarket adverse event reports retrospectively.
studies.collected_data: MAUDE database adverse event reports for 13 FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutics (PDTs); data extracted on event type, patient demographics, outcomes, and product details. Device-specific product codes used to identify reports related to each PDT.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Four reports were initially identified; two were excluded as they involved non-cleared or over-the-counter digital products. Of the remaining two: one involved Somryst (for insomnia) prescribed to a patient with a seizure disorder (a known contraindication), resulting in disability; the other involved EndeavorRx, reporting perceived ineffectiveness without clinical harm. No adverse events were identified for the remaining 11 PDTs. The authors concluded that while findings may suggest a favorable safety profile, results must be interpreted cautiously given limitations of passive surveillance systems including underreporting, reporting bias, and limited capacity to capture software-specific harms.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes; 6-month and 1-year follow-ups after the 9-week intervention period
studies.duration_additional_info: The 9-week intervention period involved 6 interactive treatment cores. Assessments were completed at baseline, before completing treatment cores, immediately post-intervention, at 6 months, and at 1 year.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study; 1565 adults accessed the PDT (Somryst®). No control group. 908 (58.0%) were considered adherent and 733 (46.8%) completed all 6 cores.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm: all participants received the FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutic (PDT; Somryst®) delivering cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) via six interactive treatment cores and daily sleep diaries over a 9-week intervention period.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI), 8-item Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-8), Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 scale (GAD-7), adherence/completion rates (Core 4 completion as exposure to all mechanisms of action, completion of all 6 cores). Assessments collected at baseline, before completing each treatment core, immediately post-intervention, 6-month follow-up, and 1-year follow-up.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: For participants who completed assessments for all 6 cores (48.4%), ISI decreased from 18.8 at baseline to a mean of 9.9 (P<.001). ISI scores remained significantly lower than baseline at immediate post-intervention (11.0), 6-month (11.6), and 1-year (12.2) follow-ups (all P<.001). PHQ-8 and GAD-7 also showed significant decreases at all measured timepoints from baseline (P<.001). 58% of those who began the program completed Core 4 (adherent); 46.8% completed all 6 cores.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: 2 years
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary clinical endpoint (ISI score change) assessed at 9 weeks; long-term HCRU analysis compared 2 years pre-index to 2 years post-index. Index date defined as successful initiation of the therapeutic between June 1, 2016 and December 31, 2018.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single cohort of 248 patients treated with Somryst (digital CBT-I therapeutic); pre/post design with no separate control group (2-year pre-index period served as comparator for HCRU outcomes).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single-arm real-world study. All participants received Somryst (formerly SHUTi), a digital therapeutic delivering cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I). A pre/post analysis was used for HCRU comparisons.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) score (baseline and last observed score); ISI responder status (improvement ≥7 points); insomnia remission (ISI score <8); healthcare resource utilization (HCRU) including emergency department visits, hospitalizations, hospital outpatient visits, ambulatory surgical center visits, and office visits (2 years pre- and post-index); sleep aid medication use (proportion of patients and number of prescriptions pre- and post-index); total healthcare costs (2 years pre- and post-index).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: After 9 weeks, mean ISI score declined 37.2% from baseline (19.1 vs 12.0). 58.8% of patients achieved ISI responder status (NNT: 1.7); 26.6% achieved insomnia remission (NNT: 3.8). Over 2-year follow-up, ED visits reduced by 53% (IRR: 0.47; 95% CI 0.27–0.82; P=0.008); hospitalizations reduced by 21% (IRR: 0.79; 95% CI 0.46–1.35; P=0.389); hospital outpatient visits reduced by 13% (IRR: 0.87; 95% CI 0.66–1.14; P=0.315). Ambulatory surgical center visits increased 2% (IRR: 1.02; P=0.903) and office visits increased 2% (IRR: 1.02; P=0.672). Sleep aid medication use dropped from 52.4% to 42.7% (-18.5%). Average prescriptions decreased from 3.98 to 3.73 (P=0.552). Total 2-year cost reduction was $510,678 (-$2,059 per patient).

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: The program had an average duration of 10 weeks; the primary outcome was assessed from baseline to program-end.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study: 326 participants started the program; 264 (81.0%) completed the intervention. No control group.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm: all participants received a remote digital care program (DCP) combining education and pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) with real-time biofeedback, managed asynchronously by a physical therapist.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Urinary Impact Questionnaire-short form (UIQ-7) change from baseline to program-end, analyzed via latent growth curve analysis (LGCA).
